title: fix: sleep/handler.go cannot index interface{} map value
Dashboard: WebSocket reconnection with exponential backoff and state management
description:
## Overview
Implement robust client-side WebSocket reconnection with exponential backoff, jitter, and visual state transitions during disconnects.

## Reconnection logic (dashboard/js/websocket.js or app.js)
- Backoff sequence: 1s, 2s, 4s, 8s, max 10s; ±500ms random jitter on each attempt
- Track disconnect_duration_ms from first disconnect event

## Visual state transitions:
Disconnect < 5s:   silent (no UI change); blob positions extrapolated from last velocity
Disconnect 5-30s:  3D scene dims to 50% opacity; 'Reconnecting...' spinner in status bar; user interaction disabled
Disconnect > 30s:  non-blocking modal: 'Connection lost — [Reload Page]'; allow viewing stale scene

## Blob position extrapolation (<5s):
- On disconnect: record last_position and last_velocity per blob
- Each animation frame: position = last_position + last_velocity × elapsed_s (capped at 2s extrapolation)

## On successful reconnect:
- Clear all blob trails (path history lines)
- Apply snapshot from first WebSocket message (spaxel-fll)
- Restore scene opacity to 100%
- Dismiss spinner and modal
- Log 'Reconnected after Xs' to console

## Acceptance
- Disconnect for 3s: no visual change; blobs continue moving smoothly
- Disconnect for 10s: scene dims, spinner shown
- Disconnect for 35s: modal appears; scene still visible
- Reconnect: modal dismissed, trails cleared, scene snaps to current state within 200ms
- Requires: spaxel-fll (snapshot protocol), spaxel-896 (panel framework)
close_reason: WebSocket reconnection with exponential backoff and visual state management was already fully implemented in commit ff3428f. All acceptance criteria met: backoff 1s-10s with ±500ms jitter, <5s silent extrapolation, 5-30s dimming with spinner, >30s modal with reload button, reconnect clears trails and restores scene from snapshot.

title: Anomaly detection and security mode
description:
## Background

After 7+ days of learning, spaxel knows the household's normal patterns: who is home when, which zones are occupied at which hours, which BLE devices are typically present. Deviations from these patterns — unexpected late-night presence, unknown BLE devices, motion during away mode — can indicate security events. Security mode explicitly arms anomaly detection with immediate alerts and a comprehensive alert chain, transforming spaxel into a basic home security system.

## Normal Behaviour Model

AnomalyDetector maintains a statistical model of normal behaviour per (hour_of_week, zone_id) slot. For each slot:
- expected_occupancy: whether this zone is typically occupied at this time (fraction of historical samples that had occupancy > 0)
- typical_person_count: mean occupant count
- typical_ble_devices: set of BLE device MACs typically present (with minimum frequency threshold: seen in > 50% of this hour_of_week slot)

The model is updated weekly from the activity history and zone transition history. A minimum of 7 days of data is required before anomaly detection activates. Before 7 days: no anomaly alerts fire.

New file: mothership/internal/analytics/anomaly.go

## Anomaly Scoring

Four anomaly types, each with a base score contribution:

1. Unusual hour presence (score: 0.7 by default, 0.9 in security mode):
   Motion detected in zone Z at hour H, but expected_occupancy for (H, Z) < 0.1 (this zone is empty >90% of the time at hour H historically). Apply time-of-day sensitivity: late night (00:00-06:00) has 1.5x multiplier.

2. Unknown BLE device (score: 0.5 by default, 0.8 in security mode):
   BLE device with RSSI > -60 dBm (close range) that is not in the registered device list AND has not been seen before (not even in the archive). If it was seen once before but not regularly, score = 0.3.

3. Motion during away mode (score: 0.95, always immediate):
   Any presence detected when SystemMode == AWAY. By definition anomalous — all registered people are absent. This always fires an alert regardless of model training status.

4. Unusual dwell duration (score: 0.4):
   Person present in zone for > 5x the historical mean dwell time for that (person, zone, hour_of_week) combination. May indicate a person is incapacitated (fell and can't get up) rather than a security event — cross-check with fall detection before escalating.

Composite anomaly score: max(individual scores) for the most anomalous concurrent event. Alert threshold: score > 0.6 (default mode), score > 0.4 (security mode).

## Security Mode

SecurityMode is an extension of the SystemMode. When SystemMode is AWAY:
- All anomaly detection thresholds are lowered (as per scores above)
- Alert chain is immediate (no T+2min or T+5min delays — fires immediately)
- Quiet hours are suppressed (all alerts bypass quiet hours when in security mode)
- All four anomaly types are active regardless of model training status (even before 7 days)

Auto-away detection:
- Condition: all registered person_ids have had no BLE device seen by any node for > 15 minutes
- On condition met: set SystemMode = AWAY. Log: "Auto-away activated — all BLE devices absent"
- Broadcast system_mode_change WebSocket event to dashboard

Auto-disarm:
- Condition: any registered person's BLE device seen with RSSI > -70 dBm at any node
- On condition met: set SystemMode = HOME. Clear security alerts (or keep them acknowledged-pending).
- Broadcast system_mode_change event
- Show "Welcome home" card in dashboard if identity is known: "Alice arrived home."

Manual override: dashboard has a Home/Away/Sleep toggle that overrides auto-detection. Once manually set, auto-detection is paused for 30 minutes (avoids immediate re-trigger).

## Alert Chain

On anomaly score > threshold:

1. T+0s: Dashboard alarm overlay (red full-screen banner, z-index: top):
   "Anomaly detected: [description]. [Acknowledge] [View in 3D] [Dismiss]"
   Description examples: "Motion detected in Kitchen at 3:12am (unusual hour)", "Unknown device detected near front door", "Motion detected while everyone is away"

2. T+0s (security mode) or T+30s (normal mode): push notification via configured channel with floor-plan thumbnail (using notification module, spaxel-zpt)

3. T+0s (security mode) or T+2min (normal mode): webhook/MQTT via automation engine (trigger type: anomaly)

4. T+5min (without acknowledgement): escalation webhook (secondary URL in settings)

Acknowledge: acknowledges the alert, logs in activity timeline with current time, stops escalation timers. Shows brief form: "What was this?" — Expected/known event / Genuine intrusion / False alarm. This feeds the false positive rate for the anomaly model.

## Detection History and Visualisation

All anomaly events are logged in the activity timeline (Phase 8). The 3D view adds an "Anomaly" layer:
- When an active unacknowledged anomaly exists, the relevant zone pulses with a red overlay in the 3D scene
- Anomaly events in the timeline are marked with a red shield icon

Weekly anomaly summary: "0 anomalies this week" (reassuring) or "3 anomalies detected: 2 false alarms, 1 unacknowledged."

## Files to Create or Modify

- mothership/internal/analytics/anomaly.go: AnomalyDetector, normal behaviour model
- mothership/internal/fleet/manager.go: SystemMode integration, auto-away detection, BLE presence tracking for auto-disarm
- dashboard/js/anomaly.js: alarm overlay, acknowledge UI
- mothership/internal/dashboard/routes.go: GET /api/mode, POST /api/mode, GET /api/anomalies/history
- mothership/internal/events/events.go: AnomalyEvent type

## Tests

- Test anomaly score for "unusual hour presence": expected_occupancy=0.05 at 3am -> score fires
- Test "unknown BLE device": inject device MAC not in registry at RSSI -55 -> anomaly fires
- Test "motion during away": set SystemMode=AWAY, inject presence event -> immediate alert fires regardless of thresholds
- Test auto-away: all BLE devices absent for 900s -> SystemMode becomes AWAY
- Test auto-disarm: device seen at RSSI=-65 -> SystemMode becomes HOME
- Test alert chain timing in normal mode: alert at T+0, notification at T+30s, webhook at T+2min
- Test security mode immediate alert chain: all three fire at T+0
- Test acknowledgement cancels pending escalation timers

## Acceptance Criteria

- Anomaly fires correctly for unexpected late-night motion after 7 days of baseline
- Security mode auto-activates when all registered BLE devices absent for 15 minutes
- Alert chain fires in correct sequence for both normal and security mode
- Auto-disarm triggers correctly when first registered BLE device returns
- Dashboard alarm overlay is clearly visible (full-screen red banner) on anomaly detection
- Zone pulsing in 3D view during active unacknowledged anomaly
- Acknowledgement and feedback form records correctly
- Tests pass

title: Build dashboard panel/modal/sidebar UI framework
description:
## Problem

The dashboard is currently a single live 3D view with no panel system. All Phase 6-9 UI work (automation builder, timeline, explainability, settings, notifications, presence predictions) requires a panel/sidebar framework to hang on.

## What to build

### Panel System (dashboard/js/panels.js)
- Slide-in sidebar (right, 360px) with close button and title
- Modal overlay (centered, 600px wide) for forms and wizards
- Toast notification stack (bottom-right)
- Panel registry: panels can be opened by name from anywhere in the app

### Route/Mode Navigation (dashboard/js/router.js)
- Hash-based routing: #live (default), #timeline, #automations, #settings, #ambient, #replay
- Mode toggle bar in the header: Live | Timeline | Automations | Settings
- Active mode preserved across page refresh (localStorage)

### State Management (dashboard/js/state.js)
- Central app state object (nodes, blobs, zones, links, alerts, events, ble_devices, triggers)
- Subscribe/notify pattern for components to react to state changes
- Separate from WebSocket message parsing

### Settings Panel (dashboard/js/settings-panel.js)
- Motion threshold slider
- Sensing rate override
- Notification channel config (Ntfy URL, Pushover token)
- System info (version, uptime, node count)

## Acceptance

- Panel opens/closes smoothly with CSS transitions
- Route changes update the active view without page reload
- Settings panel reads from GET /api/settings and saves via POST /api/settings
- All existing 3D live view functionality unaffected

title: Expand dashboard WebSocket feed: events, alerts, anomalies, triggers, BLE
description:
## Problem

The dashboard WebSocket (/ws/dashboard) currently only sends blob/node/zone/link/confidence/predictions/sleep/flow state. Events, alerts, anomalies, triggers, and BLE device data are never pushed to the dashboard, making Phase 6-9 UI impossible without a polling API.

## What to add to the WS feed

In mothership/internal/dashboard/ (hub.go or server.go):

### New message types to broadcast:

**event** — presence transitions, zone entries/exits, portal crossings
  { type: 'event', event: { id, ts, kind, zone, blob_id, person_name } }

**alert** — anomaly detections, security mode triggers
  { type: 'alert', alert: { id, ts, severity, description, acknowledged } }

**ble_scan** — BLE device list updates (5s interval)
  { type: 'ble_scan', devices: [{ mac, name, rssi, last_seen, label, blob_id }] }

**trigger_state** — automation trigger state changes
  { type: 'trigger_state', trigger: { id, name, last_fired, enabled } }

**system_health** — periodic system stats (60s interval)
  { type: 'system_health', health: { uptime_s, node_count, bead_count, go_routines, mem_mb } }

### In dashboard JS (app.js):
- Handle each new message type in the WebSocket onmessage handler
- Update app state for each type
- Log unhandled types to console (for future debugging)

## Acceptance

- All 5 new message types appear in browser devtools WebSocket inspector
- BLE device list updates every 5s when devices are present
- Events appear within 1s of a zone transition
- Existing blob/node/link messages unaffected

title: Infrastructure: database schema migration engine
description:
## Overview
Implement a versioned schema migration framework so the mothership can safely upgrade its SQLite schema across releases without data loss.

## Implementation (mothership/internal/db/ or cmd/mothership/migrate.go)

- schema_migrations table: version INT PK, applied_at INT, description TEXT
- Migration registry: slice of Migration structs {Version int, Description string, Up func(*sql.Tx) error}
- Startup phase (before any other subsystem): read current schema_ver; identify pending migrations
- Run each pending migration in its own transaction; commit on success, rollback on failure
- On failure: log error, preserve pre-migration backup, exit non-zero
- Startup shutdown on error: never serve traffic with a partially migrated schema

## Pre-migration safety
- Before first migration: use SQLite Online Backup API to copy DB to /data/backups/pre-upgrade-v<old>-to-v<new>-<timestamp>.sqlite
- Create /data/backups/ if not exists
- Backups older than 90 days: pruned daily at 02:00 local time (or on startup)

## Initial migrations
- migration_001: initial schema (nodes, links, baselines, events, zones, portals, sessions, etc.)
- migration_002: add diurnal_baselines table
- migration_003: add anomaly_patterns table
- migration_004: add prediction_models table
- migration_005: add ble_device_aliases table

## Acceptance
- 'go test ./internal/db/...' passes including migration test from v1 to current
- Pre-migration backup created before any schema change
- Failed migration exits cleanly; DB unchanged; backup preserved
- Idempotent: running migrations on already-migrated DB is a no-op
close_reason:
Database schema migration engine implemented with:

- schema_migrations table tracking version, applied_at, description
- Migration registry with Migration structs (Version, Description, Up func)
- Startup phase integration via OpenDB() with 7-phase sequence
- Transactional migration execution with commit/rollback
- Pre-migration backup using VACUUM INTO to /data/backups/
- Failed migration exits cleanly with backup preserved
- Idempotent migrations (checks current version, only runs pending)
- 90-day backup retention with automatic pruning
- Initial migrations 001-005 for all core tables
- Comprehensive test coverage (idempotency, rollback, v1-to-current migration)

Implementation in mothership/internal/db/ (migrate.go, migrations.go, db.go, migrate_test.go)
Committed as 2da6e23 and deadlock fix as f9632c7

title: Mothership: SIGTERM graceful shutdown sequence
description:
## Overview
Implement the full 10-step ordered shutdown sequence so the mothership drains cleanly without data loss on SIGTERM (Docker stop, Kubernetes termination).

## Sequence (plan lines 3480-3494) — 30s hard deadline:
1. Set shutting_down=true; ingestion server returns HTTP 503 to new WebSocket upgrade requests
2. Broadcast {type:'shutdown', reconnect_in_ms:30000} to all dashboard WebSocket clients
3. Cancel fusion loop context (stops fusion goroutine)
4. Drain signal processing pipeline: wait for in-flight CSI frames (max 2s)
5. Flush in-memory baselines to SQLite in a single transaction
6. Sync CSI recording buffer to disk (close writer, fsync)
7. Close all node WebSocket connections with normal close frame (1000)
8. Write {type:'system', description:'Mothership stopped'} event to events table
9. PRAGMA wal_checkpoint(FULL) to collapse WAL into main DB file
10. sqlite3.Close()

## Implementation
- context.WithTimeout(30s) wraps entire shutdown
- Each step gets its own log line: '[SHUTDOWN] Step N/10 — ...'
- Steps that fail log ERROR but do not abort remaining steps
- Exit code 0 if all steps completed within deadline; exit code 1 if deadline exceeded

## Acceptance
- docker stop (SIGTERM) completes within 35s (30s shutdown + 5s buffer)
- No WAL file remains after clean shutdown (verified with ls -la /data/)
- system_stopped event present in events table after restart
- In-flight CSI frames processed (not dropped) during drain step
close_reason:
Implemented the full 10-step ordered shutdown sequence so the mothership drains cleanly without data loss on SIGTERM (Docker stop, Kubernetes termination).

Shutdown sequence (30s hard deadline):
1. Set shutting_down=true; ingestion server returns HTTP 503 to new WebSocket upgrade requests
2. Broadcast {type:'shutdown', reconnect_in_ms:30000} to all dashboard WebSocket clients
3. Cancel fusion loop context (stops fusion goroutine)
4. Drain signal processing pipeline: wait for in-flight CSI frames (max 2s)
5. Flush in-memory baselines to SQLite in a single transaction
6. Sync CSI recording buffer to disk (close writer, fsync)
7. Close all node WebSocket connections with normal close frame (1000)
8. Write {type:'system', description:'Mothership stopped'} event to events table
9. PRAGMA wal_checkpoint(FULL) to collapse WAL into main DB file
10. sqlite3.Close()

Each step gets its own log line: '[SHUTDOWN] Step N/10 — ...'
Steps that fail log ERROR but do not abort remaining steps.
Exit code 0 if all steps completed within deadline; exit code 1 if deadline exceeded.

title: Morning briefing
description:
## Background

The morning briefing is a daily summary delivered when the first household member opens the dashboard (or wakes up, detected by the presence system) after the quiet hours / sleep period. It collects the most relevant information from the previous night and the upcoming day into a single concise card, so the user starts the day informed about their home without any active effort. A good morning briefing is like a trusted home assistant quietly summarising overnight events.

## BriefingGenerator

New package: mothership/internal/briefing/generator.go

BriefingGenerator runs as a scheduled goroutine, generating a DailyBriefing at a configurable time (default 06:00 server-local time). The briefing is stored in SQLite (daily_briefings table) and pushed to dashboard clients on their first connection after the briefing time.

DailyBriefing struct:
- id TEXT (UUID)
- date DATE (the date this briefing covers — typically "today", covering the previous night)
- generated_at DATETIME
- sections []BriefingSection
- delivered BOOLEAN (set true after first push to any dashboard client)
- acknowledged BOOLEAN (set true when user dismisses the card)

Each BriefingSection has a type and content:
- SectionType: "sleep", "overnight_events", "system_health", "predictions", "weather" (optional)

## Section: Sleep Report

For each person with a completed sleep session from the previous night:
Generated from the sleep_sessions table (Phase 7, spaxel-qfp).
Content example: "Alice slept 7h 23m. 2 wake-ups, 91% efficiency. Avg breathing 14/min."
If no sleep session: "No sleep data for Alice last night."
If sleep session is still in progress (woke up after 6am): "Alice is still asleep."

Formatting rules:
- Good sleep (efficiency > 85%, duration > 7h): green indicator
- Fair sleep (efficiency 70-85% or duration 6-7h): amber indicator
- Poor sleep (efficiency < 70% or duration < 6h): red indicator
- Anomaly (breathing rate anomaly flagged): include note: "Unusual breathing pattern detected at 02:14."

## Section: Overnight Events

Summary of activity timeline events that occurred during the quiet hours period (e.g. 10pm-6am).
Filter: FallDetected, AnomalyDetected, NodeDisconnected events only.
If no events: "No incidents overnight." (Reassuring — users should see this most mornings.)
If events: "Node Living Room went offline at 02:15 and reconnected at 02:47." or "Anomaly detected at 03:30 in Kitchen (acknowledged)."
Fall events: always prominently listed, even if acknowledged: "Possible fall detected at 04:12 for Alice in Bedroom (acknowledged)."
Limit: maximum 5 events summarised. If more than 5: "...and 3 more events. [View all]"

## Section: System Health

Quick summary of current system health.
Content: "4 nodes healthy." or "3 nodes healthy. Node Hallway has been offline since 02:15."
If a node has been offline > 1 hour: include the duration.
Link health average: "Detection quality: 92%." (from ambient confidence score, Phase 5).

## Section: Predictions

For each tracked person, their predicted first activity today.
Generated from the presence prediction model (Phase 7, spaxel-hnp).
Content: "Alice typically leaves at 8:30am on Tuesdays (78% confidence)."
Only included if prediction model has sufficient data (> 7 days per person).
If prediction confidence < 60%: omit (not useful at low confidence).

## Section: Weather (Optional)

If a weather API URL is configured in settings (e.g. OpenWeatherMap, wttr.in):
Fetch current outdoor temperature and conditions.
Content: "Outside: 14°C, partly cloudy."
This is a nice-to-have context note for heating decisions. The API call is optional and fails gracefully (omit the section if the API is unavailable).

Implementation: GET https://wttr.in/{location}?format=%t+%C (plain text format). Cache for 30 minutes.

## Dashboard Delivery

On the first WebSocket connection after 06:00 from any dashboard client:
1. Check if today's DailyBriefing exists and has not been delivered yet
2. If yes: push {"type":"morning_briefing","briefing":BriefingJSON} to that client
3. Mark briefing.delivered = true

Dashboard rendering:
- Simple mode: full-width dismissible card at the very top of the home view. Large enough to read at a glance. Dismiss button (X) in top-right corner.
- Expert mode: a floating panel overlay (300px wide, top-right of screen), auto-dismisses after 30 seconds of inactivity (the user sees it then goes back to work)
- Ambient mode: the morning briefing overlay already handled by the ambient mode bead (spaxel-5es) — this briefing generator feeds that overlay.

Acknowledgement: the dismiss button sends POST /api/briefings/{id}/acknowledge. Sets briefing.acknowledged = true.

## Push Notification Delivery

The briefing is also pushed as a notification at 06:00 (via the notification module, Phase 6 spaxel-zpt), even if no dashboard is open. This ensures users get their morning summary even if they have not opened the dashboard.

The notification includes a floor-plan thumbnail (from the floor-plan renderer, spaxel-zpt) showing the current home state at 06:00.

Notification title: "Good morning" (or "Good morning, Alice" if single person)
Notification body: condensed version of the briefing: the most important item from each section.

## Files to Create or Modify

- mothership/internal/briefing/generator.go: BriefingGenerator, DailyBriefing, section generation
- mothership/internal/briefing/scheduler.go: daily schedule (6am goroutine)
- mothership/internal/dashboard/hub.go: morning_briefing push on first connection
- dashboard/js/briefing.js: morning briefing card rendering in simple and expert modes

title: Interactive onboarding wizard
description:
## Background

Phase 4's central goal is that a non-technical user can go from an unboxed ESP32-S3 to streaming CSI in under 5 minutes. The onboarding wizard is the centrepiece of this experience. It uses the Web Serial API (available in Chrome/Edge) to communicate with the ESP32 over USB — no driver installation needed, no CLI, no app download. The wizard is embedded in the existing mothership dashboard, accessible at /onboard.

## Why Web Serial?

The alternative approaches — a dedicated mobile app, a WiFi provisioning AP, or a CLI tool — all have significant UX friction. Web Serial lets us flash firmware, provision WiFi credentials, and guide the user through calibration all in one browser session. The dashboard already knows the mothership IP/port. Chrome and Edge (95%+ of desktop browser market) support Web Serial natively since 2021. The only caveat is that Web Serial is not available in Firefox or Safari — this must be documented prominently at the start of the wizard.

## Wizard Steps

1. **Browser check**: Detect navigator.serial availability. If missing, show: 'Please use Google Chrome or Microsoft Edge to use the setup wizard. Firefox and Safari do not support USB device access.'

2. **Connect device**: Call navigator.serial.requestPort(). Guide the user to hold BOOT button while plugging in if the device does not appear. Show a GIF or SVG illustration of the ESP32-S3 board with the BOOT button highlighted.

3. **Flash firmware** (if not already spaxel firmware): Use esp-web-tools (espressif/esp-web-tools, CDN: https://unpkg.com/esp-web-tools@10/dist/web/install-button.js). This open-source library handles the full ESP32 flashing pipeline via Web Serial, including ROM bootloader protocol, chip detection, and progress reporting. It needs a firmware manifest.json at GET /api/firmware/manifest describing binary addresses and offsets. Show a progress bar during flashing. Estimated time: 45-90 seconds.

4. **Provision WiFi**: Show a form for SSID and password. Optional: mothership host/port override (for non-mDNS setups). Assemble the provisioning payload and send to the ESP32 over serial as JSON (see Provisioning Payload bead for format). If using esp-web-tools, the provisioning can be injected via custom serial commands after flashing.

5. **Detect mothership**: Once provisioned and rebooted, the ESP32 boots and discovers the mothership via mDNS (spaxel-mothership.local) or the configured host. Poll GET /api/nodes every 3s for up to 120s waiting for the new node to appear. Show animated 'Connecting...' indicator. On timeout: show WiFi troubleshooting guidance (5GHz check, SSID typo check, distance check).

6. **Guided calibration**: Show the CSI waveform for the new node's links as they come online. Steps:
   a. 'Walk around your space for 30 seconds' — CSI amplitude should show activity. If flat: check node orientation.
   b. 'Stand still at the far end of the room' — capture baseline. Show countdown. Green check when baseline is captured.
   c. 'Walk through the centre of the room' — Fresnel zone lights up in 3D view, blob appears. 'The sensor can see you!'
   d. 'Sit down and stay still for 30 seconds' — test stationary detection (if Phase 5 available). Otherwise skip.

7. **Node placement guidance**: Transition to the coverage painting UI (spaxel-qq6) for optimal node positioning. Show GDOP overlay for the current node placement. Suggest additional node positions if coverage is poor.

## Files to Create/Modify

- dashboard/js/onboard.js: wizard state machine, Web Serial API calls, step rendering
- dashboard/index.html: add /onboard route and wizard container div, import esp-web-tools
- mothership/internal/dashboard/routes.go (or similar): add GET /api/firmware/manifest route
- mothership/internal/dashboard/hub.go: no changes needed (wizard uses REST polling, not WebSocket for this flow)

## esp-web-tools Integration



Manifest served at GET /api/firmware/manifest:


## Wizard State Machine

States: BROWSER_CHECK → CONNECT_DEVICE → FLASH_FIRMWARE → PROVISION_WIFI → DETECT_NODE → CALIBRATE → PLACEMENT → COMPLETE

Each state has: render() function, onEnter() side effects, onNext() transition, onBack() for revert, onError() for failure handling.

Persisted in sessionStorage so a page refresh during onboarding resumes from the last step (critical for the reboot-then-detect step).

## Error Handling

Map every known failure to a human-friendly message:
- 'NotFoundError: No port selected' → 'No device detected. Make sure the USB cable is connected and hold the BOOT button while plugging in.'
- 'NetworkError' during flash → 'The connection was interrupted. Check the USB cable is not loose and try again.'
- Node not appearing after 120s → 'Your node connected to WiFi but cannot reach the mothership. Check: 1) Your router blocks device-to-device communication (AP isolation). 2) The mothership address is correct. 3) Your network uses a VLAN that separates devices.'
- Wrong SSID/password → Node will fall into captive portal mode after 10 failures, triggering a 'Captive portal detected' guidance flow.

Never show stack traces, WebSocket error codes, or Go error strings to the user.

## Tests

- Mock navigator.serial API in Jest (using jest-serial-port or a hand-written mock) to test wizard state transitions without real hardware
- Test that provisioning payload is correctly assembled and sent over the mocked serial port
- Test that polling GET /api/nodes correctly detects node appearance and transitions to DETECT_NODE → CALIBRATE
- Test that BROWSER_CHECK step correctly detects missing serial API and shows the correct error
- Test that sessionStorage correctly restores wizard state on page refresh at each step

## Acceptance Criteria

- Wizard completes in under 5 minutes on a fresh ESP32-S3 with a working WiFi network
- User sees live CSI waveform during calibration step
- Node appears in dashboard after wizard completion, with correct label
- All known error conditions show human-friendly guidance, not technical errors
- All existing dashboard tests pass
- Wizard state is resumable after page refresh

title: Fix simple.html blank page (ES module loaded as classic script)
description:
## Problem

`simple.html` renders completely blank. Browser console shows:

```
Unexpected token 'export'
```

An ES-module script is being loaded with a classic `<script>` tag.

## Fix

1. Identify the offending script (likely `js/esptool-bundle.js` or a module imported by `simple.html`).
2. Either:
   - Change the tag to `<script type="module" src="...">`, or
   - Convert the file to a classic script (remove `export`, expose via global).

The existing `type="module"` import of `js/fxaa.js` in `index.html` is the pattern to mirror.

## Verification

- Load `simple.html` locally — page renders with content.
- Browser console shows no syntax errors.
- Playwright screenshot of `simple.html` at 1440×900 is non-blank and follows plan.md §8e design.

Traceability: plan.md §8f item 7.
close_reason: Fixed simple.html blank page caused by notifications.js using ES module export class syntax while being loaded as a classic script tag. Removed the export keyword.

title: Pre-deployment simulator
description:
## Background

A person considering buying 4+ ESP32-S3 nodes wants to know: will this work in my house? Where should I put the nodes? How accurate will it be with 4 nodes vs 6? The pre-deployment simulator lets users model their space, place virtual nodes, and run synthetic walkers to get an expected accuracy estimate — all before spending any money or touching hardware. It is also a powerful teaching tool for understanding how WiFi CSI sensing works.

## Simulator Mode

New dashboard route /simulate. A separate UI state that reuses the 3D scene infrastructure but replaces all live data with simulated data.

The simulator does NOT connect to any hardware or the mothership's live pipeline. It runs entirely in the browser with synthetic CSI generation via a WebAssembly module or JavaScript implementation of the physics model.

(Alternative: run the simulation on the mothership server side, with a separate "simulation session" API endpoint. This is more complex but allows reusing the Go signal processing code directly. Recommend the server-side approach for accuracy.)

## Space Editor

Reuses the 3D editor from the node placement UI (spaxel-qq6). Additional tools for the simulator:
- Wall tool: draw lines on the floor plane that represent walls. Walls are stored as line segments and affect the path loss model (each wall crossing reduces RSSI by ~3-6 dB, configurable).
- Furniture tool: place box-shaped obstacle volumes. Obstacles block direct path and affect Fresnel zone intersection.
- Room dimensions tool: define the room boundaries (already in node placement UI).

## Virtual Node Placement

Same node placement UI as the real dashboard. Virtual nodes have the same placement interface but no hardware connection. Node icons are greyed out to indicate virtual.

The placement UI renders the GDOP overlay (from Phase 3, spaxel-qq6) using the virtual node positions. This is the same GDOP computation as the real system.

## Synthetic Walkers

1-4 animated figures that move through the virtual space. Walker types:
- Random walk: starts at a random position, takes random steps of 0.3-0.8m every 500ms, bounces off walls.
- Path walk: user draws a polyline path in the 3D view. Walker follows the path and loops.
- Zone walk: user selects a set of zones. Walker randomly transitions between zones using the zone transition portal geometry.

Walker animation: rendered as the same humanoid mesh used for real blobs in the 3D view but with a distinctive "ghost" colour (semi-transparent white) to distinguish from real detections.

## Simulated CSI Generation

For each virtual walker position at each 100ms timestep, compute the expected CSI frame for each virtual node pair:

1. Path loss: RSSI = RSSI_at_1m - 20*log10(d) - n_walls * wall_attenuation_db
   where d = direct path distance, n_walls = number of walls crossed, wall_attenuation_db = 4 dB default.

2. Fresnel zone contribution: for the walker at position P, compute the Fresnel zone overlap fraction for each link (TX, RX). This uses the same geometry as the FusionEngine (spaxel-m9a).

3. deltaRMS simulation: expected_delta_rms = fresnel_overlap * signal_amplitude + gaussian_noise(sigma)
   signal_amplitude = 0.05 * exp(-distance_from_fresnel_centre / sigma_fresnel) where sigma_fresnel = 0.3m.
   gaussian_noise sigma is calibrated from real-world measurements (see docs/research/ for empirical noise floor).

4. Generate binary CSI frame in the same format as real hardware (24-byte header + I/Q payload). Feed through the actual mothership signal processing pipeline via a simulation API endpoint.

## Pipeline Integration

The mothership exposes a simulation API: POST /api/simulate/session creates a simulation session. Within a session:
- Virtual nodes are registered as if they had connected via WebSocket
- Synthetic frames are injected via POST /api/simulate/session/{id}/frames
- The standard processing pipeline runs on the injected frames
- Blob positions are returned in the response

The dashboard simulator mode polls this API to get blob positions for each simulation timestep.

## Accuracy Estimation

After running the simulation with N walkers for 30 seconds:
- Collect all blob positions from the mothership pipeline
- Compare to walker ground truth positions (known from the simulation)
- Compute median position error: median(|blob_position - walker_position|) for matched pairs
- Compute false positive rate: blob detections when no walker is in that area
- Compute recall: fraction of walker positions that had a matched blob within 1m

Report: "With this layout, expected accuracy is ±{N}m median error, {M}% detection rate."

## Recommendations Engine

Based on the simulation results, generate actionable layout recommendations:
- "Adding a node near the hallway would reduce the east-side dead zone by ~30% GDOP improvement."
- "Node A and Node B are nearly collinear. Moving Node B 1.5m to the left would improve coverage."
- "With 4 nodes, you can achieve ±0.8m accuracy. Adding a 5th node would improve to ±0.5m."

These recommendations are generated by:
1. Running the GDOP computation for the current layout
2. Identifying zones with GDOP > 2.5 (poor coverage) — dead zone detection
3. Trying a set of candidate additional-node positions and computing GDOP improvement

## Shopping List

Based on the virtual node count in the simulation:
"For this layout you need: {N} × ESP32-S3 Development Board, {N} × USB-C Power Supply (5V 1A), {N} × Adhesive Cable Clips for routing."
Include a pre-filled Amazon search URL template (not an affiliate link, just a query).

## Files to Create or Modify

- mothership/internal/simulator/session.go: SimulationSession, synthetic frame injection API
- mothership/internal/simulator/physics.go: path loss model, Fresnel zone CSI generation
- mothership/internal/simulator/accuracy.go: accuracy estimation, recommendation engine
- dashboard/js/simulate.js: simulator UI, walker rendering, recommendations display
- mothership/internal/dashboard/routes.go: POST/GET /api/simulate/ endpoints

## Tests

- Test Fresnel zone CSI simulation: walker at the midpoint of a TX-RX link should produce delta_rms > 0.03; walker at 2m off-axis should produce delta_rms < 0.01
- Test path loss model: d=1m, n_walls=0 -> RSSI = RSSI_at_1m; d=2m, n_walls=1 -> RSSI = RSSI_at_1m - 6 - 4 = -10 dB relative
- Test accuracy estimation: 1 walker at known position, simulation produces 1 blob within 0.5m -> accuracy report shows ≤ 0.5m error
- Test recommendations engine: GDOP > 2.5 in east corner -> recommendation to add node near east corner

## Acceptance Criteria

- Simulator runs without any hardware (all computation in mothership API + browser)
- GDOP overlay renders correctly for virtual node placements
- Synthetic walkers produce blob detections via the real mothership pipeline
- Accuracy estimate is produced after 30-second simulation run
- Recommendation engine suggests at least one improvement for any layout with a dead zone
- Shopping list rendered with correct node count
- Tests pass

title: Mothership: comprehensive /healthz endpoint
description:
## Overview
Expand the /healthz endpoint from the current minimal response to the full spec required for Docker HEALTHCHECK and Traefik health routing.

## Current state
GET /healthz returns: {status:'ok', version:'...'} — missing required fields

## Required response (plan lines 3507-3510):
Healthy: HTTP 200 — {status:'ok', uptime_s:N, version:'...', nodes_online:N, db:'ok', load_level:0-3}
Degraded: HTTP 503 — {status:'degraded', reason:'...', uptime_s:N, nodes_online:N, db:'ok'|'failing', load_level:0-3}

## Implementation
- Track start_time at mothership boot; compute uptime_s = int(time.Since(start).Seconds())
- nodes_online: query ingestion server's connected node count (atomic counter)
- db health: run 'SELECT 1' against SQLite with 100ms timeout; 'ok' or 'failing'
- load_level: read from load shedding state (spaxel-zvb)
- Degraded conditions: db='failing', or load_level=3 for >60s, or nodes_online=0 after 5min uptime
- reason field: human-readable explanation of degradation

## Docker integration
Update Dockerfile HEALTHCHECK:
HEALTHCHECK --interval=30s --timeout=5s --start-period=15s --retries=3 CMD wget -qO- http://localhost:8080/healthz | grep -q '"status":"ok"' || exit 1

## Acceptance
- GET /healthz returns all required fields with correct types
- HTTP 503 returned when SQLite unreachable (test by renaming DB file)
- uptime_s increments correctly across multiple calls
- Docker HEALTHCHECK transitions to 'healthy' within start-period
close_reason: Comprehensive /healthz endpoint already implemented in commit e44dd34. All required fields present: status, uptime_s, version, nodes_online, db, load_level, reason. Degraded conditions: DB failing, load_level=3 >60s, no nodes after 5min. HTTP 200/503 codes. Docker HEALTHCHECK configured.

title: Phase 5: Reliability & Intelligence
description:
Goal: Production-quality detection for daily home use.

Deliverables:
- Diurnal adaptive baseline (24-slot hourly vectors, 7-day learning, crossfade, confidence indicator)
- Stationary person detection (breathing band 0.1-0.5 Hz, long-dwell logic)
- Ambient confidence score (per-link health: SNR, phase stability, packet rate, drift; composite gauge)

Exit criteria: System runs unattended 7+ days with <5% false positive rate.

title: Fall detection
description:
## Background

Fall detection is one of the highest-value safety features for homes with elderly residents or people with medical conditions. Approximately 1 in 4 adults over 65 fall each year, and a fall without prompt help can be fatal. A system that detects falls without requiring the person to press a button (wearable panic buttons are often not worn consistently) can be life-saving.

A CSI-based fall detector works by analysing the track's Z-position history from the biomechanical blob tracker (spaxel-n9n). A fall is characterised by: rapid Z-axis descent (person goes from standing height ~1.7m to floor level ~0.2m in less than 1 second) followed by sustained stillness (no motion for > 30 seconds at floor level). False positives — sitting down quickly, a child lying on the floor, someone doing yoga — must be minimised through careful gating and the post-fall confirmation step.

## Fall Detection Algorithm

Implemented in mothership/internal/tracker/fall.go or as a method on TrackManager.

State machine per track:
- NORMAL: no fall detected
- DESCENT_DETECTED: Z velocity trigger fired, monitoring for confirmation
- FALL_CONFIRMED: post-fall stillness confirmed, alert active
- ALERT_ACKNOWLEDGED: user acknowledged, monitoring continues

Step 1 — Descent trigger:
Monitor Z position history over a 200ms rolling window. Compute Z velocity (dZ/dt in m/s).
Trigger condition: Z velocity < -1.5 m/s AND total Z drop in last 1s > 0.8m.
On trigger: transition to DESCENT_DETECTED, record trigger_time.

Step 2 — Post-fall confirmation (the critical false-positive reduction step):
After the descent trigger fires:
a. Check that current Z position is < 0.4m (person is at floor level, not just crouching)
b. Check that motion (deltaRMS from the contributing links) is < 0.01 for > 30 consecutive seconds
If both conditions are met: transition to FALL_CONFIRMED, fire alert chain.
If Z rises above 0.4m within the 30-second window (person got up): cancel, return to NORMAL.

Step 3 — False positive suppression:
Apply a higher threshold (drop > 1.2m instead of 0.8m) if the track has a "sitting" or "lying" posture classification in the last 10 minutes (from posture hints in spaxel-n9n). This reduces false alarms for people who sit down quickly or lie down intentionally.
Apply no fall detection in user-defined "children's zones" (zones with the is_children_zone flag) where floor-level activity is normal.

## Alert Chain

1. T+0s: FALL_CONFIRMED transition fires.
   Dashboard: full-width banner with high urgency styling. "Possible fall detected — [person name] in [zone name]. Are you OK? [Acknowledge] [Call for help]"
   Browser notification API (if permission granted): "Spaxel: Possible fall detected — Alice in Hallway"

2. T+2 minutes (without user acknowledgement):
   Webhook/MQTT alert fires (via AutomationEngine with trigger type "fall_detected").
   Push notification via configured channel (ntfy/Pushover).

3. T+5 minutes (without acknowledgement):
   Escalation: second webhook fires to any configured escalation URL (separate from primary webhook). Push notification with "URGENT" prefix. Dashboard banner pulses red.

Acknowledgement: the [Acknowledge] button on the dashboard banner:
- Transitions state to ALERT_ACKNOWLEDGED
- Cancels the T+2min and T+5min timers
- Logs the acknowledgement in the activity timeline with timestamp
- Shows a brief form: "How is [person name]?" — [Fine, just fell] / [Needed help] / [False alarm]
- Feedback stored for accuracy tracking (Phase 7)

## Person Identification in Alerts

If the track has a confirmed BLE identity:
- Alert: "Possible fall detected — Alice in Hallway"
- Notification: includes person name and zone

If the track is anonymous:
- Alert: "Possible fall detected — unknown person in Hallway"
- Fall event still fires and escalates on the same timeline

## Children's Zones

Add is_children_zone boolean flag to the zones table (see portals bead). A zone marked as children's zone suppresses fall detection for all tracks within it. Set via zone editor in the dashboard. Rationale: young children spend significant time at floor level (crawling, playing), and false fall alerts in a nursery or playroom are highly counterproductive.

## Z Position Accuracy Dependency

Fall detection quality is directly tied to the accuracy of 3D position estimates from the tracker (spaxel-n9n). The UKF state includes Z position, but Z is the hardest axis to localise with WiFi CSI — the nodes are typically all at similar heights (wall-mounted or tabletop), so the vertical Fresnel zone geometry is poor. In practice:
- Z accuracy is typically ±0.3m for direct LoS links
- Fall detection requires a Z drop of > 0.8m, which should be detectable even with ±0.3m Z error
- The 30-second stillness confirmation is the primary false-positive filter — it tolerates poor Z accuracy

Document this in the dashboard: "Fall detection works best with nodes at different heights (e.g. one high, one low). Single-level node deployment may miss smaller Z changes."

## Integration with Existing Systems

FallDetector subscribes to TrackManager updates (the same 10 Hz update loop as identity matching). It receives the full TrackState including position history and posture hints.

FallDetector emits FallEvent to the internal event bus on FALL_CONFIRMED. The event bus (Phase 8 activity timeline bead) logs it to the timeline. The AutomationEngine processes it for automation triggers. The spatial context notifications module (Phase 6) sends the push notification.

## Files to Create or Modify

- mothership/internal/tracker/fall.go: FallDetector struct, state machine, descent/stillness detection
- mothership/internal/tracker/manager.go: integrate FallDetector in update loop
- mothership/internal/events/events.go: FallEvent type
- dashboard/js/fall.js: fall alert banner, acknowledgement handler, escalation countdown
- mothership/internal/dashboard/routes.go: POST /api/fall/{event_id}/acknowledge

## Tests

- Test descent trigger: inject synthetic track with Z velocity = -2.0 m/s over 500ms and drop from 1.7m to 0.2m. Verify DESCENT_DETECTED transition.
- Test post-fall confirmation: after descent trigger, inject 30 seconds of deltaRMS < 0.005 at Z = 0.2m. Verify FALL_CONFIRMED transition.
- Test that a quick sit-down (Z drops from 1.7m to 0.5m at -1.0 m/s — below speed threshold) does NOT trigger DESCENT_DETECTED.
- Test false positive suppression: track has "sitting" posture history, then fall of 0.9m fires — verify higher threshold (1.2m) is applied and no trigger for 0.9m drop.
- Test alert chain timing: verify T+2min and T+5min timers fire without acknowledgement.
- Test that acknowledgement cancels pending timers.
- Test that children's zone flag suppresses fall detection.

## Acceptance Criteria

- Fall detector transitions to FALL_CONFIRMED on a simulated fall trajectory (2.0 m/s descent, 1.5m drop, 30s floor stillness)
- Post-fall stillness confirmation fires after 30 consecutive seconds of stillness at Z < 0.4m
- False positive suppression reduces alerts for quick sit-down actions (drop < 0.8m or speed < 1.5 m/s)
- Alert chain fires at T+0, T+2min, T+5min without acknowledgement
- Acknowledgement correctly cancels pending escalation timers
- Person name included in alert when BLE identity is confirmed
- Children's zone flag suppresses all fall detection for tracks within that zone
- Tests pass

title: Bidirectional node protocol
description:
## Background

Phase 1 established the WebSocket connection and binary/JSON frame parsing. Phase 3 requires a richer downstream command set for multi-node coordination. The firmware (firmware/main/websocket.c) already parses these commands — what's missing is the mothership sending them in response to real events.

## What Already Exists
- mothership/internal/ingestion/message.go: hello, health, ble, ota_status, motion_hint message types parsed
- mothership/internal/ingestion/server.go: handles incoming messages, sends initial role and config on connect
- firmware/main/websocket.c: parses role, config, ota, reboot, identify, reject downstream commands

## What to Implement

### 1. NodeSession abstraction
Create mothership/internal/ingestion/session.go: NodeSession struct wrapping a WebSocket connection. Methods: SendRole(role string), SendConfig(sampleRate int, passiveBSSID string), SendOTA(url, md5, version string), SendReboot(), SendIdentify() (triggers LED blink). NodeSession is stored in a registry keyed by nodeMAC.

### 2. Role push after hello
On receiving a hello message, look up the node's assigned role from the fleet manager (spaxel-8u3). If fleet manager not yet wired (Phase 3 in progress), default to 'rx'. Send role command immediately after hello processing.

### 3. Config push
After role push, send config: {type:'config', sample_rate:20, passive_bssid:'' (if passive role, populated from fleet manager)}. This replaces the hardcoded config send in the current server.go.

### 4. OTA trigger
POST /api/nodes/{mac}/ota already planned (OTA bead). Wire NodeSession.SendOTA() to be callable from the fleet/OTA HTTP handler.

### 5. BLE relay to identity service
On receiving a ble message, forward BLE device list to a BLERegistry stub (Phase 6). For now, log at INFO level with structured fields: node_mac, device_count, devices[].

### 6. NodeSession registry
mothership/internal/ingestion/registry.go: thread-safe map[string]*NodeSession. On node disconnect (WebSocket close), remove from registry. Expose: Register(mac, session), Get(mac) (*NodeSession, bool), All() []*NodeSession.

## Key Files
- mothership/internal/ingestion/server.go — main handler to refactor
- mothership/internal/ingestion/message.go — message type reference
- firmware/main/websocket.c — firmware-side command parsing (read-only reference)

## Acceptance Criteria
- Node receives role push within 100ms of hello
- Config push follows role push with correct sample_rate
- NodeSession registry correctly tracks connected nodes
- NodeSession.Get(mac) returns nil for disconnected node
- BLE messages logged at INFO with correct fields
- go test ./internal/ingestion/... passes
close_reason: Implemented: ingestion/server.go + ratecontrol.go (bcfd1e3) — role/config/OTA push within 100ms of hello, NodeSession abstraction, firmware websocket.c+csi.c dynamic rate

title: Trigger spaxel CI build and verify ardenone-cluster deployment
description:
## Problem

The spaxel deployment on ardenone-cluster has been stuck in ErrImagePull for 4+ days:

  Pod: spaxel-7998b64b7b-ff2qd (namespace: spaxel)
  Error: docker.io/ronaldraygun/spaxel:0.1.0: not found

The image has never been built/pushed to Docker Hub. The ArgoCD app (spaxel-ns-ardenone-cluster) is Synced but health is Degraded.

## What's in place

- Manifests: declarative-config/k8s/ardenone-cluster/spaxel/ (deployment, service, namespace, PVC) ✓
- Secret: docker-hub-registry in spaxel namespace ✓
- WorkflowTemplate: spaxel-build deployed to iad-ci argo-workflows ✓
- ArgoCD ApplicationSet auto-syncs from declarative-config ✓

## Steps

1. Trigger the spaxel-build WorkflowTemplate on iad-ci to build and push ronaldraygun/spaxel:0.1.0
   kubectl --kubeconfig=/home/coding/.kube/iad-ci.kubeconfig create -f - <<EOF
   apiVersion: argoproj.io/v1alpha1
   kind: Workflow
   metadata:
     generateName: spaxel-build-manual-
     namespace: argo-workflows
   spec:
     workflowTemplateRef:
       name: spaxel-build
   EOF

2. Monitor the build workflow until completion

3. Verify the pod comes up healthy on ardenone-cluster:
   /usr/local/bin/kubectl --server=http://traefik-ardenone-cluster:8001 get pods -n spaxel

4. Confirm ArgoCD app health flips to Healthy:
   curl -sk https://argocd-ro-ardenone-manager-ts.ardenone.com:8444/api/v1/applications/spaxel-ns-ardenone-cluster | python3 -c 'import json,sys; s=json.load(sys.stdin)["status"]; print(s["health"]["status"])'

title: Spatial quick actions context menu
description:
## Background

The 3D scene contains many interactive elements: humanoid track blobs, node spheres, zone cuboids, portal planes, trigger volumes, and empty space. Performing operations on these elements — labelling a track, triggering OTA, creating an automation for a zone — currently requires navigating through multiple panels and menus. The context menu provides a faster, more discoverable path: right-click or long-press on any element, and immediately see the most relevant actions for that element.

This is a UI polish bead, but one with significant usability impact for power users who frequently interact with the 3D scene.

## Raycasting and Element Detection

When a right-click (or 300ms long-press on touch) event fires on the Three.js canvas:
1. Convert the mouse/touch position to normalised device coordinates (NDC): x = (event.clientX / canvas.width) * 2 - 1, y = -(event.clientY / canvas.height) * 2 + 1
2. Create a THREE.Raycaster and call raycaster.setFromCamera(ndcCoords, camera)
3. Cast the ray against the following scene object groups (in priority order, check outermost first):
   a. Track blobs and humanoid meshes (highest priority — users click on people most often)
   b. Node spheres/cylinders
   c. Zone cuboids (bounding box meshes)
   d. Portal plane meshes
   e. Trigger volume bounding boxes
   f. Ground plane (for empty space context menu — always intersects last)
4. Take the closest intersection with a ray distance check (ignore intersections behind the camera)
5. Identify the element type from the mesh's userData.type field (set at mesh creation time: "track", "node", "zone", "portal", "trigger_volume", "ground")

## Context Menu Rendering

The context menu is an HTML div overlay (not a Three.js object) positioned at the mouse/touch coordinates. It must:
- Appear instantly (no animation delay)
- Stay within viewport bounds (reposition if near edges)
- Dismiss on: Escape key, click anywhere outside, second right-click, Tab
- Not interfere with OrbitControls (prevent camera orbit while menu is open: set controls.enabled = false while menu is visible, restore on dismiss)

Context menu HTML: a `div.context-menu` with `ul.context-menu-list` containing `li.context-menu-item` elements. Each item has an icon (SVG inline) and a label. Dividers are `li.context-menu-divider`.

CSS: box-shadow, border-radius, background #1e293b (dark), 14px font, 36px min item height, hover state highlight.

## Menu Items Per Element Type

Track/blob:
- "Who is this? Assign label" — opens the People & Devices panel filtered to this track
- "Follow (camera)" — enables follow-camera mode for this track
- "View history" — jumps activity timeline to this track's events (filter by track_id)
- "Mark as false positive" — shortcut to the feedback form with FALSE_POSITIVE pre-selected
- "Explain detection" — triggers explain mode (spaxel-ez4 explainability overlay)
- divider
- "Set as unknown (anonymous)" — removes identity assignment from this track

Node:
- "Edit label" — opens an inline edit field directly on the node's label in the 3D scene
- "View health details" — opens the link health panel focused on this node's links
- "Trigger OTA update" — triggers OTA for this specific node (with confirmation dialog if node is the last online)
- "Locate node (blink LED)" — sends a blink/identify command via WebSocket to the node
- "Re-assign role" — opens a role picker (TX/RX/TX-RX/passive) inline
- divider
- "Remove from fleet" — opens confirmation: "This will disconnect Node [label] and remove its data."

Empty space:
- "Add virtual node here" — places a new virtual node at the clicked ground position (for placement simulation)
- "Create zone here" — starts zone creation mode with the clicked position as one corner
- "Set as home point" — sets the coordinate origin to this position (recentres the floor plan)
- "Place portal here" — starts portal creation mode centred at this position

Zone:
- "Edit zone bounds" — enters zone edit mode (drag handles to resize)
- "Rename zone" — inline rename in the zone label
- "View occupancy history" — opens timeline filtered to this zone's transition events
- "Create automation for this zone" — opens the automation builder with this zone pre-selected as trigger target
- divider
- "Delete zone" — with confirmation

Portal:
- "Edit portal" — enters portal edit mode (move/resize)
- "View crossing history" — opens timeline filtered to portal crossing events for this portal
- divider
- "Delete portal" — with confirmation

Trigger volume:
- "Edit trigger" — opens the automation associated with this volume in the automation builder
- "Test fire" — fires the automation with test_mode=true flag
- "Enable / Disable" — toggles the automation's enabled flag
- divider
- "Delete trigger volume" — deletes the volume and its associated automation trigger

## Follow Camera Mode

When "Follow (camera)" is selected from a track's context menu:
1. Camera enters follow mode: every render frame, camera.position and camera.target are smoothly interpolated toward a position N metres behind and M metres above the track's current position. N=3m, M=2m default (third-person camera).
2. A "Following: Alice" chip appears in the top-left corner of the 3D canvas.
3. Camera zoom and pan are disabled during follow mode (OrbitControls disabled).
4. "Unfollow" button in the chip: click to exit follow mode and restore OrbitControls.
5. If the track is deleted or becomes DELETED state: automatically exit follow mode.

The interpolation uses Three.js VectorLerp and QuaternionSlerp (or equivalent). The follow distance can be adjusted with the scroll wheel even during follow mode (override only the dolly part of OrbitControls).

## Files to Create or Modify

- dashboard/js/contextmenu.js: ContextMenuManager, raycasting, menu rendering, action dispatch
- dashboard/js/camera.js (or app.js): follow camera mode logic
- dashboard/js/contextmenu.css: context menu styles
- dashboard/js/app.js: register right-click and long-press listeners, integrate ContextMenuManager

## Tests

- Test raycasting correctly identifies element type: mock scene with a "track" mesh and a "node" mesh; raycast at the track's screen position -> returns element type "track"
- Test that correct menu items appear for each element type: mock scene with one of each type, right-click each, verify menu item labels match the specification
- Test that "Follow" camera mode activates: verify controls.enabled = false and camera follow interpolation fires on each render frame
- Test dismiss on Escape key, click outside, and second right-click
- Test that menu stays within viewport: when context menu would extend beyond right edge, it repositions to left of cursor
- Test "Trigger OTA" opens confirmation dialog when node is last online
- Test "Mark as false positive" dispatches correct feedback event

## Acceptance Criteria

- Context menu appears on right-click for all element types in under 50ms
- Correct action set shown for each element type (no irrelevant actions)
- "Follow" camera mode smoothly tracks the selected track with correct camera offset
- "Unfollow" exits follow mode and restores normal OrbitControls
- All menu actions execute correctly (dispatching to the correct handler)
- Menu repositions to stay within viewport bounds
- Menu dismisses on Escape, click outside, and second right-click
- Tests pass
close_reason:
Implemented spatial quick actions context menu for 3D scene with all required functionality:

- Raycasting with priority order for element detection
- Context menu rendering (<50ms response time)
- Element-specific actions for tracks, nodes, zones, portals, triggers, and empty space
- Follow camera mode with smooth interpolation (3m behind, 2m above default)
- Unfollow button and auto-exit when track deleted
- Scroll wheel zoom adjustment during follow mode
- OrbitControls disabled during follow mode
- Menu dismisses on Escape, click outside, and second right-click
- Viewport-aware positioning

All 22 tests pass covering raycasting, menu items, follow mode, dismissal behavior, action execution, and performance.

title: Build guided troubleshooting
description: Proactive contextual help and post-feedback explanations.
close_reason:
Implemented Component 36: Guided Troubleshooting with proactive contextual help.

Backend:
- FleetNotifier: Track node offline events with 2-hour threshold before triggering help
- EditTracker: Monitor repeated settings changes (3+ within 60min triggers hint)
- ZoneQualityTracker: Detect degraded detection quality (>24h below 60%)
- DiscoveryTracker: First-time feature discovery tooltips (shown once per feature)
- Manager: Coordinates all guided troubleshooting features with 5min periodic checks
- REST API: /api/guided/* endpoints for issues, tooltips, feedback, calibration

Frontend:
- tooltip.js: Feature discovery tooltips with server-side coordination
- tooltips.js: Sequential tooltip tour manager for first-time users  
- troubleshoot.js: Troubleshooting manager for quality/offline/calibration events
- guided-help.js: Step-by-step guidance content

Triggers:
- Detection quality drops below 60% for >24 hours
- Same setting key modified 3+ times in 60 minutes
- Node offline for >2 hours
- First-time feature access (tooltips)
- After false positive feedback (explanation + adjustment offer)
- After successful calibration (positive reinforcement)

Design principles:
- Reactive, not proactive: Help appears only when something seems wrong
- Dismissible in one tap: Never blocks the UI
- Never repeats after dismissal (stored in localStorage + server)
- Always explains what will happen next
- Never condescending: Assumes the user is intelligent

title: Consolidate duplicate dashboard component files (command-palette, ble-panel)
description:
## Problem

The dashboard ships two implementations of the same components:

- `js/command-palette.js` + `css/command-palette.css` **vs** `js/commandpalette.js` + `css/commandpalette.css`
- `js/ble-panel.js` + `css/ble-panel.css` **vs** `js/blepanel.js`

Both are loaded by `index.html`, inflating CSS bytes and causing subtle conflicts.

## Fix

For each duplicate pair:

1. Pick the canonical version (the hyphenated one — matches `fleet-page.*` naming convention).
2. Diff the two implementations to ensure no unique logic is lost from the version being removed.
3. Delete the non-canonical files.
4. Update all references in `index.html`, `ambient.html`, `simple.html`, `fleet.html`, `integrations.html`, and any JS that imports them.
5. Run the existing Jest suite (`commandpalette.test.js` may need to be renamed to `command-palette.test.js`).

## Verification

- No 404s in the DevTools Network tab on dashboard load.
- `grep -r commandpalette dashboard/` returns no results (similarly for `blepanel`).
- Command palette still opens with Cmd/Ctrl+K.
- BLE panel still renders and scans.

Traceability: plan.md §8f item 2, §8e "Component library".

title: Detection explainability overlay
description:
## Background

When a blob appears in an unexpected position, or an alert fires that seems wrong, the first question is "why?" The explainability overlay answers this question visually in the 3D scene, without requiring the user to understand deltaRMS, Fresnel zones, or UKF — though the data is available for those who want it. This transforms a "magic box" into a comprehensible physical system.

This is also the most important debugging tool for a developer tuning the system: seeing which links contributed most to a blob position, and by how much, is the fastest path to understanding localisation errors.

## ExplainabilitySnapshot

The FusionEngine (spaxel-m9a) is extended to emit an ExplainabilitySnapshot alongside each BlobUpdate. This snapshot contains all the data needed to explain why a specific blob appeared at a specific position.

ExplainabilitySnapshot struct (mothership/internal/fusion/explain.go):
- blob_id: the ID of the blob being explained
- blob_position: Vec3 — final estimated position
- per_link_contributions: []LinkContribution
  - link_id, tx_mac, rx_mac
  - weight float64 — the geometric Fresnel weight for this blob position
  - learned_weight float64 — the learned spatial weight (from weight learner, Phase 7)
  - combined_weight float64 = weight * learned_weight
  - delta_rms float64 — the current deltaRMS for this link
  - contribution_pct float64 — percentage of total fusion score contributed by this link
  - fresnel_intersection_volume float64 — volume of Fresnel zone ellipsoid that overlaps the blob's voxel (proxy for "how much does this link see this position")
- ble_match: optional — if identity is matched: {device_mac, person_id, person_label, ble_distance_m, triangulation_confidence}
- fusion_score float64 — total occupancy grid score at blob position
- timestamp of snapshot

The snapshot is broadcast via WebSocket as "blob_explain" message type, alongside the regular "blob_update". The frontend requests a snapshot by sending {"type":"request_explain","blob_id":"..."} — the server then enriches the next blob update with the explain data.

## 3D Explain Mode UI

Right-click (desktop) or long-press (mobile, 300ms) on any blob/track in the Three.js scene triggers explain mode.

Scene transformation in explain mode:
1. All link lines dim to 20% opacity (using THREE.MeshBasicMaterial.opacity)
2. Contributing links — those with contribution_pct > 2% — increase to 100% opacity and glow with colour intensity mapped to contribution_pct (low contribution = pale blue, high contribution = bright yellow)
3. First Fresnel zone ellipsoids rendered for each contributing link: THREE.Mesh with SphereGeometry scaled by (a, b, b) and rotated to the link axis, translucent wireframe + fill (opacity 0.1). The ellipsoid colour matches the link line colour.
4. A "blob explanation panel" (sidebar overlay, not a Three.js object) shows the breakdown:
   - Blob position in metres: "Detected at (3.2m, 1.8m, 1.0m)"
   - Fusion score: "Detection confidence: [N]%"
   - Contributing links table: link name, contribution %, deltaRMS, health score — sorted by contribution descending
   - Motion sparkline: small 30-second deltaRMS chart per link (uses the recording buffer data if available, otherwise the in-memory history)
   - BLE match details: "Identity: Alice (BLE triangulation, confidence 82%, 0.4m from blob)"
   - If no BLE match: "Identity: Unknown (no BLE device match)"

Exit explain mode: click anywhere outside the blob, or press Escape. Scene returns to normal opacity levels.

## Fresnel Ellipsoid Geometry

The first Fresnel zone ellipsoid geometry for a link:
- TX position P1, RX position P2
- Link distance d = |P1 - P2|
- WiFi wavelength lambda = 0.06m (5 GHz) or 0.125m (2.4 GHz) — use the channel from the node's hello message
- Semi-major axis: a = (d + lambda/2) / 2
- Semi-minor axis: b = sqrt(a^2 - (d/2)^2)
- Centre: midpoint(P1, P2)
- Orientation: the major axis is along the P1->P2 unit vector

In Three.js: SphereGeometry with radius=1, then scale (a, b, b) with the correct rotation matrix (use THREE.Quaternion.setFromUnitVectors to align with P1->P2 direction).

## Motion Sparkline

For each contributing link in the explanation panel, show a 30-second history of deltaRMS as a small canvas sparkline (using the existing amplitude history if available from the dashboard WebSocket connection, or fetching from GET /api/recordings/{link_id}/recent?seconds=30 if the recording buffer is available).

The sparkline shows the moment of detection as a vertical line at the right edge. A horizontal dashed line shows the current motion threshold. Visually conveying "the signal crossed the threshold at this moment."

## Files to Create or Modify

- mothership/internal/fusion/explain.go: ExplainabilitySnapshot, emission logic in FusionEngine
- mothership/internal/fusion/engine.go: extend to emit ExplainabilitySnapshot alongside BlobUpdate
- dashboard/js/explain.js: explain mode 3D scene transforms, sidebar panel
- dashboard/js/fresnel.js: Fresnel ellipsoid geometry helper (reused by Fresnel debug overlay bead)
- mothership/internal/dashboard/hub.go: blob_explain WebSocket message type

## Tests

- Test ExplainabilitySnapshot generation: with 3 known links and a blob at a known position, verify per_link_contributions are computed correctly
- Test contribution_pct sums to approximately 100% across all links with non-zero weight
- Test Fresnel ellipsoid geometry: for TX at (0,0,0) and RX at (4,0,0) with lambda=0.06: a ≈ 2.015, b ≈ 0.345. Verify these values from the geometry computation.
- Test that explain mode correctly dims/highlights links in the Three.js scene (test via scene state inspection, not visual rendering)
- Test that WebSocket "request_explain" message triggers snapshot emission in the next update cycle
- Test sidebar panel rendering with mock ExplainabilitySnapshot data

## Acceptance Criteria

- Right-click on any blob triggers explain mode with correct contributing link highlighting
- Fresnel ellipsoids render at correct positions and sizes for all contributing links
- Confidence breakdown panel shows per-link contributions that sum to 100%
- Non-contributing links visually dimmed in explain mode
- Motion sparklines show 30-second history for each contributing link
- BLE match details shown when identity is available
- Escaping explain mode restores all link opacities to normal
- Tests pass
close_reason: Detection explainability overlay complete. Backend: ExplainabilitySnapshot with per-link contributions, Fresnel zone ellipsoids, BLE identity. Frontend: explain.js entry point, explainability.js sidebar/X-ray overlay/sparklines, fresnel.js geometry. WebSocket blob_explain message type. All Go tests pass, go vet clean.

title: Dashboard WebSocket: snapshot-on-connect + incremental update protocol
description:
## Overview
Implement the snapshot+incremental WebSocket protocol so the dashboard renders immediately on connect without waiting for a full state cycle.

## Protocol spec

### On new /ws/dashboard connection (within 100 ms):
Send a full snapshot message:
  {type: 'snapshot', blobs: [...], nodes: [...], zones: [...], links: [...], alerts: [...], ble_devices: [...], triggers: [...], timestamp_ms: N}

### Subsequent messages (at 10 Hz):
Omit type field; send only state that changed since last tick:
  {blobs: [...], nodes: [...], confidence: 0.87, timestamp_ms: N}
Unchanged arrays may be omitted entirely (null = no change)

## Implementation (mothership/internal/dashboard/hub.go)

- Hub maintains lastSnapshot: full state snapshot updated on each tick
- On new client connection: serialize lastSnapshot as JSON, send immediately
- On each tick: compute delta (changed fields only); broadcast to all established clients
- Snapshot must be sent before the client is added to the broadcast list to avoid race

## Reconnect handling (dashboard/js/app.js)
- On WebSocket open: set awaitingSnapshot = true
- On first message: if type === 'snapshot', merge into app state and clear flag
- On subsequent messages: apply as incremental updates

## Performance requirement
- Snapshot delivery: < 100 ms after connection established, even with 10+ blobs, 16+ nodes, 20+ zones
- Test: connect client, measure time to first render; must be < 150 ms end-to-end

## Acceptance
- Browser devtools shows first WS message with type='snapshot' within 100 ms of upgrade
- Subsequent messages at 10 Hz omit type field
- Reconnect after 5s disconnection shows correct current state immediately

title: Implement Internal Event Bus

Provide bus.Publish(EventType, EventPayload) and bus.Subscribe(EventType) returning a channel. Support multiple subscribers per event type with fan-out.

title: Anomaly detection: 7-day pattern learning algorithm
description:
## Overview
Implement the statistical pattern learning engine for anomaly detection — per-zone, per-hour-of-day, per-day-of-week occupancy modeling using Welford's online algorithm.

## Backend (mothership/analytics/ or signal/)
- Pattern model: per (zone_id, hour_of_day, day_of_week): mean_count, variance, sample_count via Welford's algorithm
- Hourly update goroutine: every hour, observe zone occupancy counts and update model
- Cold start: suppress all anomaly alerts for 7 days; model slot 'ready' when sample_count >= 50
- Anomaly scoring:
  - z_score = (observed_count - mean) / sqrt(variance + epsilon)
  - time_score = normalized z_score for this hour/day combo
  - zone_score = 1.0 if zone normally empty at this time, else 0.0
  - composite_score = max(time_score, zone_score) with fallback
  - threshold: alert if composite > 0.85; yellow warning at 0.60
- Outlier protection: skip model update when anomaly_score >= 0.5 (don't learn from anomalies)
- Security mode override: any detection = score 1.0 regardless of model
- SQLite anomaly_patterns table: zone_id, hour_of_day (0-23), day_of_week (0-6), mean_count REAL, variance REAL, sample_count INT, updated_at INT

## REST API
- GET /api/anomalies?since=24h — list recent anomaly events with scores
- GET /api/anomaly_patterns?zone= — inspect pattern model for debugging

## Acceptance
- Pattern model survives server restart (persisted to SQLite)
- No alerts during 7-day cold start regardless of activity
- Welford update is numerically stable: no NaN/Inf at any sample count
- Outlier protection confirmed: injecting synthetic anomaly does not corrupt model after 3 occurrences
- Requires: spaxel-jcc (phase 6 integration)

title: fix: apdetector imports wrong module prefix (jedarden vs spaxel)
description:
## Problem
`internal/apdetector/detector.go:14` imports `github.com/jedarden/spaxel/mothership/internal/oui` but the module is `github.com/spaxel/mothership`.

## Fix
In `mothership/internal/apdetector/detector.go` line 14, change:
```go
"github.com/jedarden/spaxel/mothership/internal/oui"
```
to:
```go
"github.com/spaxel/mothership/internal/oui"
```

## Verify
```bash
cd /home/coding/spaxel/mothership && PATH=$PATH:/home/coding/go/bin go build ./internal/apdetector/
```
Must compile with no errors.

title: BLE device discovery & registration dashboard panel
description:
## Overview
People & Devices panel for discovering, registering, and labeling BLE devices seen by the fleet.

## Dashboard (dashboard/js/ble-panel.js)
- 'People & Devices' panel (via spaxel-896 panel framework)
- Discovered devices list: sorted by sighting frequency; shows MAC (truncated), name, RSSI, last seen, type icon
- Registration UI: click device → assign label, type (person/pet/object), color
- Auto-type hints from manufacturer data: iPhone, Apple Watch, Fitbit, Tile, AirTag
- Manual pre-registration by address (for tracker tags not yet seen)
- Unregistered count badge on panel toggle button

## Backend
- SQLite ble_devices table: addr, label, type, color, icon, first_seen, last_seen, last_rssi, sighting_count
- GET /api/ble/devices?registered=true|false — filter registered vs discovered
- PUT /api/ble/devices/{mac} — set label, type, color, assign to person
- GET /api/ble/devices/{mac}/history — sighting timeline

## Acceptance
- Panel shows all devices seen in last 24h by default
- Label assignment persists across server restart
- Registered devices show up with name in 3D blob labels and timeline events
- Requires: spaxel-896 (panel framework), spaxel-9eg (BLE WS feed)

title: Add mobile performance optimizations
description:
On screens < 1024px width, cap devicePixelRatio at 2.0, disable or reduce shadow map quality, use FXAA instead of MSAA if needed, and optionally cap frame rate at 30fps.

**Files:** dashboard/js/app.js or dashboard/js/expert.js (renderer initialization)

**Acceptance Criteria:**
- devicePixelRatio capped at 2.0 on mobile (Math.min(window.devicePixelRatio, 2.0))
- Shadow maps disabled on mobile (or capped at 512x512)
- FXAA used instead of MSAA on mobile if antialiasing needed
- Frame rate optionally capped at 30fps on struggling devices
close_reason: Mobile performance optimizations already implemented: devicePixelRatio capped at 2.0 on mobile, shadow maps disabled on mobile, FXAA replaces MSAA on mobile, frame rate capped at 30fps on struggling devices with auto-detection. All acceptance criteria met. Go tests and vet pass.
