title: Dashboard: PIN setup, login, and session cookie authentication
description:
## Overview
Protect the dashboard with a PIN that is set on first run and verified on every subsequent visit via session cookies.

## Backend (mothership/internal/auth/)
- SQLite auth table: pin_bcrypt TEXT, install_secret TEXT (singleton row)
- GET /api/auth/status — return {pin_configured: bool} — no auth required
- POST /api/auth/setup — body: {pin:'1234'} — only works if pin not yet configured; bcrypt cost=12; store hash
- POST /api/auth/login — body: {pin:'1234'} — verify bcrypt; on success issue session cookie:
    Name: spaxel_session, HttpOnly, SameSite=Strict (if TLS), Path=/, Max-Age=604800
    Store session_id → expires_at in SQLite sessions table
- POST /api/auth/logout — clear session cookie; delete session from SQLite
- Session middleware: all /api/* and /ws/* require valid session cookie; return 401 if missing/expired
- Rolling window: on each authenticated request, if within 24h of expiry, extend by 7 days

## Dashboard (dashboard/js/auth.js)
- On load: GET /api/auth/status; if pin_configured=false → show first-run PIN setup page (full-screen, blocks dashboard)
- First-run page: enter PIN + confirm PIN → POST /api/auth/setup → reload
- Login page: shown on 401; PIN entry form → POST /api/auth/login → reload on success
- Logout button in settings panel → POST /api/auth/logout → redirect to login

## Acceptance
- Fresh install: setup page shown before any dashboard content
- After PIN set: login required on next visit
- Session cookie survives page refresh; expires after 7 days of inactivity
- 401 returned immediately for any /api/ call without valid cookie

title: BLE-to-blob identity matching
description:
## Background

The BLE device registry (spaxel-2wg) tells us which labelled devices are present in the home and their per-node RSSI. The CSI fusion engine (spaxel-m9a) and blob tracker (spaxel-n9n) tell us where anonymous humanoid figures are located in 3D space. Identity matching bridges these two systems: given named BLE devices with per-node RSSI observations, and anonymous CSI blobs with estimated 3D positions, assign device identities to blobs.

When a match is confident, the humanoid figure in the 3D view gains a name label and person colour. "Alice is in the Kitchen" becomes a real-time fact rather than an approximation.

## RSSI Triangulation Algorithm

For each BLE device with observations from multiple nodes in the current scan cycle (last 5 seconds):

Step 1: Convert RSSI to distance for each observing node.
Distance model: d = d0 * 10^((RSSI_ref - RSSI) / (10 * n))
Parameters: d0 = 1.0m (reference distance), RSSI_ref = -65 dBm (RSSI at 1m in typical indoor environment), n = 2.5 (indoor path loss exponent — typical range 2.0-3.5; 2.5 is a reasonable default for residential spaces).

Step 2: With 2+ nodes, perform weighted least squares triangulation.
Position estimate: argmin_P { sum_i w_i * (|P - node_i| - d_i)^2 }
where w_i = 1/sigma_i^2, sigma_i = d_i * ln(10) / (10 * n) * RSSI_noise_sigma (typically 5 dBm).
Solve with 3-5 iterations of gradient descent or Gauss-Newton (the system is nonlinear).
With 1 node: only an approximate range estimate; triangulation confidence = 0.2.
With 2 nodes: 2D position estimate on a circle/arc; confidence = 0.5.
With 3+ nodes: full 2D position estimate with residual as quality indicator; confidence = min(1.0, 0.7 + 0.1 * (n-3)) where n is node count.

Step 3: Assign to nearest CSI blob.
For each triangulated BLE position, find the nearest active CSI blob (from TrackManager) within a 2-metre search radius. The assignment uses Euclidean distance in the horizontal plane (ignore Z for BLE since antenna height is variable).

If multiple BLE devices map to the same blob (e.g. Alice has a phone AND a Fitbit), use the device with the highest triangulation confidence.

If a BLE device position is triangulated but no CSI blob is within 2 metres: create a "BLE-only" placeholder track at the BLE position. BLE-only tracks are shown as a different visual (outlined circle rather than filled, lower opacity) to indicate lower confidence.

## Match Confidence Score

For each BLE-to-blob assignment, compute a match confidence:
confidence = f_observations * f_node_count * f_residual * f_distance

where:
- f_observations: 1.0 if device seen in last 5s, 0.5 if last 15s, 0.0 if older
- f_node_count: 0.2 (1 node), 0.5 (2 nodes), 0.8+ (3+ nodes)
- f_residual: 1.0 - min(1.0, triangulation_residual_metres / 2.0) — penalise poor triangulation fit
- f_distance: 1.0 if blob distance < 0.5m, linear decay to 0 at 2.0m

Only assign identity if confidence > 0.6. Below this threshold: keep the blob anonymous ("Unknown").

## Identity Persistence

Once a BLE-blob match is made, the identity persists for 5 minutes even if:
- The BLE device rotates its MAC (new MAC will match same person via the person record)
- The BLE device briefly falls below RSSI threshold of all nodes (indoor dead zone)
- The CSI blob briefly disappears (tracker coasted state, per spaxel-n9n)

After 5 minutes of no BLE observation and no high-confidence re-match: revert to anonymous.

This 5-minute persistence prevents flickering identity labels when people walk through areas with inconsistent BLE coverage.

## TrackManager Integration

Extend the TrackState struct in mothership/internal/tracker/ (spaxel-n9n):
- PersonID string (from BLE registry people table)
- PersonLabel string
- PersonColor string
- IdentityConfidence float64
- IdentitySource string ("ble_triangulation" or "ble_only" or "")

TrackManager.UpdateIdentities(blePositions map[deviceMAC]Vec3, registry BLERegistry) method: runs the matching algorithm and updates track identity fields.

Called at the same frequency as FusionEngine (10 Hz), but BLE positions only update at 5s intervals — cache the last BLE positions and re-use them between BLE updates.

## API

GET /api/tracks enriches the existing track list with person_id, person_label, person_color, and identity_confidence fields.

Example response:
[{
  "id": "track-1",
  "position": {"x": 3.2, "y": 1.8, "z": 1.0},
  "velocity": {"x": 0.1, "y": -0.2, "z": 0.0},
  "confidence": 0.87,
  "posture_hint": "standing",
  "person_id": "uuid-alice",
  "person_label": "Alice",
  "person_color": "#3b82f6",
  "identity_confidence": 0.82
}]

## Dashboard Integration

In the 3D view (Three.js scene): tracks with confirmed identity display a floating text label (person name) above the humanoid figure mesh, rendered using THREE.Sprite with a canvas texture containing the name in the person's colour.

In the "People and Devices" panel: each person row shows "Currently: Kitchen" (current zone from room transition portals, Phase 6) and "In 3D view: [jump to track]" link.

## Tests

- Test RSSI-to-distance conversion with known values: RSSI=-65 -> d=1.0m, RSSI=-75 -> d=2.5m (with default params)
- Test triangulation with 3 nodes at known positions and known distances: verify position error < 0.5m
- Test nearest-blob assignment: 2 blobs at (2,2) and (5,5), BLE device triangulated at (2.3,1.9) -> assigns to first blob
- Test confidence gate: confidence=0.55 -> no assignment; confidence=0.65 -> assignment made
- Test BLE-only placeholder track creation when no blob within 2m
- Test identity persistence: after BLE device disappears, track retains identity for 5 minutes then reverts
- Test identity handoff when BLE device MAC rotates: same person assignment maintained via person record

## Acceptance Criteria

- Named identity labels appear on 3D blobs when person carries a labelled BLE device with confidence > 0.6
- Identity is maintained through brief BLE dead zones (up to 5 minutes)
- Two people in the same room get correct distinct identities when their BLE devices are distinguishable
- Confidence gate prevents wrong assignments (no identity label when confidence < 0.6)
- BLE-only placeholder tracks appear for people whose BLE device is heard but no CSI blob is nearby
- Tests pass

title: Fresnel zone debug overlay
description:
## Background

The Fresnel zone geometry is at the heart of the fusion algorithm (spaxel-m9a). Every link's contribution to the 3D occupancy grid is weighted by how much of the candidate voxel falls within the first Fresnel zone ellipsoid. When debugging why localisation is placing a blob in the wrong position, being able to see the actual Fresnel ellipsoids for each active link — overlaid on the 3D scene — instantly reveals whether the zones are covering the right area.

This is a pure debug/developer tool. It is toggle-able (off by default) and intended for system tuners and developers, not end users. The explainability overlay (spaxel-ez4) already shows Fresnel ellipsoids for a specific selected blob. The Fresnel zone debug overlay shows them persistently for all active links simultaneously.

## Ellipsoid Geometry (Recap)

For each active link with TX at position P1 and RX at position P2:
- Link distance d = |P1 - P2|
- WiFi channel wavelength lambda: 5 GHz -> lambda = 0.06m, 2.4 GHz -> lambda = 0.125m. Use the channel reported in the node's hello message (or the channel from the link's last received CSI frame header).
- Semi-major axis: a = (d + lambda/2) / 2
- Semi-minor axis: b = sqrt(a^2 - (d/2)^2)
- Ellipsoid centre: midpoint(P1, P2)
- Ellipsoid orientation: major axis along the P1->P2 unit vector

The first Fresnel zone ellipsoid represents the region where a point reflector would shorten the signal path by at most half a wavelength relative to the direct path. Motion within this region has maximum impact on CSI.

## Three.js Rendering

Ellipsoid mesh construction:
1. Start with THREE.SphereGeometry(1, 32, 16) — a unit sphere
2. Apply non-uniform scaling: new THREE.Vector3(a, b, b) via mesh.scale.set(a, b, b)
3. Rotate to align with the link axis: compute the quaternion that maps (1,0,0) to the P1->P2 unit vector using THREE.Quaternion.setFromUnitVectors(new THREE.Vector3(1,0,0), linkAxis)
4. Apply the quaternion to mesh.quaternion

Material: TWO materials:
- Wireframe: THREE.LineSegments with EdgesGeometry for crisp wireframe edges, line color matching the link line colour, opacity 0.6
- Fill: THREE.MeshBasicMaterial with transparent=true, opacity 0.08, colour matching the link, depthWrite=false (so the fill doesn't obscure other geometry)

Layer toggle: add "Fresnel Zones" checkbox to the 3D layer control panel (in the "Debug" section, only visible when expert mode is active). Default: off. When toggled on, add all ellipsoid meshes to the scene. When toggled off, remove them.

## Per-Link Controls

When a user hovers over a Fresnel ellipsoid in the 3D scene (using Three.js raycasting):
- The corresponding link line highlights (brightness increase)
- A tooltip appears (HTML overlay, positioned at screen coordinates of the hover point):
  "Link: [tx_label] to [rx_label]
   Fresnel zone radius at midpoint: {b:.2f}m
   Link distance: {d:.2f}m
   Wavelength: {lambda:.3f}m (channel {ch})
   Link health: {health_score:.0%}"

Clicking an ellipsoid:
- Selects the corresponding link in the link panel (sidebar)
- Highlights the link entry in the link list

## Performance Considerations

With 6 active links, we render 6 pairs of meshes (wireframe + fill = 12 Three.js objects). This is negligible for any modern GPU. However, the wireframe geometry uses EdgesGeometry which creates one Line for each edge — for a sphere with 32 horizontal and 16 vertical segments, that's approximately 1000 line segments per ellipsoid. At 6 links, 6000 line segments total. This should render at 60 fps on any modern device, but if performance is an issue on mobile, reduce the sphere segment count to 16x8 when the debug overlay is active on mobile viewports.

Pre-compute at link addition time: when a new link is registered (node hello + peer MAC), compute the ellipsoid geometry and add it to the scene (hidden if the layer is off). Update on node position change. Remove when link becomes inactive (no frames for > 30s).

## Relationship to Explainability Overlay

The Fresnel zone debug overlay (this bead) and the detection explainability overlay (spaxel-ez4) both render Fresnel ellipsoids. They share the same geometry computation code (dashboard/js/fresnel.js — the ellipsoid helper function). The difference:
- This overlay: shows all active link ellipsoids simultaneously, toggle-able layer
- Explainability overlay: shows only contributing link ellipsoids for a specific selected blob, in explain mode

Both import from fresnel.js. The helper function FresnelEllipsoid(P1, P2, lambda) returns a Three.js Mesh ready for scene insertion.

## Files to Create or Modify

- dashboard/js/fresnel.js: FresnelEllipsoid helper function (shared with explainability)
- dashboard/js/layers.js: add "Fresnel Zones" toggle in the debug layer section
- dashboard/js/app.js: integrate Fresnel overlay management — create/update/remove ellipsoids on link events

## Tests

- Test ellipsoid geometry computation with known TX/RX positions: TX at (0,0,0), RX at (4,0,0), lambda=0.06m -> a ~= 2.015, b ~= 0.345. Verify to 3 decimal places.
- Test semi-minor axis for edge case: very short link d=0.1m -> b should be very small but positive
- Test for diagonal link: TX at (0,0,0), RX at (3,4,0) (distance=5m) -> verify a and b are computed correctly
- Test that toggling the layer on/off adds/removes the correct number of mesh objects from the scene (mock Three.js scene)
- Test hover tooltip shows correct data (link health from mock, link endpoints from mock)
- Test that ellipsoids update when node position changes

## Acceptance Criteria

- Fresnel zone ellipsoids render correctly for all active links when the debug layer is toggled on
- Ellipsoid semi-major and semi-minor axes match theoretical first Fresnel zone values for the link distance and frequency
- Toggle shows/hides all ellipsoids cleanly without leaving orphan objects in the scene
- Hovering an ellipsoid shows the correct tooltip with link details and health score
- Clicking an ellipsoid selects the corresponding link in the link panel
- Geometry computation is shared with the explainability overlay via fresnel.js
- Tests pass
close_reason: Verified Fresnel zone debug overlay is fully implemented. fresnel.js has shared FresnelEllipsoid helper. layers.js has FRESNEL toggle. app.js integrates rebuild/hover/click/tooltip. All 16 tests pass.

title: Bidirectional node protocol

title: End-to-end integration test harness (simulator + mothership assertions)
description:
## Overview
Build an automated integration test that starts the mothership, runs the CSI simulator against it, and asserts on observable behavior — providing a production-realism gate for CI.

## Test harness (tests/e2e/run.sh or Go test)

### Setup:
1. Start mothership container (or binary): docker run -d -p 8080:8080 ronaldraygun/spaxel:latest
2. Wait for /healthz to return {status:'ok'} with 15s timeout (poll every 500ms)
3. If PIN auth enabled: POST /api/auth/setup with test PIN; POST /api/auth/login

### Run simulator:
4. Start: sim --mothership http://localhost:8080 --nodes 4 --walkers 2 --duration 30s --rate 20 --ble --seed 42
5. Simulator exits non-zero if it receives {type:'reject'} message; test fails immediately

### Assert during run (poll every 1s for 30s):
6. GET /api/blobs (or WebSocket) → assert blob_count > 0 within first 15s
7. GET /api/nodes → assert nodes_online == 4 within first 5s
8. GET /healthz → assert status=='ok' throughout entire run

### Assert after run:
9. GET /api/events?type=detection → assert at least 1 detection event recorded
10. Simulator printed per-second frame counts to stdout; verify no frame-rate drop >20% from target

## CI integration
- GitHub Actions workflow: .github/workflows/e2e.yml (but only triggers from Argo Workflows via spaxel-ci)
- Build image → run test harness → post result as bead comment

## Acceptance
- Test passes on fresh container with seed 42 configuration
- Test fails clearly when mothership rejects frames (wrong protocol)
- Test runs in <90s total (15s startup + 30s sim + 45s buffer)
close_reason:
Implemented end-to-end integration test harness for Spaxel:

1. Argo WorkflowTemplate spaxel-e2e-workflowtemplate.yml added to declarative-config for CI/CD integration

2. Existing test infrastructure already in place:
   - Bash script harness: tests/e2e/run.sh
   - Go test harness: mothership/tests/e2e/e2e_test.go
   - CSI simulator: mothership/cmd/sim/main.go
   - GitHub Actions workflow: .github/workflows/e2e.yml

3. Test harness behavior:
   - Starts mothership container (or local binary)
   - Waits for /healthz to return ok (15s timeout, 500ms poll)
   - Handles PIN auth setup if enabled
   - Runs simulator with configurable nodes/walkers/duration/rate
   - Asserts during run: health ok, nodes online, blobs detected
   - Asserts after run: detection events, frame rate
   - Runs in under 90s total

4. CI integration via Argo Workflows spaxel-e2e template

title: Firmware: NVS schema migration on boot
description:
## Overview
Implement versioned NVS key migration on ESP32-S3 firmware so OTA-updated firmware gracefully handles NVS written by older versions.

## Implementation (firmware/main/nvs_migration.c)
- On boot, open 'spaxel' NVS namespace and read schema_ver (uint8); if missing, write schema_ver=1
- If schema_ver < COMPILED_NVS_VERSION: run migration functions in order (v1→v2, v2→v3, etc.)
- Each migration: add/rename/remove specific NVS keys; call nvs_commit() after each write
- After all migrations: update schema_ver = COMPILED_NVS_VERSION and commit
- Log each migration step to UART for debugging

## Example migration v1→v2:
- Rename 'ms_ip' to 'mothership_ip' (read old key, write new key, erase old key)
- Add 'ntp_server' key with default value 'pool.ntp.org'

## Acceptance
- Flash firmware v1.0 with known NVS schema; flash v1.1 firmware; verify all keys present
- Migration runs exactly once (schema_ver correctly incremented)
- Migration failure leaves NVS in consistent state (tested via simulated write failure)
close_reason: Implemented NVS schema migration on boot for ESP32-S3 firmware. Added nvs_migration.c/h with migration framework that reads schema_ver from NVS, initializes to 1 if missing, and runs migrations sequentially when schema_ver < COMPILED_NVS_VERSION. Each migration commits after each write for durability. Example v1→v2 migration renames 'ms_ip' to 'mothership_ip' and adds 'ntp_server' with default 'pool.ntp.org'. All migration steps logged to UART for debugging. Migration failure leaves NVS in consistent state.

title: Implement BLE Devices REST endpoints
description: Implement GET /api/ble/devices to list known devices. Add PUT /api/ble/devices/{mac} to set label and assign to person. Include OpenAPI-style godoc comments.

title: Wire HMAC token validation into ingestion WebSocket
description:
## Problem
mothership/internal/ingestion/server.go:128 declares a `tokenValidator func(mac, token string) bool` field on the Server struct, but it is never set from cmd/mothership/main.go:468 (no `ingestSrv.SetTokenValidator(...)` call) and never referenced in the hello-message handler (server.go:417-476). Any ESP32 that can reach the ingestion WebSocket and send a well-formed hello is accepted with no auth.

Meanwhile, provisioning/server.go already mints per-device HMAC-SHA256 tokens from the installation secret + MAC, and the firmware sends the token in the hello. The validation side is just unplugged.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] `ingestion.Server` exposes `SetTokenValidator(fn func(mac, token string) bool)`
- [ ] main.go wires a validator that re-derives HMAC-SHA256(installSecret, mac) and constant-time-compares against hello.Token
- [ ] Hello handler rejects nodes with missing or mismatched tokens via `sendReject` and closes the connection
- [ ] Unit test in server_test.go covering: valid token accepted, missing token rejected, wrong token rejected
- [ ] e2e test verifying an unprovisioned node cannot post CSI frames
- [ ] No regression: existing provisioning flow continues to work (test via Web Serial wizard or simulator)

## Notes
- Plan reference: docs/plan/plan.md lines 284-292, 429-434
- This is the core unfulfilled security promise from Phase 4. Without it, installation secret / PIN protect the dashboard but leave ingestion wide open to any node that reaches the port.
close_reason: Wired HMAC token validation into ingestion WebSocket. Added ingestSrv.SetTokenValidator(provSrv.ValidateToken) in main.go connecting the provisioning server HMAC-SHA256 derivation to the ingestion hello handler. Nodes with missing or invalid tokens are rejected. Added 5 unit/e2e tests. All tests pass.

title: Provisioning: carry ms_ip in payload for mDNS-less networks
description:
## Problem
First boot on a network where mDNS is blocked/filtered (common on enterprise WiFi, some mesh setups, VLANed networks) leaves the node stuck. The firmware discovery path at firmware/main/wifi.c:186-239 only uses `mdns_query_ptr(_spaxel._tcp, ...)` and only populates the `ms_ip` NVS cache *after* a successful query. The cache is a fallback for subsequent boots, not for the first one.

The provisioning payload (provisioning/server.go:29) includes `ms_mdns` (the service name to look up) but no direct-IP escape hatch. The Web Serial wizard's host-override field (onboard.js:695-696) only changes which mDNS name is queried, not how discovery happens. There is no code path that writes NVS_KEY_MS_IP during provisioning (firmware/main/provision.c:149).

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Provisioning JSON gains optional `ms_ip` field (string, IPv4 literal)
- [ ] firmware/main/provision.c writes ms_ip to NVS when present, alongside ms_mdns
- [ ] firmware/main/main.c prefers ms_ip from provisioning for first connect attempt, falls back to mDNS query, then to previously-cached ms_ip
- [ ] Web Serial wizard adds an optional "Mothership IP (advanced)" field shown when user expands "Network troubleshooting" on the Wi-Fi step
- [ ] Wizard auto-populates the field with the current browser's resolved IP for the mothership hostname when available
- [ ] Documentation update in docs/notes/ or plan.md explaining when to use the override

## Notes
- Do NOT make ms_ip required — mDNS-first remains the default so nodes survive mothership IP changes on DHCP networks
- Related discovery path: main.c:236-247 uses `strlen(g_state.ms_ip) > 0` as the cached fallback check
close_reason: Implemented ms_ip provisioning override for mDNS-less networks. Mothership adds optional ms_ip field to provisioning payload; firmware writes to both NVS ms_ip and ms_ip_prov keys; discovery logic prefers provisioned IP on first attempt then falls back to mDNS and cached IP; Web Serial wizard adds Mothership IP field in Network Troubleshooting section with auto-populate; docs/notes/mdns-override.md documents usage. Go tests and vet pass clean.

title: Backup: SQLite Online Backup API streaming endpoint
description:
## Overview
Implement GET /api/backup using SQLite's Online Backup API for consistent hot backups without downtime or temp files.

## Implementation (mothership/internal/ — new backup.go)

### Why Online Backup API:
- Simple file copy misses in-flight WAL pages and produces inconsistent backups
- sqlite3_backup_* copies page-by-page; readers/writers continue uninterrupted
- No temp file needed: stream directly to HTTP response

### Go implementation using go-sqlite3 (CGO) or modernc.org/sqlite:
func StreamBackup(w http.ResponseWriter, src *sql.DB):
  1. Open in-memory destination DB: sqlite3_open(':memory:', &pDest)
  2. Init backup: pBackup = sqlite3_backup_init(pDest, 'main', pSrc, 'main')
  3. Loop: sqlite3_backup_step(pBackup, 100) until SQLITE_DONE
  4. sqlite3_backup_finish(pBackup)
  5. Read all bytes from pDest and write to http.ResponseWriter

### Response format:
- Content-Type: application/zip
- Content-Disposition: attachment; filename='spaxel-backup-<timestamp>.zip'
- Zip contents:
  - spaxel.db (from backup)
  - floor_plan/ directory (if exists)
  - VERSION file

### Endpoint:
GET /api/backup — requires session auth; streams zip directly; no temp files written

## Acceptance
- Backup completes while mothership is actively processing CSI frames
- Downloaded .db file opens cleanly in sqlite3 CLI: PRAGMA integrity_check returns 'ok'
- Backup size reasonable (not 0 bytes, not gigabytes for fresh install)
- Simultaneous write during backup does not produce corrupt backup (verify with PRAGMA integrity_check)

title: Time-travel debugging and CSI replay
description:
## Background

The CSI recording buffer (Phase 2, spaxel-tqj) stores 48 hours of raw CSI frames on disk. Time-travel debugging lets you pause the live 3D view, scrub a timeline to any point in that 48-hour window, and replay the 3D scene exactly as it was at that moment. This is the most powerful debugging tool in spaxel: if a false alert fired at 3am, you can replay those 10 minutes and see exactly which links fired, what the blob positions were, and why the alert triggered. Parameter tuning without hardware becomes possible: change the motion threshold slider and immediately see how different the replay result would have been.

## ReplayEngine

New package: mothership/internal/replay/engine.go

ReplayEngine manages the replay lifecycle:
- state: LIVE, PAUSED, REPLAYING, SEEKING
- replay_position: current replay timestamp
- replay_speed: float64 (1.0 = real-time, 5.0 = 5x speed, 0.0 = paused)
- linked_session_id: the WebSocket session ID of the client requesting replay (each dashboard session has its own replay state)

ReplayEngine.Seek(t time.Time): reads the recording buffer to the specified timestamp. Uses the segment file structure from spaxel-tqj: finds the correct segment file for time t, scans forward to the exact frame at t. Target: seek time < 1 second.

ReplayEngine.Play(speed float64): starts reading frames from the buffer at the specified speed and feeding them through the signal processing pipeline.

## Replay Processing Pipeline

The replay pipeline is a copy of the live processing pipeline but with all outputs redirected to "replay" namespaced WebSocket messages:
- "replay_blob_update" instead of "blob_update"
- "replay_track_update" instead of "track_update"
- "replay_link_health" instead of "link_health"

The replay pipeline uses a separate instance of:
- SignalProcessor (with possibly modified parameters from the tuning sliders)
- FusionEngine
- TrackManager

These are cloned from the live instances at replay start so they inherit the current configuration, then modified by slider values.

The replay pipeline is self-contained: it does not affect the live pipeline in any way. Live detection continues while replay is active.

## Parameter Tuning During Replay

While in replay mode, the dashboard shows a "Tuning" panel with sliders for key signal processing parameters:
- Motion threshold: deltaRMS threshold for motion detection (default from config, range 0.001 to 0.1)
- Baseline tau: EMA time constant in seconds (default 30s, range 5s to 300s)
- Fresnel weight sigma: Gaussian sigma for Fresnel zone contribution (default 0.1m, range 0.01m to 0.5m)
- Minimum confidence for detection: composite minimum confidence before blob is reported (default 0.3)

Changing any slider: the replay engine discards the current replay pipeline state and re-processes from the current replay_position with the new parameters. This takes at most 1-2 seconds for a typical segment (the CSI frames are already on disk; it's fast CPU processing).

"Apply to Live" button: copies the currently-active replay parameters to the live configuration and persists them to the mothership config file. The live pipeline picks up the new values within one processing cycle. Requires confirmation modal: "This will change the live detection configuration. Continue?"

## Dashboard Controls

Entering replay mode: clicking the "Pause" button (or pressing Space) on the live dashboard:
1. Pauses the live 3D view (3D scene stops updating)
2. Shows the timeline scrubber: a horizontal bar spanning the 48-hour recording window
3. Event markers appear on the scrubber at the timestamps of activity timeline events (zone transitions, alerts, etc.)
4. "Live" chip in the dashboard header changes to "Replay" chip

Timeline scrubber:
- Click to seek to any position in the 48-hour window
- Drag for continuous scrubbing
- Event markers: small coloured ticks on the scrubber. Clicking a marker seeks to that event and jumps the activity timeline selection to that event row.
- The current replay position is shown as a draggable thumb with a timestamp tooltip ("2026-03-27 03:14:22")

Playback controls:
- Play/Pause button (Space key shortcut)
- Speed selector: 1x, 5x, 10x
- Step-forward button: advances replay by 1 second
- "Back to Live" button: exits replay mode and resumes live updates

The 3D scene in replay mode: shows a "REPLAY" watermark badge in the top-left corner (so it's clear the view is not live). All live blob and track updates are suppressed while in replay mode (only replay_ prefixed messages update the scene).

## Seek Performance

The recording buffer (spaxel-tqj) uses 1-hour segment files. To seek to timestamp T:
1. Identify the correct segment file: {linkID}-{year}-{month}-{day}-{hour}.csi
2. Binary search within the file: CSI frames are variable-length but each has a 24-byte header with timestamp_us. Scan forward from start of file to the frame nearest T. O(n) but files are ≤ 1 hour = at most 180,000 frames at 50 Hz. At 64-byte average header read, this is < 10MB scan and typically completes in < 200ms.
3. Buffer a few seconds of frames ahead of T for smooth playback start.

For all active links: seek all link segment files in parallel (goroutines). Total seek time < 1s.

## Files to Create or Modify

- mothership/internal/replay/engine.go: ReplayEngine, state machine, seek, play, parameter injection
- mothership/internal/replay/pipeline.go: replay signal processing pipeline (cloned from live)
- mothership/internal/recording/ (spaxel-tqj): add SeekToTimestamp(t time.Time) method
- mothership/internal/dashboard/hub.go: replay_ namespaced WebSocket message routing
- dashboard/js/replay.js: timeline scrubber UI, playback controls, tuning panel
- mothership/internal/dashboard/routes.go: WebSocket commands for replay control (type: "replay_seek", "replay_play", "replay_pause", "replay_set_params")

## Tests

- Test seek: create a mock recording buffer with known frames at known timestamps. Seek to an arbitrary timestamp, verify the returned frame is the closest one to the target.
- Test that replay pipeline processes frames identically to live pipeline for the same input (regression test with saved CSI data and known expected output blobs)
- Test parameter slider: change motion_threshold via replay command, verify the replay pipeline uses the new threshold on subsequent frames
- Test "Apply to Live" correctly writes parameter changes to the live config
- Test that live pipeline output is unaffected while replay is active (isolation test)
- Test seek performance: 1-hour segment file with 180,000 frames, seek to timestamp in the middle, complete in < 500ms

## Acceptance Criteria

- Seek to any point in 48-hour window completes in < 1 second for all active links
- Replay produces identical blob positions to original live processing for the same CSI input
- Parameter sliders re-process the current replay position in < 3 seconds
- "Apply to Live" copies parameters correctly and live detection immediately uses new values
- Timeline scrubber event markers correctly align with activity timeline events
- "Back to Live" correctly resumes live detection without any stale state
- Tests pass

title: Self-improving localisation with BLE ground truth
description:
## Background

The Fresnel zone fusion engine (spaxel-m9a) computes localisation by weighting each link's deltaRMS contribution according to the geometric intersection of candidate voxels with the Fresnel zone ellipsoid. These weights are currently uniform and based purely on geometry. In practice, some links are better at detecting motion in specific parts of the room than others — due to reflection geometry, multipath, furniture layout, and antenna orientation. By using BLE RSSI positions as continuous ground truth (when a person's labelled phone or wearable is visible), we can refine the per-link, per-zone weights to match observed physical reality.

## Self-Improving Mechanism

New package: mothership/internal/learning/weights.go

WeightLearner runs as a background goroutine. It operates on ground truth samples collected during normal operation.

A ground truth sample is collected when BOTH:
1. A confident BLE triangulated position is available for a known person (confidence > 0.7 from identity matching bead spaxel-nqh)
2. A CSI blob position is within 0.5m of the BLE position (confirming the blob corresponds to that person)

Sample structure: {timestamp, person_id, ble_position Vec3, blob_position Vec3, per_link_delta_rms map[linkID]float64, per_link_health map[linkID]float64}

These samples are stored in SQLite: ground_truth_samples (id, timestamp, person_id, position_xyz, per_link_deltas_json, per_link_health_json). The table is capped at 10,000 samples per person (oldest first out) to prevent unbounded growth.

## Online Weight Learning

After accumulating 100+ samples for a given spatial zone (the room is divided into zones of 0.5m x 0.5m grid cells for this purpose), run incremental linear regression:

Prediction model: position_estimate = sum_i (w_i * delta_rms_i) / sum_i w_i, where w_i are the learnable per-link weights.

The objective is to minimise the mean squared error between the position estimate from the weighted fusion and the ground truth BLE positions, over all samples in the zone.

Update rule (stochastic gradient descent, online):
For each new ground truth sample:
- Compute current position estimate using current weights
- Compute error = ground_truth_position - estimated_position
- For each link i: w_i += learning_rate * error * delta_rms_i / |delta_rms_vector|
- learning_rate = 0.001 (small to prevent overfitting to transient environmental changes)
- Apply L2 regularisation: w_i *= (1 - regularisation * learning_rate) where regularisation = 0.01

Clip weights to [0, 5] to prevent divergence. Normalise weight vector to unit sum after each update.

## Validation Gate

To prevent the learned weights from degrading accuracy (overfitting, transient environmental changes, sensor noise):

Hold out 20% of samples as a validation set (random selection). After each batch of 50 weight updates, compute the mean position error on the validation set using the updated weights vs. the original (geometric) weights.

Only persist the updated weights if: validation_error_new < validation_error_original * 0.95 (at least 5% improvement on the validation set).

If the validation check fails, discard the weight update and log: "Weight update rejected: no improvement on validation set. Keeping current weights."

This is a conservative gate. The threshold is configurable (fleet.weight_improvement_threshold, default 0.05).

## Weight Storage

SQLite table: link_weights (link_id TEXT, zone_grid_x INT, zone_grid_y INT, weight REAL, sample_count INT, last_updated DATETIME, validation_improvement REAL, PRIMARY KEY (link_id, zone_grid_x, zone_grid_y)).

Zone grid: floor is divided into 0.5m cells. zone_grid_x = floor(x / 0.5), zone_grid_y = floor(y / 0.5). This allows position-dependent weights — a link might be excellent for localisation in one area and poor in another.

On FusionEngine update: instead of using geometric Fresnel zone weights alone, multiply by the learned spatial weight for the voxel being evaluated (bilinear interpolation between grid cells for smooth transitions).

Fallback: if no learned weight exists for a grid cell (insufficient samples), use the geometric weight (learned weight = 1.0). This ensures correctness during the learning period.

## Accuracy Trend in Dashboard

The accuracy improvement from learning should be visible to users. In the "Accuracy" dashboard panel (Phase 7 feedback loop bead):

Add "Position accuracy" subsection:
- Median position error (m): computed weekly from ground truth samples. median(|ble_position - blob_position|) over all weekly samples.
- Week-over-week trend: sparkline of weekly median position error. Arrow indicating direction (improving/degrading).
- Sample count: "Based on N position measurements from M people this week"
- "Accuracy improving" badge when position error has decreased by > 10% vs previous week.

## Files to Create or Modify

- mothership/internal/learning/weights.go: WeightLearner, SGD update, validation gate
- mothership/internal/learning/samples.go: ground truth sample collection, SQLite storage
- mothership/internal/fusion/engine.go (spaxel-m9a): integrate learned weights in FusionEngine
- mothership/internal/dashboard/routes.go: GET /api/accuracy/weights (debug endpoint showing current weight map)
- dashboard/js/accuracy.js: position accuracy trend chart

## Tests

- Test ground truth sample collection gates correctly: confidence > 0.7 AND BLE-blob distance < 0.5m -> sample collected; confidence = 0.6 -> no sample
- Test SGD weight update: after 100 samples with known ground truth, verify weights move in the direction that reduces error
- Test validation gate: inject a batch of adversarial samples that would degrade accuracy, verify gate rejects the update
- Test bilinear interpolation between adjacent grid cells produces smooth weight values
- Test weight fallback: FusionEngine correctly uses geometric weight=1.0 when no learned weight exists for a grid cell
- Test SQLite cap: inserting 10,001 samples removes the oldest one, maintaining the 10,000 cap

## Acceptance Criteria

- Position error decreases measurably over 2+ weeks of operation with BLE ground truth data (target: from initial ~1.2m to < 0.8m median error)
- Validation gate prevents weight regressions (mock adversarial samples do not degrade fusion accuracy)
- Weight updates persist across mothership restarts
- Position accuracy trend visible in dashboard Accuracy panel
- Sample collection rate visible (samples per day per person) in dashboard
- Tests pass
close_reason: Implemented self-improving localization with BLE ground truth. Created spatial weight learner with SGD, validation gate, bilinear interpolation. Added position accuracy visualization to dashboard. All tests implemented.

title: Add axe-core accessibility CI gate for dashboard
description:
## Goal

Every dashboard page passes axe-core with zero WCAG AA violations. CI fails the build if a violation is introduced.

## Scope

1. Add `@axe-core/playwright` to `dashboard/package.json` devDependencies.
2. Add a test file `dashboard/tests/a11y.spec.js` that loads each page (`index`, `live`, `fleet`, `setup`, `integrations`) via the local dev server and asserts `AxeBuilder.analyze()` returns no violations at the `wcag2aa` tag.
3. Wire into the existing Argo workflow (`spaxel-build`) — add a step that runs `npm run test:a11y` before the container build.
4. Document the local run command in `dashboard/README.md`.

## Verification

- `npm run test:a11y` passes locally after the design system bead (spaxel-0n8r) lands.
- CI fails deliberately when a contrast violation is introduced, and passes when it is corrected.

Traceability: plan.md §8e "Accessibility", §8f item 9.
close_reason: Axe-core accessibility CI gate already implemented and pushed (commit 6951ff1). All 5 dashboard pages pass wcag2a+wcag2aa with zero violations. Test suite uses @axe-core/playwright with Playwright auto-starting http-server. CI docs in dashboard/README.md.

title: Sleep quality monitoring
description:
## Background

The breathing analysis feature (Phase 5, spaxel-r37) detects the micro-motion of breathing in stationary people. Run continuously in bedroom zones overnight, it can compute sleep quality metrics without any wearable device. Chest displacement during breathing at 15 breaths/minute produces a detectable 0.25 Hz signal in CSI. By tracking this overnight, combined with motion events (wake episodes) and the timing of presence in the bedroom zone, we can produce a sleep summary that rivals basic commercial sleep trackers — without the user wearing anything.

## Sleep Session Detection

SleepMonitor in mothership/internal/sleep/monitor.go.

Session onset detection (all conditions must hold):
1. Person is in a bedroom zone (zone with is_bedroom flag = true, set in zone editor)
2. Stationary detection fires (STATIONARY_DETECTED state from breathing analysis bead)
3. BLE device shows reduced activity (optional enhancement: phone advertising rate drops when screen is off; this is a bonus signal, not required)
Tentative onset: all conditions met. Confirmed onset: conditions hold for 15 consecutive minutes.

Session end detection:
1. Person leaves bedroom zone (zone transition event fires)
2. OR: motion detection fires for > 2 minutes (sustained motion = getting up)
3. OR: stationary detection drops and does not return for > 30 minutes (person left room without portal crossing — reconciliation path)

Session record stored in SQLite:
CREATE TABLE sleep_sessions (
  id TEXT PRIMARY KEY,
  person_id TEXT NOT NULL,
  zone_id TEXT NOT NULL,  -- bedroom zone
  session_date DATE NOT NULL,  -- the date this sleep night belongs to (typically today-1 for morning reports)
  sleep_onset DATETIME,  -- time tentative detection was confirmed
  wake_time DATETIME,
  time_in_bed_minutes REAL,
  sleep_latency_minutes REAL,  -- time from entering bedroom to sleep onset
  wake_episode_count INTEGER DEFAULT 0,
  wake_after_sleep_onset_minutes REAL,  -- total time awake after first sleep onset
  breathing_rate_mean REAL,
  breathing_rate_stddev REAL,
  breathing_anomaly_count INTEGER DEFAULT 0,  -- breathing < 8 or > 25 per minute
  sleep_efficiency REAL  -- (time_in_bed - waso) / time_in_bed * 100
);

CREATE TABLE sleep_wake_episodes (
  id TEXT PRIMARY KEY,
  session_id TEXT,
  episode_start DATETIME,
  episode_end DATETIME,
  duration_seconds REAL
);

## Sleep Metrics Computation

During the sleep session, SleepMonitor subscribes to:
- Breathing data: periodic sample of breathing_freq_hz from BreathingDetector (spaxel-r37). Store in a rolling buffer.
- Motion events: MOTION_DETECTED state transitions from LinkProcessor. Each motion event during a confirmed sleep session is a potential wake episode.

Wake episode classification:
- If deltaRMS > threshold for > 3 seconds: wake episode starts
- If deltaRMS returns below threshold and breathing signal resumes: wake episode ends
- Store episode start/end in sleep_wake_episodes

Breathing analysis during sleep:
- Mean breathing rate (bpm): mean(breathing_freq_hz * 60) over all samples in session
- Breathing rate standard deviation: indicates sleep stage variability (higher variance may indicate REM activity)
- Breathing anomaly: if breathing_freq_hz * 60 < 8 or > 25 for > 3 consecutive minutes: log anomaly. This is a proxy for potential sleep apnoea or hyperventilation.

Sleep efficiency: (time_in_bed_minutes - wake_after_sleep_onset_minutes) / time_in_bed_minutes * 100. A value above 85% is considered good sleep efficiency.

## Morning Summary Card

On first WebSocket connection from the dashboard after 6am AND after a sleep session has ended (wake_time is set):
- Mothership pushes a "morning_summary" WebSocket message with the completed session data
- Dashboard renders a dismissible card in simple mode (full width at top) and as a floating panel in expert mode

Card content:
- "Last night: [sleep_duration] h [mm] min"
- Colored efficiency indicator: green (>85%), amber (70-85%), red (<70%)
- Wake episodes: "2 wake episodes, [total waso] min awake after sleep onset"
- Breathing: "Average breathing: [N] breaths/min"
- Anomaly note (if applicable): "Unusual breathing detected at [time]. [View details]"
- "View full sleep report" link (opens detailed timeline view in expert mode)

## Weekly Trends

Dashboard "Sleep" panel:
- 7-day sparkline of sleep duration per night
- 7-day sparkline of sleep efficiency per night
- Average breathing rate over the week
- Week-over-week comparison: "This week you slept 6h 48m on average (vs. 7h 12m last week)"

## Per-Person Tracking

Sleep monitoring is person-specific and requires BLE identity (so the system knows whose bedroom this is). Multiple people sharing a bedroom: each person has their own sleep session if their BLE devices can be distinguished. If both people are in bed simultaneously, the breathing detector may pick up a blend of two breathing rates — acknowledge this limitation in documentation.

For anonymous tracks (no BLE identity): detect in-bedroom stationary presence only (no per-person sleep report). Log "Unidentified person in bedroom zone" for 8+ hour periods.

## Zone Configuration

The zone editor (portals bead, spaxel-qlh) is extended with a zone type selector:
- Normal zone (default)
- Bedroom (enables sleep monitoring)
- Kitchen (no special behavior)
- Children's zone (suppresses fall detection)

This is stored as zone_type in the zones table.

## Files to Create or Modify

- mothership/internal/sleep/monitor.go: SleepMonitor, session detection, metric computation
- mothership/internal/sleep/report.go: morning summary generation, weekly trend aggregation
- mothership/internal/signal/breathing.go (spaxel-r37): add tick-based sample reporting for sleep monitor
- dashboard/js/sleep.js: morning summary card, Sleep panel
- mothership/internal/events/events.go: SleepSessionStartEvent, SleepSessionEndEvent

## Tests

- Test sleep session onset: stationary detection fires, person in bedroom, 15 minutes -> session confirmed
- Test that stationary detection < 15 minutes does not create a session (avoids brief naps misclassified)
- Test wake episode counting: 3 MOTION_DETECTED events > 3s each during a session -> wake_episode_count = 3
- Test wake after sleep onset calculation: 3 episodes of 5 minutes each -> waso = 15 minutes
- Test sleep efficiency calculation: 480 minutes in bed, 45 minutes waso -> efficiency = 90.6%
- Test breathing anomaly detection: inject 4 minutes of breathing_freq_hz = 0.1 (6 bpm) -> anomaly logged
- Test morning summary trigger fires only on first connection after 6am AND after session end

## Acceptance Criteria

- Sleep session detected within 15 minutes of confirmed onset (stationary in bedroom zone)
- Wake episodes counted correctly (tested with synthetic motion event injection)
- Morning summary card appears on first dashboard open after wake time (6am by default, configurable)
- Weekly trends sparkline shows 7 nights of data after 7 days
- Sleep session data persists in SQLite across mothership restarts
- Breathing anomaly flag fires correctly for rate < 8 or > 25 bpm
- Tests pass

title: Implement NTP client in ESP32 firmware
description:
Add NTP synchronization to firmware/main/wifi.c or ntp.c:
- Call esp_sntp_setservername(0, ntp_server) before esp_sntp_init() on boot
- ntp_server read from NVS 'ntp_server' key (default: 'pool.ntp.org')
- Attempt sync for up to 10 seconds after WiFi connect; log WARN if sync fails
- On sync failure: proceed without stagger (rely on CSMA/CA)
- Resync every 10 minutes via esp_timer periodic callback
- Include ntp_synced status in health JSON message

Acceptance: Node health messages show ntp_synced: true when pool is reachable; ntp_synced: false when NTP blocked — node still operates normally; resync occurs every ~600s (verified via UART logs)

title: Room transition portals and zone occupancy
description:
## Background

Knowing a blob is at coordinates (3.2m, 1.8m, 1.0m) is useful to the algorithm, but "Alice is in the Kitchen" is useful to a person. Room transition portals define doorway planes between named zones. When a track's trajectory intersects a portal plane, the zone occupancy counts update and a transition event fires. This is the foundation for natural language presence display ("Alice is in the Kitchen"), automation triggers ("when Alice enters the bedroom"), and the activity timeline ("Alice moved from Living Room to Kitchen at 14:23").

## Zone Definitions

Zones are named 3D volumes represented as axis-aligned bounding boxes (AABB) for simplicity. Each zone has: id (uuid), name ("Kitchen"), bounds_min (Vec3), bounds_max (Vec3), color (hex string for 3D overlay), created_at.

SQLite schema:
CREATE TABLE zones (
  id TEXT PRIMARY KEY,
  name TEXT NOT NULL,
  bounds_min_x REAL, bounds_min_y REAL, bounds_min_z REAL,
  bounds_max_x REAL, bounds_max_y REAL, bounds_max_z REAL,
  color TEXT DEFAULT '#3b82f6',
  created_at DATETIME DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP
);

Containment test: a position P is in zone Z if bounds_min_x <= P.x <= bounds_max_x AND bounds_min_y <= P.y <= bounds_max_y. The Z bounds are typically 0 to ceiling height (usually 2.5m) since we track floor-plane position.

## Portal Definitions

A portal is a vertical plane segment spanning a doorway. It divides two zones and detects crossings.

Portal schema:
CREATE TABLE portals (
  id TEXT PRIMARY KEY,
  name TEXT,  -- e.g. "Kitchen Door"
  zone_a_id TEXT,   -- zone on one side
  zone_b_id TEXT,   -- zone on other side
  plane_point Vec3, -- a point on the portal plane (e.g. centre of doorway)
  plane_normal Vec3, -- unit normal vector of the portal plane
  width REAL,       -- width of the doorway in metres
  height REAL,      -- height of the doorway (default: 2.1m)
  created_at DATETIME
);

A portal normal points from zone_a toward zone_b. A crossing from zone_a to zone_b has dot(velocity, normal) > 0. A crossing from zone_b to zone_a has dot(velocity, normal) < 0.

## Portal Editor (3D Dashboard)

Extend the node placement UI (spaxel-qq6) with portal editing:
1. User clicks "Add Portal" button
2. A vertical plane appears in the 3D scene at the camera's focal point
3. User drags the plane using TransformControls (from Three.js addons) to position it across a doorway
4. User adjusts width and assigns zone names on each side (dropdown of existing zones or "Create new zone")
5. User clicks "Save" — portal is stored in SQLite and rendered as a semi-transparent divider plane in the 3D scene

Portal rendering: thin coloured plane (opacity 0.3, colour #a855f7 purple) with a label at the top edge showing the portal name. When a track crosses the portal, the plane briefly flashes brighter (animated opacity increase then decay back to 0.3).

Zone rendering: semi-transparent coloured cuboid volumes (opacity 0.1, colour from zone.color). Zone name displayed as a floating text label at the zone centroid (using THREE.Sprite). A "Zones" layer toggle in the 3D view hides/shows all zones simultaneously.

## Crossing Detection

CrossingDetector runs as part of the TrackManager update loop (10 Hz). For each track update:

1. For each active portal, test if the track crossed the portal plane in the last update step:
   - Previous position P_prev, current position P_curr
   - Check if the line segment P_prev -> P_curr intersects the portal plane within the portal's rectangular bounds (width x height centered on plane_point)
   - Intersection test: t = dot(plane_point - P_prev, normal) / dot(P_curr - P_prev, normal). If 0 <= t <= 1, compute intersection point P_int = P_prev + t*(P_curr - P_prev), then check if P_int is within the doorway rectangle.
   - Crossing direction: if dot(P_curr - P_prev, normal) > 0, direction is A_to_B; otherwise B_to_A.

2. On crossing detected: update occupancy counts, emit ZoneCrossingEvent.

ZoneCrossingEvent: {portal_id, track_id, person_id, person_label, from_zone_id, from_zone_name, to_zone_id, to_zone_name, direction, timestamp}.

This event is:
- Published to the internal event bus
- Broadcast via WebSocket to dashboard as type "zone_transition"
- Appended to activity timeline (Phase 8)
- Processed by automation engine (Phase 6)

## Occupancy Counter

OccupancyManager maintains a per-zone current occupant list (map[zoneID][]TrackID).

Updates from two sources:
1. CrossingDetector portal events: when a track crosses from zone A to B, move its entry in the occupancy map from A to B.
2. Direct containment check: run every 30 seconds as a reconciliation pass. For each active track, check if it is within any zone's bounding box. If the track is in zone C but the occupancy map says it is in zone A (e.g. track was created inside a zone without crossing a portal), update accordingly.
The containment check prevents "teleportation" inconsistencies when tracks are created or resume from coasting state.

## WebSocket Broadcast

On each zone occupancy change, the mothership broadcasts:
{"type":"zone_occupancy","zones":[{"id":"zone-kitchen","name":"Kitchen","occupants":[{"track_id":"track-1","person_id":"uuid-alice","person_label":"Alice"}]},{"id":"zone-living","name":"Living Room","occupants":[]}]}

And specifically on crossings:
{"type":"zone_transition","portal_id":"...","person_label":"Alice","from_zone":"Kitchen","to_zone":"Living Room","timestamp":"2026-03-27T14:23:00Z"}

## REST API

GET /api/zones: list all zones with current occupancy
POST /api/zones: create zone
PUT /api/zones/{id}: update zone bounds/name/color
DELETE /api/zones/{id}: delete zone (removes from all occupancy tracking)

GET /api/portals: list all portals
POST /api/portals: create portal
PUT /api/portals/{id}: update portal
DELETE /api/portals/{id}: delete portal

GET /api/zones/{id}/history?since=2026-03-27T00:00:00Z: get crossing history for zone (list of ZoneCrossingEvent)

## Tests

- Test portal crossing detection with a track path that passes through the portal plane: verify crossing event fires with correct direction
- Test that a track path that runs parallel to a portal plane but within 0.1m does not fire a false crossing
- Test that a track path outside the portal's width bounds does not fire a crossing
- Test occupancy count updates: zone Kitchen starts with 1 occupant, track crosses portal to Living Room, Kitchen count = 0, Living Room count = 1
- Test the 30-second reconciliation pass: track that appears inside a zone without crossing a portal is correctly assigned to that zone
- Test zone containment with a position exactly on the bounds_min edge (inclusive boundary)
- Test that zone_transition WebSocket message is broadcast with correct from_zone and to_zone names

## Acceptance Criteria

- Portal editor allows placing vertical plane portals across doorways in the 3D scene
- Zone bounding boxes are editable and render as semi-transparent volumes in 3D view
- Zone labels update in real-time as people move between zones ("Kitchen: Alice, Bob")
- Zone transition events fire within one track update cycle (100ms) of the crossing occurring
- Reconciliation pass correctly handles tracks that appear inside zones without portal crossings
- Zone and portal data persists across mothership restarts via SQLite
- WebSocket broadcasts zone_occupancy after every occupancy change
- Tests pass

title: Webhook action firing & fault tolerance for automations
description:
## Overview
Reliable webhook delivery for automation trigger actions with error handling and dashboard feedback.

## Backend (mothership/automation/)
- HTTP client: POST to configured URL with 5s timeout; fire-and-forget (no retry)
- Payload schema: {trigger_id, trigger_name, condition, blob_id, person, position:{x,y,z}, zone, dwell_s, timestamp_ms}
- Error handling:
  - 4xx response: disable trigger + set trigger.error_message; push WS alert to dashboard
  - 5xx / timeout: log warning + increment trigger.error_count; do NOT disable
  - error_count resets on first 2xx response
- Test endpoint: POST /api/triggers/{id}/test — fires webhook once with synthetic payload, returns {status, response_ms, error}
- Audit log: webhook_log table (trigger_id, fired_at_ms, url, status_code, latency_ms, error)

## Dashboard
- Error badge on trigger card when disabled due to 4xx
- 'Test Webhook' button in trigger edit panel — shows response in real time
- Last N firings visible in trigger detail view (from webhook_log)
- 'Re-enable' button to clear error state and retry

## Acceptance
- 5xx failures do not disable triggers
- 4xx disables trigger and shows dashboard warning within 2s
- Test endpoint returns response within timeout + 500ms overhead
- Requires: spaxel-6ha (REST API), spaxel-vuw (trigger volumes), spaxel-9eg (WS alerts)

title: Implement firmware LED blink handler
description:
Parse downstream JSON message {type:'identify', duration_ms: 5000} and implement LED blink handler that toggles GPIO LED pin at 100ms on/100ms off for duration_ms. LED GPIO is board-specific (default GPIO8 for ESP32-S3-DevKitC; configurable via sdkconfig). Run blink in a FreeRTOS task so it doesn't block WebSocket processing. Cancel any running blink when new identify message received or on disconnect.

**Acceptance:**
- LED blinks at ~5 Hz for the specified duration when identify message received
- Blink stops automatically when duration_ms expires

title: Add sleep quality monitoring
description:
Implement overnight sleep analysis and reporting.

Deliverables:

- Motion scoring integration
- Morning summary generation

Acceptance: System generates daily sleep quality reports with breathing and motion metrics.
close_reason: Implemented overnight sleep quality monitoring with breathing analysis, motion scoring, and morning summary generation. System generates daily sleep reports with quality scores (0-100) based on breathing (40%), motion (30%), and continuity (30%) metrics. Added REST API at /api/sleep/* for status and reports.

title: Node placement UI and coverage painting
description:
Interactive 3D node placement with real-time GDOP coverage overlay.

## Deliverables

Frontend: TransformControls for node dragging, room dimension editor, GDOP overlay (128x128 DataTexture with HDOP computation), real-time updates during drag, virtual node support, click-to-select via raycasting, GDOP legend. Committed as 7b32570.

title: Guided troubleshooting and first-time UX
description:
## Background

The onboarding wizard (spaxel-zvo) handles the happy path from unboxed ESP32 to streaming CSI. This bead handles what happens when things go wrong — during onboarding and during normal operation — and provides first-time feature discovery for users after successful setup. The design principle is that a non-technical household member should never need to read documentation, open a terminal, or contact support for any common problem. Every failure mode has a recovery path visible in the UI.

## Scope

This Phase 4 bead covers foundational troubleshooting infrastructure. A Phase 9 bead (Guided troubleshooting enhanced for production) extends this with proactive quality prompts and production-polish improvements.

## 1. Onboarding Failure Guidance

At each step of the onboarding wizard, intercept known failure modes and render human-readable recovery steps instead of technical errors. Each wizard step has an error state with specific guidance:

Browser check errors:
- navigator.serial unavailable -> "Please use Google Chrome or Microsoft Edge. Firefox and Safari do not support USB device communication."

Device connection errors:
- No device selected -> "Did you hold the BOOT button while plugging in? Try again: hold BOOT, then plug in the USB cable."
- Permission denied -> "Browser blocked USB access. Check your browser's site permissions for this address."
- Access denied (device in use) -> "Another application is using this USB port. Close Arduino IDE, esptool, or any other serial monitor and try again."

WiFi provisioning errors:
- Node not appearing after 120s -> Stepped guidance: (1) Check SSID/password are exactly correct. (2) ESP32-S3 only supports 2.4GHz — check your router's 2.4GHz band is enabled. (3) Check if your router has AP isolation / client isolation enabled (blocks device-to-device communication). (4) Try moving the node closer to the router.
- Node appears but no CSI data -> "The node connected but is not sensing yet. Check the antenna orientation — the PCB antenna should face away from walls."

## 2. Node Offline Troubleshooting

When a node goes offline (detected via heartbeat timeout in mothership), the dashboard shows a troubleshooting timeline card:

"Node [Living Room] went offline at [time]."
Timeline of suggested actions:
1. Check the node's power LED is on (solid green = powered and connected, blinking = attempting WiFi)
2. If blinking: move the node closer to your WiFi router temporarily
3. If the LED is blinking rapidly after 5 minutes: the node has lost its WiFi configuration. Connect to 'spaxel-{last4mac}' WiFi network to reconfigure. [Link to captive portal guide]
4. If the LED is off: check the power supply and USB cable
5. Still stuck? [Reset to factory defaults] (button that sends a "reboot" downstream command if node reconnects, or shows instructions for manual factory reset via BOOT button hold)

The card remains visible until the node reconnects. It includes last-known position on the floor plan (greyed out) and how long it has been offline.

## 3. First-Time Feature Tooltips

On the first dashboard open after a node is successfully added (tracked by a localStorage key "spaxel_tooltips_shown"), show contextual tooltips pointing to key features:
- Point at the CSI amplitude chart: "This is your live signal. Motion causes the waves to change."
- Point at the 3D view (if available): "This 3D space updates as people move around."
- Point at the presence indicator: "Green = no one detected. Red = motion detected."
- Point at the link list: "Each line between two nodes is a sensing link."

Tooltips: auto-dismiss after 8 seconds, dismiss-all button, never re-appear (localStorage flag per tooltip ID). Rendered as floating HTML divs with an arrow pointing to the target element, positioned via getBoundingClientRect().

## 4. Post-Calibration Reinforcement

After the guided calibration walk in the onboarding wizard completes (blob detected successfully), show a "You're all set" card:
- Summary: "[Node A] calibrated. 1 sensing link active. Motion detection: Ready."
- What to expect: "You'll see the CSI waveform react when someone walks through the room. The system learns your space over the next few hours and becomes more accurate."
- Next step prompt: "Want to add another node for more precise location tracking? [Add another node] [I'm done for now]"

## 5. Detection Quality Prompts (Phase 4 basic version)

When a link's packet rate drops below 50% of the configured rate for more than 60 seconds (basic quality issue detectable without Phase 5 full confidence scoring):
- Show a non-blocking banner: "Node A is having trouble communicating. Check that it is powered on and within WiFi range."
- Auto-dismiss when packet rate recovers.

## Implementation

Troubleshooting logic lives in dashboard/js/troubleshoot.js:
- TroubleshootManager class subscribes to WebSocket events: node_offline, node_online, low_packet_rate, calibration_complete
- For each event type, renders the appropriate UI component
- Uses a simple state machine per issue: DETECTED -> NOTIFIED -> RESOLVED/DISMISSED
- Issue state persisted in memory (cleared on page refresh — issues re-fire on next event)

Tooltip system: dashboard/js/tooltips.js
- TooltipManager class: show(tooltipId, targetSelector, text, direction)
- Checks localStorage "spaxel_tooltip_{id}_shown" before showing
- Sets localStorage flag on dismiss
- All tooltips in a manifest array, shown in sequence on first visit

A dedicated troubleshoot.css for the offline card and tooltip styles.

## Design Principles

- Guidance must be actionable, not diagnostic: "Move the node closer to your router" not "WiFi RSSI is -78 dBm"
- Never condescending: "Your node went offline" not "Error: WebSocket connection closed with code 1006"
- Never blocks normal operation: every troubleshooting element is dismissible
- Avoid information overload: show one most-likely cause first, with "More options" expander for alternatives
- Use progressive disclosure: simple guidance first, technical details behind "Advanced" toggle

## Tests

- Test that node_offline WebSocket event triggers the troubleshooting panel render with correct node label
- Test that tooltip TooltipManager correctly checks localStorage before showing each tooltip
- Test that tooltips set the localStorage flag on dismiss and do not re-appear on subsequent show() calls
- Test that node_online event after node_offline dismisses the offline card
- Test that low_packet_rate event below 50% threshold triggers the quality banner
- Test that the post-calibration card renders with correct link count and node label

## Acceptance Criteria

- All common onboarding failure modes have human-readable recovery paths in the wizard
- Node offline card appears in the dashboard within 30 seconds of disconnection
- Offline card includes actionable steps and the captive portal AP SSID
- First-time tooltips appear exactly once on first dashboard open after node addition
- Tooltips never re-appear after dismissal (localStorage persistence)
- Post-calibration card shows correct summary after wizard completion
- Detection quality banner fires when packet rate drops below 50% threshold
- All UI elements are dismissible without blocking normal dashboard use
- Tests pass
close_reason:
Implemented guided troubleshooting and first-time UX:

1. Onboarding failure guidance - human-readable recovery paths at each wizard step
2. Node offline troubleshooting - timeline cards with progressive disclosure, factory reset modal, captive portal AP SSID
3. First-time feature tooltips - 4 contextual tooltips with localStorage persistence, auto-dismiss, sequential tour
4. Post-calibration reinforcement card with summary and next steps
5. Detection quality prompts - client-side link health check with auto-recovery
6. 30 new tests, all 96 tests pass

title: Stationary person detection via breathing analysis
description:
## Background

Standard deltaRMS motion detection fires when someone physically moves. But a stationary person — reading, sleeping, watching TV — produces micro-motion from breathing: chest displacement of approximately 5mm at 0.1-0.5 Hz. This is well below the standard motion threshold but is detectable with careful bandpass filtering of the CSI signal. This is one of the hardest and most valuable features in the system. It transforms spaxel from a "motion detector" into a true "presence detector" that can tell you a sleeping baby is still breathing.

## Physics of Breathing Detection in CSI

When a person breathes, their chest moves approximately 5-10mm. This tiny displacement changes the path length of reflected wireless signals by up to 20mm (round trip). At 5 GHz (lambda ~= 0.06m), a 20mm path length change corresponds to a phase shift of 2*pi*0.02/0.06 ~= 2.1 radians — measurable in phase-sensitive CSI. In amplitude (IQ magnitude), the change is typically ~0.1% of the total amplitude — tiny but consistent.

The key is that breathing is periodic. A person breathes at 12-20 times per minute (0.2-0.33 Hz at rest, up to 0.5 Hz during mild activity). The CSI signal shows a weak periodic oscillation at this frequency. By taking the FFT of a 30-second window of deltaRMS samples, a sharp peak at the breathing frequency emerges from the noise floor.

The phase change cycles at TWICE the physical breathing rate because path length change cycles twice per breath cycle (chest goes out and comes back in, changing path length from +d to -d and back to +d). So a 15-breath/minute breathing rate (0.25 Hz) produces a CSI phase oscillation at 0.5 Hz, and we look for peaks at 0.2-1.0 Hz in the FFT spectrum.

## BandpassDetector Implementation

New file: mothership/internal/signal/breathing.go

BreathingDetector struct:
- rollingBuffer []float64: circular buffer of deltaRMS samples, default 60 samples (30s at 2Hz adaptive rate)
- bufferSize int: configurable, default 60
- windowFn []float64: precomputed Hann window coefficients (reduces spectral leakage)
- sampleRateHz float64: current sample rate (adaptive, from Phase 2 adaptive sensing rate bead)
- minFreqHz float64: low end of breathing band (default 0.2 Hz)
- maxFreqHz float64: high end of breathing band (default 1.0 Hz)
- snrThreshold float64: minimum peak-to-noise ratio in dB to declare breathing (default 3 dB)

Methods:
- AddSample(deltaRMS float64): append to rolling buffer, overwrite oldest when full
- Detect() BreathingResult: run FFT, find peak in breathing band, compute SNR, return result
- BreathingResult: {IsBreathing bool, FrequencyHz float64, Confidence float64, PeakSNRdB float64}

FFT implementation: use gonum.org/v1/gonum/dsp/fourier (already a project dependency from the UKF). Apply Hann window to buffer before FFT to reduce spectral leakage. FFT output is complex64 array; take abs to get amplitude spectrum. Bin resolution = sampleRateHz / bufferSize. For 2Hz * 60 samples: resolution = 0.033 Hz, which gives good separation of breathing harmonics.

SNR computation: peak amplitude in [0.2, 1.0] Hz band divided by median amplitude of the full spectrum (median is robust to other peaks). SNR in dB = 20 * log10(peak/median). If SNR > snrThreshold, return IsBreathing=true.

## Long-Dwell Logic

Even without a breathing signal, a person who was detected in motion and then becomes still is likely still present for some time. Add a DwellTracker per link in the LinkProcessor (mothership/internal/signal/processor.go):

Dwell states:
- CLEAR: no recent motion, no breathing signal
- MOTION_DETECTED: current deltaRMS > motion threshold
- POSSIBLY_PRESENT: was MOTION_DETECTED within last 10 seconds, now below threshold. Report as "possibly present" to fusion engine (lower weight).
- STATIONARY_DETECTED: BreathingDetector reports IsBreathing=true. Report as "stationary person" with the breathing frequency.

Transitions:
- CLEAR -> MOTION_DETECTED: deltaRMS > motion threshold
- MOTION_DETECTED -> POSSIBLY_PRESENT: deltaRMS < threshold for > 0.5s (debounce)
- POSSIBLY_PRESENT -> MOTION_DETECTED: deltaRMS > threshold again
- POSSIBLY_PRESENT -> STATIONARY_DETECTED: BreathingDetector fires
- POSSIBLY_PRESENT -> CLEAR: 60 seconds without motion or breathing signal
- STATIONARY_DETECTED -> POSSIBLY_PRESENT: BreathingDetector no longer fires
- STATIONARY_DETECTED -> CLEAR: 120 seconds without motion or breathing signal (longer timeout because breathing detection is highly confident)

The dwell timer prevents premature "CLEAR" declarations for people sitting quietly, which is a common and highly frustrating false-negative.

## Sensitivity Constraints

Breathing detection only works reliably under these conditions:
1. Direct line-of-sight (LoS) or single-reflection path between TX and RX — through-wall detection is too noisy
2. The person is within the first Fresnel zone of the TX-RX link (see fusion bead)
3. Link health score (ambient confidence bead) > 0.7 — low-confidence links produce too much noise
4. No other people moving in the scene (other motion dominates the signal)
5. Minimum duration: 15s of data before the first detection can fire (half the FFT window)

The system should gate breathing detection using the link health score from the ambient confidence bead. If health_score < 0.7, set BreathingDetector.enabled = false for that link.

## Dashboard Integration

Add a "Stationary person" indicator to the dashboard link presence panel (distinct from the motion indicator):
- Slow-pulsing blue dot (not the motion red/green) when STATIONARY_DETECTED state
- Tooltip showing estimated breathing rate in breaths-per-minute (=frequencyHz * 60)
- Timeline event logged: "Stationary person detected on [link] at [time] — breathing at {N} bpm"

Add to the link health WebSocket message ("link_health" type): breathing_state ("CLEAR"/"POSSIBLY_PRESENT"/"MOTION_DETECTED"/"STATIONARY_DETECTED"), breathing_freq_hz (null if not detected).

## Tests

- Test FFT output with synthetic breathing waveform: inject 60 samples of sin(2*pi*0.3*t) + noise (sigma=0.001) into BreathingDetector.AddSample(), verify Detect() returns IsBreathing=true, FrequencyHz ~= 0.3, SNR > 3 dB
- Test that uniform random noise (no periodic component) does not trigger breathing detection (false positive rate < 5% across 1000 trials with sigma=0.001)
- Test long-dwell timer transitions: MOTION_DETECTED -> POSSIBLY_PRESENT after 0.5s quiescence, POSSIBLY_PRESENT -> CLEAR after 60s, STATIONARY_DETECTED -> CLEAR after 120s
- Test that BreathingDetector is disabled when health_score < 0.7
- Test Hann window application produces expected output for a known input
- Test that a breathing frequency outside the [0.2, 1.0] Hz band is not reported

## Acceptance Criteria

- Breathing detection fires for a stationary person in direct LoS with good link quality (health_score > 0.7) at SNR > 15 dB
- False positive rate < 5% on an empty room with a high-quality link
- Breathing frequency displayed in dashboard in breaths-per-minute (converted from FFT peak Hz)
- Long-dwell logic prevents premature "CLEAR" declaration for a stationary person for at least 60 seconds after last motion
- Breathing detection correctly gated off on low-health links
- Dwell state transitions logged in activity timeline
- Tests pass
close_reason: Implemented stationary person detection via FFT-based breathing analysis. FFTBreathingDetector with 30s rolling buffer, DwellTracker state machine, health gating, dashboard integration with pulsing blue indicator, timeline event logging. All tests passing.

title: BLE address rotation detection & identity continuity
description:
## Overview
Handle MAC address rotation in BLE devices (phones rotate every 15-30 min) to maintain continuous identity tracking.

## Backend (mothership/ble/)
- Rotation heuristics: manufacturer data fingerprinting, time+RSSI proximity, position continuity, merge confirmation
- ble_device_aliases table: addr, canonical_addr, confidence, first_seen, last_seen
- Alias matching in blob-to-device scoring: resolve rotated address to canonical identity
- Graceful fallback: 5-min window before clearing identity when rotation is unresolved

## Dashboard UI
- Rotation icon indicator in BLE device registry
- Manual merge/split UI: 'These look like the same device. Merge?' confirmation
- Alias history expandable in device detail panel

## Acceptance
- Identity continuity across address rotation with >90% precision in test scenarios
- No duplicate person tracks created on rotation event
- Alias history queryable via GET /api/ble/devices/{mac}/aliases
close_reason: Implemented BLE address rotation detection & identity continuity with manufacturer data fingerprinting, time+RSSI proximity heuristics, and merge confirmation. Backend includes RotationDetector, ble_device_aliases table, and REST API endpoints. Dashboard UI includes rotation icon indicator, manual merge/split UI, and alias history expandable panel.

title: config + main: wire migration window from SPAXEL_MIGRATION_WINDOW_HOURS
description:

title: Restructure index.html home page around glanceable status + 3 cards
description:
## Goal

Per plan.md §8e information architecture, `index.html` becomes a small home page, not a dumping ground for every panel.

## New home layout

- **Row 1 — Status headline:** One line, one background color. Content: aggregate summary ("All clear — 3 people home, 5 devices online" / "2 devices offline" / "Fall alert in Kitchen"). Uses `--ok` / `--warn` / `--alert` backgrounds.
- **Row 2 — Three cards:**
  1. **People & Zones** — occupancy count + top-of-list zone(s) with people names.
  2. **Devices & Fleet Health** — online/total node count + any device in warn/offline state.
  3. **Recent Events** — last 3–5 events with relative time.
- **Row 3 — Optional:** Morning briefing card (when fresh), security-mode toggle, anomaly banner.

Each card is a link to its detail route. No inline 3D scene. No fleet table. No security modal on home.

## Scope

1. Move 3D viewer and its supporting panels from `index.html` to a new route `/live` (can reuse existing markup; serve as `live.html`).
2. Keep `fleet.html` as `/fleet`.
3. Create `/setup` route for calibration/floor-plan setup (already partly in `index.html`).
4. Rewrite `index.html` to contain only the status headline + cards layout, styled with tokens from spaxel-0n8r.
5. Add `js/home-cards.js` that pulls the initial snapshot from `/ws/dashboard` and populates the three cards. Incremental updates refresh card counts only.

## Verification

- `wc -l dashboard/index.html` < 300.
- Playwright screenshot of home page at 1440×900 shows: status headline, three cards in a row, no overlapping panels.
- Mobile screenshot (390×844) shows the same content as a single column with bottom nav.
- Click on each card navigates to the correct detail route.

Traceability: plan.md §8e "Information architecture", §8f item 4.
close_reason: Home page restructuring already complete (commit 7162d45). index.html is 109 lines (well under 300). Status headline uses --ok/--warn/--alert backgrounds. Three cards (People and Zones, Devices and Fleet Health, Recent Events) populated by js/home-cards.js from /ws/dashboard snapshot with incremental updates. Row 3 extras: briefing card, anomaly banner, security toggle. Mobile bottom nav. All go tests pass, go vet clean.
