title: Activity timeline
description:
## Background

Spaxel generates a continuous stream of events: presence detections, zone transitions, alerts, system events, learning milestones, health changes. Without a structured event stream, debugging is difficult, history is lost, and the system appears as a black box. The activity timeline is the universal event log — a chronological record of everything the system has seen. It doubles as the primary debugging interface and enables time-travel replay (an engineer can tap any timeline event and the 3D view jumps back to that moment).

## Internal Event Bus

New package: mothership/internal/events/bus.go

EventBus provides a typed publish-subscribe mechanism for all internal events. All subsystems publish to the bus; the timeline, automation engine, and notification module subscribe.

Implementation: a simple channel-based pub/sub. Publisher side: bus.Publish(EventType, EventPayload). Subscriber side: bus.Subscribe(EventType) returns a channel. Multiple subscribers per event type are supported (fan-out).

EventType enum:
- MotionDetected, MotionCleared
- ZoneTransition (a person crossed a portal)
- ZoneOccupancyChanged (any occupancy change, including by anonymous tracks)
- FallDetected, FallAcknowledged
- NodeConnected, NodeDisconnected, NodeOTAComplete
- BLEDeviceFirstSeen, BLEIdentityAssigned
- WeightUpdate, DiurnalBaselineActivated
- AnomalyDetected, AnomalyAcknowledged
- SleepSessionStart, SleepSessionEnd
- FeedbackSubmitted

EventPayload is a typed interface. Each event type has its own concrete struct.

## Timeline Storage

SQLite table:
CREATE TABLE events (
  id TEXT PRIMARY KEY,
  type TEXT NOT NULL,
  timestamp DATETIME NOT NULL,
  person_id TEXT,
  zone_id TEXT,
  data_json TEXT NOT NULL,  -- full event payload as JSON
  feedback_type TEXT,       -- populated by feedback loop (Phase 7)
  created_at DATETIME DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP
);
CREATE INDEX idx_events_timestamp ON events (timestamp DESC);
CREATE INDEX idx_events_person ON events (person_id, timestamp DESC);
CREATE INDEX idx_events_zone ON events (zone_id, timestamp DESC);
CREATE INDEX idx_events_type ON events (type, timestamp DESC);

Timeline subscriber: a goroutine that reads from the bus and writes to SQLite. Buffered with a 1000-event queue to avoid blocking publishers. If the queue fills: log a warning and drop the oldest (the bus is lossy for the storage subscriber, but this should never happen at normal event rates).

## Dashboard Timeline Panel

Sidebar panel showing events in reverse-chronological order.

Event visual rendering per type:
- MotionDetected / ZoneTransition: person avatar (coloured circle with initial) + description + timestamp + thumbs
- FallDetected: red shield icon + "Possible fall: [person] in [zone]" + Acknowledge button
- NodeConnected / NodeDisconnected: grey dot icon + "Node [label] connected/disconnected"
- WeightUpdate / DiurnalBaselineActivated: green brain icon + "Detection accuracy improved" / "Daily patterns activated"
- AnomalyDetected: orange warning icon + "Anomaly: [description]"
- SleepSessionStart/End: moon icon + "Alice went to sleep" / "Alice woke up"

Event description templates (plain English, no jargon):
- ZoneTransition: "{person_name} walked from {from_zone} to {to_zone}"
- MotionDetected: "Motion detected in {zone_name}" (if no identity)
- NodeDisconnected: "Node {label} went offline — {duration} downtime"
- DiurnalBaselineActivated: "System has learned {person_name}'s daily patterns. Detection accuracy improved."

Virtualized rendering: use a virtual scroll list (render only visible items) since the timeline can have thousands of events. Implement using IntersectionObserver API for lazy loading of off-screen items.

Thumbs-up/down on each event: delegates to the feedback module (spaxel-3ps). Rendered as small icon buttons on the right side of each event row.

## Search and Filter

Filter bar above timeline:
- Type filter: checkboxes for event categories (Presence, Zones, Alerts, System, Learning). Default: all.
- Person filter: dropdown "All people / Alice / Bob / Unknown"
- Zone filter: dropdown "All zones / Kitchen / Bedroom / etc."
- Date range: "Today / Last 7 days / Last 30 days / Custom"
- Text search: fuzzy match on event description text (client-side filtering on loaded events; server-side for date-range queries)

Filtered queries use the indexed columns in the events table. Return at most 500 events per page; "Load more" button for pagination.

## Expert vs Simple Mode

Expert mode: all event types visible. System events (node health, weight updates) shown as secondary (smaller text, greyed color).

Simple mode: only person-relevant events: ZoneTransition, FallDetected, AnomalyDetected, SleepSessionEnd (morning summary). System events hidden. This prevents "terminal-style" log noise from confusing non-technical users.

Mode is set by the current dashboard mode (expert vs simple) and passed as ?mode=expert or ?mode=simple to the API.

## Tap-to-Jump (Time-Travel Coordination)

When a timeline event is clicked (in expert mode), the dashboard emits a "jump_to_time" command with the event's timestamp. The time-travel replay module (Phase 8, separate bead) listens for this command and:
1. Pauses live playback
2. Seeks the CSI recording buffer to the event timestamp
3. Begins replay from that point
4. The 3D scene shows the "replay" state at that timestamp

Clicking the event also highlights it in the timeline (selected state) and shows a "Now replaying" chip in the timeline header.

## REST API

GET /api/events?since=&until=&type=&person_id=&zone_id=&limit=&mode=expert|simple
Returns: paginated list of Event objects with all fields.

GET /api/events/{id}: single event detail
POST /api/events/{id}/feedback: submit feedback for an event (delegates to feedback module)

## Tests

- Test EventBus pub/sub: publish event, verify subscriber channel receives it within 10ms
- Test that multiple subscribers all receive the same event
- Test timeline storage: publish 10 events of different types, verify all appear in SQLite with correct fields
- Test search and filter: insert events for two people and two zones, query by person -> correct subset returned
- Test time-range filtering: insert events at T-1h and T-25h; query since T-24h -> only T-1h event
- Test virtualized rendering handles 1000+ events without layout jank (performance test in browser)
- Test tap-to-jump emits correct timestamp to time-travel player
- Test expert vs simple mode filter: system events excluded in simple mode

## Acceptance Criteria

- All event types appear in the timeline within 1 second of firing
- Search and filter queries return correct subsets
- Tap-to-jump coordinates with time-travel player (3D scene seeks to correct timestamp)
- Simple mode hides system events while showing person-relevant events
- Feedback buttons appear on each event and invoke the feedback module correctly
- Timeline handles 10,000+ events without UI slowdown via virtualised rendering
- Tests pass

title: Ambient confidence score and link health
description:
## Background

Not all sensing links are equal. A link where a wall bisects the Fresnel zone produces consistently noisy detections. A link experiencing WiFi congestion from neighbour networks drops packets and has unreliable amplitude measurements. A link near a microwave oven sees periodic interference bursts. Without a quality metric, the fusion engine treats all links equally, and poor-quality links introduce noise that degrades overall localisation accuracy.

The ambient confidence score is a per-link quality metric that: (1) gates and weights detection algorithms so poor links contribute less, (2) surfaces actionable quality information to the user, and (3) powers the link weather diagnostics feature. A composite system-wide Detection Quality gauge summarises overall system health.

## Per-Link Health Metrics

New module: mothership/internal/health/linkhealth.go

LinkHealthScorer computes five sub-metrics per link, each in [0, 1]:

1. SNR Estimate (weight 40%)
   Ratio of motion-period delta to quiet-period delta, expressed as a quality score.
   During known-quiet periods (determined by extended absence of motion, minimum 60s), record the ambient deltaRMS variance (sigma_quiet). During motion-active periods, record deltaRMS peaks (signal level). SNR_ratio = signal_level / sigma_quiet. Map to [0,1] via: score = min(1.0, log10(SNR_ratio) / log10(100)) where SNR=100:1 -> score=1.0, SNR=10:1 -> score=0.5.

2. Phase Stability (weight 30%)
   During known-quiet periods, compute the variance of the phase offset across subcarriers. Low variance indicates stable hardware clock synchronisation between TX and RX, which is a prerequisite for reliable phase-based detection. High variance (>0.5 radians) suggests temperature drift or near-field metal interference.
   score = max(0, 1 - phase_variance / 0.5)

3. Packet Rate Health (weight 20%)
   actual_pps / configured_rate. If configured at 50 Hz and receiving 40 Hz: score = 0.8.
   Rolling average over 10-second window.

4. Baseline Drift (weight 10%)
   Rate of change of the EMA baseline over a 1-hour sliding window. High drift indicates an unstable environment (e.g. gradual temperature change, something blocking or unblocking the Fresnel zone). Computed as: drift_rate = |B_t - B_{t-1h}| / |B_{t-1h}| (normalised L2 change per hour).
   score = max(0, 1 - drift_rate / 0.1) where 10% per hour -> score=0.0.

5. Composite Score
   composite = 0.4 * snr + 0.3 * phase_stability + 0.2 * packet_rate + 0.1 * (1 - baseline_drift_normalized)
   Clamped to [0, 1]. Updated every 10 seconds.

## Dashboard Visualisation

Per-link health is surfaced in multiple places:

In the 3D view (Phase 3 node placement UI, spaxel-qq6):
- Link line thickness: 2px (health > 0.7), 1px (health 0.4-0.7), 0.5px (health < 0.4)
- Link line colour: green (#22c55e at health=1.0) through yellow (#eab308 at health=0.5) through red (#ef4444 at health=0)

In the Link Health panel (sidebar, shown on link click):
- Per-metric breakdown: four sub-score gauges (SNR, Phase Stability, Packet Rate, Baseline Drift) with label, value, and interpretation
- Sparkline chart: composite health score over last 24 hours
- "Why is this low?" contextual hint based on which sub-metric is lowest

System-wide Detection Quality gauge (dashboard header):
- Single number: weighted average of all active link composite scores
- Rendered as a circular gauge (0-100%) with colour gradient
- Tooltip: "Based on N active links. Weakest link: [link name] at [score%]"

## API

GET /api/links returns:
[{
  "link_id": "aabbccddee:ff:00:11:22:33",
  "tx_mac": "aa:bb:cc:dd:ee:ff",
  "rx_mac": "00:11:22:33:44:55",
  "health_score": 0.83,
  "health_details": {
    "snr": 0.91,
    "phase_stability": 0.78,
    "packet_rate": 0.97,
    "baseline_drift": 0.62
  },
  "last_updated": "2026-03-27T14:23:45Z"
}]

## Gating Effects

The health score gates and weights two downstream systems:
1. BreathingDetector (stationary person detection, spaxel-r37): disabled when composite health_score < 0.7
2. FusionEngine (spaxel-m9a): each link's contribution to the 3D occupancy grid is multiplied by its health_score. A link with score=0.3 contributes only 30% as much as a link with score=1.0. This prevents degraded links from producing noisy phantom blobs.

The gating thresholds (0.7 for breathing, any value for weighted fusion) are configurable via mothership config.

## Integration with Existing Code

LinkHealthScorer is instantiated in mothership/internal/ingestion/server.go alongside the existing signal processors. It receives:
- Packet arrival timestamps (to compute actual PPS vs configured)
- deltaRMS values from the signal processor (for SNR computation)
- Phase values from the signal processor (for phase stability)
- Baseline vectors from BaselineManager (for drift computation)

The health scores are updated in background via a goroutine that fires every 10 seconds. Results are published on the internal event bus as LinkHealthUpdate events, which the dashboard hub broadcasts as "link_health" WebSocket messages.

## Tests

- Test composite score computation with mock inputs: all 1.0 -> 1.0, packet_rate=0.5 others 1.0 -> weighted result
- Test SNR sub-score mapping: SNR_ratio=1 -> score=0, SNR_ratio=10 -> score=0.5, SNR_ratio=100 -> score=1.0
- Test phase stability: variance=0 -> score=1.0, variance=0.5 -> score=0.0, variance=0.25 -> score=0.5
- Test that breathing detection gating fires correctly when score drops below 0.7
- Test FusionEngine link weight reflects health score (inspect internal state after injection)
- Test API response format matches documented schema
- Test that health score updates are published to the event bus

## Acceptance Criteria

- Per-link health scores computed and visible in dashboard for all active links
- 3D link line thickness and colour reflect health score in real-time
- Detection Quality gauge shows system-wide average health, updates every 10 seconds
- BreathingDetector correctly gated off when link health < 0.7
- FusionEngine link weights reflect health scores (verified via test)

title: fix: mqtt/client.go API mismatches for paho v1.5.0
description:
## Problem
`internal/mqtt/client.go` uses methods that don't exist in paho.mqtt.golang v1.5.0 (the version in go.mod):

1. **Line 120**: `opts.SetCleanOnConnect(true)` — method doesn't exist. In paho v1.5.0 the method is `opts.SetCleanSession(true)`
2. **Line 147**: `opts.OnDisconnect = func(...)` — field doesn't exist. In paho v1.5.0 the callback is set via `opts.SetConnectionLostHandler(func(...))`
3. **Lines 402, 404**: Redundant type assertions inside a type switch:
   - `case string: data = []byte(v.(string))` — `v` is already typed as `string` in this case branch; change to `data = []byte(v)`
   - `case []byte: data = v.([]byte)` — `v` is already `[]byte`; change to `data = v`

## Fixes
1. Line 120: `opts.SetCleanOnConnect(true)` → `opts.SetCleanSession(true)`
2. Lines 147-160 (OnDisconnect assignment block): Replace with `opts.SetConnectionLostHandler(func(client mqtt.Client, err error) { ... })`
3. Lines 402, 404: Remove the redundant type assertions

## Verify
```bash
cd /home/coding/spaxel/mothership && PATH=$PATH:/home/coding/go/bin go build ./internal/mqtt/
```

title: CSI simulator Go CLI
description:
## Background

The pre-deployment simulator (spaxel-btj) provides a browser-based spatial planning tool. The CSI simulator CLI is a complementary developer tool: a standalone Go command-line program that opens WebSocket connections to a running mothership as virtual nodes and injects synthetic CSI binary frames. This enables automated integration testing, performance benchmarking, and algorithm development entirely without ESP32 hardware.

The critical difference: the pre-deployment simulator generates CSI on the server side (using the simulation API). The CLI simulator generates CSI externally and connects via the standard node WebSocket interface — testing the full network stack and protocol, not just the signal processing.

## CLI Implementation

New Go command: mothership/cmd/sim/main.go

Usage examples:
  sim --mothership localhost:8080 --nodes 4 --walkers 2 --rate 20 --duration 60
  sim --mothership localhost:8080 --token abc123def456 --nodes 2 --walkers 1 --seed 42
  sim --space "6x4x2.5" --nodes 4 --walkers 3 --rate 50 --duration 120 --ble
  sim --verify --mothership localhost:8080 --nodes 2 --walkers 1 --duration 10

CLI Flags:
- --mothership: URL of the mothership (default: ws://localhost:8080/ws)
- --token: provision token. If not specified, automatically provisions via POST /api/provision with synthetic credentials.
- --nodes: number of virtual nodes (default 2). Each node opens a separate WebSocket connection.
- --walkers: number of synthetic walkers (default 1).
- --rate: CSI transmission rate in Hz per node pair (default 20).
- --duration: total run time in seconds (default 60). "0" means run until Ctrl+C.
- --seed: random seed for reproducible walker paths (default: random seed, logged at startup).
- --space: room dimensions in "WxDxH" format (default "5x5x2.5").
- --ble: include synthetic BLE advertisements (one BLE device per walker, with stable random MAC).
- --verify: after --duration seconds, verify that the mothership produced the expected number of blobs. Exit 0 on success, 1 on failure.
- --noise-sigma: Gaussian noise standard deviation for I/Q generation (default 0.005).
- --wall: add a wall as "x1,y1,x2,y2" (can be repeated). Walls affect the path loss model.
- --output-csv: write synthetic ground truth (walker positions + link deltaRMS) to a CSV file for offline analysis.

## Synthetic CSI Frame Generation

For each virtual node pair (TX, RX) and each walker at each timestep:

1. Compute RSSI from path loss model (same as simulator physics, mothership/internal/simulator/physics.go — reuse this package).

2. Compute deltaRMS from Fresnel zone overlap (same physics model).

3. Generate binary CSI frame matching the Phase 1 protocol format:
   Header (24 bytes):
   - Magic: 0xABCDEF01 (4 bytes)
   - Version: 1 (1 byte)
   - Node MAC: 6 bytes (synthetic, consistent per virtual node)
   - Peer MAC: 6 bytes (TX node's MAC for RX-side frames)
   - Channel: 6 (2.4GHz ch 6) or 36 (5GHz) — configurable
   - RSSI: 1 byte (signed, from path loss calculation)
   - Num subcarriers: 64 (1 byte)
   - Timestamp_us: 8 bytes (current Unix microseconds)

   Payload (128 bytes = 64 subcarriers * 2 bytes each):
   - For each subcarrier i: I = amplitude * cos(phase_i) + noise, Q = amplitude * sin(phase_i) + noise
   - amplitude: from Fresnel zone deltaRMS model
   - phase_i: phase_base + i * phase_step + phase_noise (simulate subcarrier phase variation)
   - noise: gaussian(sigma=--noise-sigma)
   - Values are int8 (clamped to [-127, 127])

The frame format is validated against the actual firmware output by comparing to real recorded frames in docs/research/reference_frames.bin (if available).

## Connection Protocol

Each virtual node:
1. Opens WebSocket to ws://{mothership}/ws
2. Sends hello message: {"type":"hello","mac":"{virtual_mac}","firmware_version":"sim-1.0.0","capabilities":{"can_tx":true,"can_rx":true},"free_heap":200000,"wifi_rssi":-45,"ip_addr":"127.0.0.{n}"}
3. Waits for role push from mothership (expects {"type":"role","role":N})
4. If receives {"type":"reject"}: logs error and exits with code 2
5. Begins sending CSI frames at the configured rate using the binary WebSocket message format (not JSON)
6. Also sends health messages every 10s: {"type":"health","heap":200000,"rssi":-45,"uptime_s":N}
7. If --ble: sends BLE relay messages every 5s: {"type":"ble","devices":[{"mac":"...","name":"sim-person-1","rssi":-60}]}

## Verification Mode (--verify)

After --duration seconds:
1. Stop sending CSI frames
2. Wait 2 seconds for pipeline to settle
3. GET {mothership}/api/blobs — gets current list of active blobs
4. Assert: blob_count == walker_count (within ±1 tolerance)
5. If all walkers are within the room bounds: assert all walkers have a blob within 2m distance
6. Print: "PASS: {blob_count} blobs detected for {walker_count} walkers" or "FAIL: expected N blobs, got M"
7. Exit 0 (PASS) or 1 (FAIL)

This is the CI smoke test that verifies the full pipeline end-to-end without hardware.

## CI Integration

Add a CI step to the mothership GitHub Actions workflow (if one exists, or document the command):
1. Start mothership with test config (in-memory SQLite, no recording)
2. Run: sim --verify --nodes 2 --walkers 1 --duration 10 --seed 42
3. Assert exit code 0

This becomes the primary end-to-end integration test. If the sim fails to produce blobs, something in the pipeline is broken.

## Performance Testing

The simulator supports high-throughput testing:
- sim --nodes 16 --walkers 4 --rate 50 --duration 60: measures mothership throughput at 16 nodes * 50 Hz = 800 frames/second
- The simulator prints throughput statistics at the end: frames sent, frames per second, CPU time
- Use for benchmarking and profiling the mothership processing pipeline

## Files to Create

- mothership/cmd/sim/main.go: CLI entry point with all flags
- mothership/cmd/sim/generator.go: synthetic CSI frame generator
- mothership/cmd/sim/walker.go: synthetic walker movement simulation
- mothership/cmd/sim/verify.go: blob count verification logic
- mothership/internal/simulator/physics.go: reuse from pre-deployment simulator (shared package)

## Tests

- Test that generated frames have the correct binary header format (magic, version bytes in correct positions)
- Test that RSSI value is within plausible range for the given walker distance (e.g. walker at 2m, wall_attenuation=0 -> RSSI in [-50, -70])
- Test that generated I/Q values are clamped to int8 range [-127, 127]
- Test hello message format matches what the mothership ingestion server expects (parsed successfully)
- Test that --verify correctly detects missing blobs (inject 1 walker, mock mothership returns 0 blobs -> FAIL)
- Test --seed 42 produces identical walker paths across two runs (reproducibility)
- Test --output-csv generates a CSV with correct headers and ground truth positions

## Acceptance Criteria

- CLI connects and streams synthetic CSI to a running mothership
- Mothership blob count equals walker count (within ±1) when --verify is used
- CLI exits cleanly after --duration seconds
- --verify returns exit code 0 when blobs match, exit code 1 when they don't
- Works correctly in a CI environment without hardware
- High-throughput test (16 nodes, 50 Hz) completes without mothership errors or OOM
- Tests pass
close_reason: CSI simulator CLI already fully implemented and tested in mothership/cmd/sim/. All 13 tests pass, go vet clean. Features include: all CLI flags, synthetic CSI binary frame generation, Fresnel zone physics, walker simulation, BLE, blob verification, CSV output.

title: Replace Simple/Expert mode toggle with progressive disclosure
description:
## Goal

Per plan.md §8e "Information architecture", the Simple vs Expert toggle is removed. The home page is the simple surface by default; advanced views live at their own routes (`/live`, `/setup`).

## Scope

2. Remove the `localStorage` key that tracks the user's mode preference.

## Verification

All verification criteria pass: no Simple/Expert text, no simpleMode/expertMode keys, /live reachable from home via nav and card links.

title: Home automation integration (MQTT and webhooks)
description:
## Background

Many home automation systems — Home Assistant, OpenHAB, Node-RED, Domoticz — use MQTT as their primary integration backbone. By publishing spaxel state as MQTT topics with Home Assistant auto-discovery payloads, spaxel becomes a first-class presence sensor in any HA setup. No custom integration code is needed: the entities appear automatically in Home Assistant's entity registry simply by being powered on and having MQTT configured. This makes spaxel accessible to millions of home automation users who already have HA running.

## MQTT Client

Optional MQTT client in mothership/internal/mqtt/client.go.

The client is optional — if MQTT is not configured, the module is a no-op. Configuration via dashboard Settings or mothership config file:
- broker_url: e.g. "tcp://homeassistant.local:1883" or "mqtt://192.168.1.100:1883"
- username, password (optional)
- tls: true/false
- client_id: "spaxel-{mothership_id}" (unique per mothership instance)
- retain: true (retained messages for presence and occupancy topics)

Use the github.com/eclipse/paho.mqtt.golang library (the de-facto standard Go MQTT client).

Reconnect policy: exponential backoff from 5s to 120s, indefinite retry. Log reconnect attempts.

## MQTT Topic Structure

All topics are prefixed with "spaxel/{mothership_id}/" where mothership_id is the unique ID from the mothership config.

Person presence:
- Topic: spaxel/{mothership_id}/person/{person_id}/presence
- Payload: "home" or "not_home" (plain string, retained)
- Updated when: a person's first labelled BLE device is seen (home) or all their BLE devices have been absent for > 15 minutes (not_home)

Zone occupancy:
- Topic: spaxel/{mothership_id}/zone/{zone_id}/occupancy
- Payload: integer count (plain string, retained, e.g. "2")
- Topic: spaxel/{mothership_id}/zone/{zone_id}/occupants
- Payload: JSON array of person names (retained, e.g. ["Alice","Bob"])
- Updated on every zone occupancy change

Fall detection:
- Topic: spaxel/{mothership_id}/fall_detected
- Payload: JSON {"person_id":"...","person_label":"Alice","zone_id":"...","zone_name":"Hallway","timestamp":"2026-03-27T14:23:00Z"}
- Not retained (event topic)

System health:
- Topic: spaxel/{mothership_id}/system/health
- Payload: JSON {"node_count":4,"online_count":4,"detection_quality":0.87,"mode":"home"}
- Retained, updated every 60s

## Home Assistant Auto-Discovery

On initial MQTT connection (and on reconnect), publish HA auto-discovery payloads to the homeassistant/ prefix topics. HA processes these automatically and adds the entities to its registry.

Per person: binary_sensor (presence)
Discovery topic: homeassistant/binary_sensor/spaxel_{mothership_id}_{person_id}/config
Payload (JSON, retained):
{
  "name": "Alice Presence",
  "unique_id": "spaxel_{mothership_id}_{person_id}_presence",
  "state_topic": "spaxel/{mothership_id}/person/{person_id}/presence",
  "payload_on": "home",
  "payload_off": "not_home",
  "device_class": "presence",
  "device": {
    "identifiers": ["spaxel_{mothership_id}"],
    "name": "Spaxel",
    "model": "Spaxel Presence System",
    "manufacturer": "Spaxel"
  }
}

Per zone: sensor (occupancy count) + binary_sensor (zone occupied)
Discovery topic: homeassistant/sensor/spaxel_{mothership_id}_{zone_id}_occupancy/config
Payload: {name, unique_id, state_topic, unit_of_measurement: "people", device_class: null, device: ...}

Fall detection: binary_sensor with device_class: "safety"
Discovery topic: homeassistant/binary_sensor/spaxel_{mothership_id}_fall/config

System mode: select (home/away/sleep) or input_select via MQTT
Discovery + command topics so HA can set system mode.

When a person or zone is deleted, publish an empty payload to the corresponding discovery topic (HA treats this as "remove entity").

## Event Publishing

The MQTT client subscribes to the internal event bus and publishes:
- ZoneCrossingEvent -> update zone occupancy topics
- PersonPresenceChangeEvent -> update person presence topic
- FallEvent -> publish to fall_detected topic
- SystemHealthEvent -> publish to system health topic (60s interval)

## Generic Webhook Integration

A complementary feature: a system-level webhook that delivers ALL spaxel events to a single user-configured URL, not just user-configured automations. This is for integrations that want to receive the full event stream (e.g. a custom backend, a logging service, or a complex HA automation that can't be expressed in the automation builder).

POST /api/settings/system-webhook: set URL
All internal events are serialised as JSON and POST'd to this URL with event type in the X-Spaxel-Event header.
Same retry policy as automation webhooks (one retry after 30s on 5xx).

## Dashboard Settings

Settings panel -> "Integrations" tab:
- MQTT section: broker URL, username, password (masked), TLS toggle, "Test Connection" button, "Publish discovery payloads now" button
- System webhook section: URL input, "Test" button, recent delivery log (last 10 events with status)
- Status indicator: green dot if MQTT connected, red if disconnected with last error message

## Tests

- Test MQTT connection to a local broker (use go-mqtt-broker test dependency or a Docker Eclipse Mosquitto in CI)
- Test auto-discovery payload format against HA MQTT auto-discovery spec (compare JSON structure)
- Test retained messages: verify that on reconnect, retained messages are not re-published if unchanged
- Test that fall_detected topic is published on FallEvent
- Test that zone occupancy topics update correctly on ZoneCrossingEvent
- Test auto-discovery cleanup: deleting a person sends empty payload to discovery topic
- Test system webhook delivers all event types to a mock HTTP server
- Test reconnect policy: simulate broker disconnect, verify client reconnects with backoff

## Acceptance Criteria

- Spaxel entities appear automatically in Home Assistant entity list after MQTT config without any HA config editing
- Person presence binary_sensor in HA updates within 30 seconds of BLE presence change
- Zone occupancy sensor in HA reflects correct integer count
- Fall detection binary_sensor fires and resets correctly
- System mode select allows bidirectional control from HA (HA can set spaxel mode via MQTT)
- System webhook receives all event types
- Tests pass

title: Guided troubleshooting (enhanced for production)
description:
## Background

The Phase 4 guided troubleshooting bead (spaxel-r0l) covers onboarding failures and basic node offline recovery. This Phase 9 bead extends it with production-quality proactive assistance that emerges from the system's own understanding of its state: detection quality degradation warnings with root-cause analysis, post-feedback explanations that close the loop for curious users, feature discovery notifications that teach users about new capabilities as they become available, and a searchable help system for self-service support.

The philosophy: the system should feel like a knowledgeable, helpful companion that notices problems before you do and explains them in terms you understand.

## 1. Proactive Quality Prompts

Trigger: when ambient confidence score (Phase 5, spaxel-sbi) drops below 0.6 on any link for more than 5 consecutive minutes.

Action: show a non-blocking, dismissible prompt card in the dashboard:
"Detection quality has dropped on [Node A] to [Node B]. This link is at [N]% reliability."

The card includes:
- A "Diagnose" button that runs the link weather diagnostics (Phase 5, spaxel-32o) and shows the results in a slide-out panel.
- "Dismiss for today" option (hides until tomorrow or until quality recovers and drops again)
- The 3D link line is highlighted (pulsing amber) while the prompt is active

The link weather diagnostic result is shown in plain English:
- "Possible cause: new furniture may be blocking the sensing zone. [See where to move the node]"
- "Possible cause: WiFi congestion from neighbouring networks. [What to do about this]"
- "Cause unknown — system is adapting. [No action needed]"

Do NOT show this prompt if the quality drop is temporary (< 5 minutes). Many quality drops are transient (microwave oven use, brief WiFi congestion) and showing a prompt for every one would train the user to ignore them.

## 2. Repeated-Setting Change Detection

Trigger: if a user changes the same configuration setting (motion threshold, baseline tau, etc.) more than 3 times within a 24-hour period.

This is a signal that the user is struggling to find the right value. The system should proactively offer help rather than silently watch the user thrash.

Action: show a non-intrusive prompt: "Looks like you're fine-tuning the motion threshold. Would you like help finding the right value for your space?"

The "Help me tune this" button opens a guided calibration flow:
1. "Set threshold too low?" -> walk around room, system shows how many false positives at the current setting
2. "Set threshold too high?" -> sit still, system shows if motion is being missed
3. Suggests optimal value based on the diurnal baseline SNR and link health
4. "Apply suggested value: [N]" button

Track repeated changes in localStorage: {setting_name: {count, last_change_timestamp}} for the last 24h.

## 3. Post-Feedback Explanations

When a user marks a detection event as a false positive (FALSE_POSITIVE feedback from spaxel-3ps), show an explanation:

"The system detected motion here because: [link A to link B]'s signal (deltaRMS) exceeded the motion threshold by [N]x at [time]. This could be caused by: [root cause from diagnostic rules, if any match, or 'ambient RF interference' as default]. We've noted this and will apply a correction."

The explanation uses the ExplainabilitySnapshot for the event (spaxel-ez4) to identify the contributing link, and runs a quick diagnostic check (spaxel-32o rule engine) on the link's health history at the event timestamp.

Show as an expandable section ("Why did this happen?") on the feedback confirmation card, not as a separate notification.

## 4. Feature Discovery Notifications

When a feature becomes available for the first time (requires a one-time condition to be met), fire a single non-blocking notification:

Events and their messages:
- DiurnalBaselineActivated (after 7 days of data, Phase 5): "Your system has learned your home's daily patterns. Detection accuracy should improve starting today."
- FirstSleepSessionComplete (first sleep monitored, Phase 7): "Your first sleep session was tracked overnight. Tap to see your sleep summary."
- WeightUpdateApproved (first self-improving weight update, Phase 7): "Localisation accuracy improved based on your BLE device positions. Median position error decreased."
- AutomationFirstFired (first automation triggered, Phase 6): "Your first automation just ran! [View in automations log]"
- PredictionModelReady (7 days per person, Phase 7): "Presence predictions are now available for [person name]. [View predictions]"

Each notification:
- Keyed by a unique event ID stored in SQLite: feature_notifications (event_id TEXT PRIMARY KEY, fired_at DATETIME, acknowledged_at DATETIME)
- Never fires twice (primary key ensures uniqueness)
- Dismissed by tapping (marks acknowledged_at)
- Does not fire during quiet hours (uses notification manager quiet hours logic)

## 5. Contextual Help System

A "?" button in the expert mode header opens a searchable help overlay. The overlay contains:
- A search input (same fuzzy search as command palette, Phase 9 spaxel-tvq)
- A list of help articles (approximately 30 covering all major features)
- Each article: title, 1-3 sentence plain-English explanation, link to a relevant dashboard section or action

Sample articles:
- "What is a sensing link?" — "A sensing link is the path between two spaxel nodes (one transmitting, one receiving). Motion in the space between them changes the signal, which spaxel detects."
- "Why is my detection quality low?" — "Low quality usually means interference from other WiFi devices, an obstacle in the sensing zone, or the node is too far from your router. Click 'Diagnose' on the link to find the specific cause."
- "How does presence prediction work?" — "After 7 days, spaxel learns when people are typically in each room and predicts where they'll be next. Predictions appear in the Predictions panel."
- "What is the Fresnel zone?" — "The Fresnel zone is the region in space where motion has the most impact on the signal between two nodes. Spaxel uses this geometry to estimate where in the room a person is."

Articles are stored as a static JSON file (dashboard/help_articles.json, 30-50 articles). No server round-trip needed for help.

## Implementation Structure

- mothership/internal/help/notifier.go: feature discovery notification manager, persistence
- dashboard/js/proactive.js: quality prompt, repeated-setting detector, post-feedback explanation
- dashboard/js/help.js: help overlay, fuzzy search on articles, article rendering
- dashboard/help_articles.json: all help article content
- mothership/internal/diagnostics/linkweather.go: add GetDiagnosticFor(linkID, timestamp) method for post-feedback use

## Tests

- Test quality prompt trigger: mock confidence score = 0.55 for 6 minutes, verify prompt appears; confidence = 0.55 for 3 minutes, verify no prompt
- Test repeated-setting detection: mock 4 threshold changes in 20h, verify help prompt fires
- Test post-feedback explanation: inject FALSE_POSITIVE feedback on an event with a known ExplainabilitySnapshot, verify explanation text contains the contributing link name
- Test feature discovery: inject DiurnalBaselineActivated event, verify notification fires once; inject again, verify no second notification
- Test help search: query "fresnel" -> "What is the Fresnel zone?" appears in results
- Test dismissed prompts don't re-appear (localStorage + SQLite persistence)

## Acceptance Criteria

- Quality prompt appears within 5 minutes of confidence score dropping below 0.6 threshold
- Prompt does NOT appear for transient drops shorter than 5 minutes
- "Diagnose" in quality prompt shows root cause in plain language
- Repeated-setting detection fires after 3+ changes in 24h
- Post-feedback explanation shown after any FALSE_POSITIVE feedback submission
- Feature discovery notifications fire exactly once per feature, in plain language
- Help overlay opens with "?" button, search finds relevant articles
- Dismissed prompts never re-appear (unless condition reoccurs after recovery)
- Tests pass

title: Adaptive sensing rate
description:
Implement mothership-controlled sensing rate with on-device burst detection.

## Deliverables
- Mothership rate control: send rate change commands (2Hz idle ↔ 50Hz active) per link via WebSocket
- Rate decision logic: drop to 2Hz when no motion for 30s, burst to 50Hz on motion
- On-device amplitude variance check at low rate (2Hz) — if variance exceeds threshold, burst to full rate and notify mothership
- Motion hints from ESP32 to preemptively ramp adjacent links
- Firmware changes: firmware/main/csi.c to support dynamic rate changes

## Acceptance Criteria
- Idle links automatically drop to 2Hz packet rate
- Motion triggers burst to configured active rate
- ESP32 can locally detect motion onset and self-burst before mothership commands
- Adjacent links ramp up on motion hints from nearby nodes
- Tests for rate decision logic in mothership

## References
- Plan: docs/plan/plan.md item 12
- CSI capture: firmware/main/csi.c
- Signal pipeline: mothership/internal/signal/processor.go
close_reason: Implemented: ratecontrol.go (bcfd1e3, fb69190) — mothership-controlled 2Hz↔50Hz rate changes, firmware csi.c/websocket.c dynamic rate config, on-device amplitude variance burst detection

title: CSI recording buffer
description:
Implement disk-backed circular buffer for CSI frame recording.

## Deliverables
- New package: mothership/internal/recording/
- Append incoming CSI frames to disk-backed circular buffer (48h default retention)
- Binary format for efficient storage (same frame format as WebSocket)
- Configurable retention period via environment variable
- Foundation for time-travel replay feature (Phase 8)

## Acceptance Criteria
- CSI frames are persisted to disk as they arrive
- Buffer auto-prunes frames older than retention period
- Can read back frames for a given time range
- Storage is bounded (auto-prune prevents disk exhaustion)
- Tests cover write, read-back, and pruning

## References
- Frame format: mothership/internal/ingestion/frame.go (24-byte header + payload)
- Ring buffer: mothership/internal/ingestion/ring.go (in-memory reference)

title: 3D spatial visualization
description:
Full Three.js 3D scene with humanoid figures, room bounds, and node meshes.

## Deliverables
- Room bounds visualization (walls, floor, ceiling from configured dimensions)
- Floor plan texture support (uploaded image mapped to ground plane)
- Humanoid figures using SkinnedMesh + AnimationMixer (standing/walking/seated/lying)
- Vertical pillar anchors and footprint trails for tracked blobs
- Node meshes at configured 3D positions with link lines between pairs
- View presets (top-down, perspective, first-person follow)
- WebSocket integration to receive blob positions from mothership

## Acceptance Criteria
- Humanoid figures animate smoothly between postures
- User can orbit, pan, zoom with OrbitControls
- Node positions and link lines update in real-time
- Works with existing dashboard skeleton (dashboard/js/app.js)

## References
- Plan: docs/plan/plan.md item 17
- Dashboard: dashboard/index.html, dashboard/js/app.js

title: Command palette
description:
## Background

Expert users — system installers, power users, home automation enthusiasts — want to operate the dashboard entirely from the keyboard. Reaching for the mouse to navigate between nodes, zones, and features breaks focus. The command palette (Ctrl+K / Cmd+K) provides a keyboard-driven interface to every feature: navigate to any person, zone, or node by typing their name, execute commands like "Start OTA" or "Toggle Fresnel overlay", and even jump the 3D scene to specific timestamps using a time syntax.

## Trigger and Appearance

Keyboard shortcut: Ctrl+K (Windows/Linux) or Cmd+K (macOS). Also triggerable via a small search icon button in the dashboard header bar.

Available in: expert mode only. Not available in simple mode or ambient mode (those are for non-technical users who don't benefit from a command palette).

The palette appears as a centred modal overlay:
- Semi-transparent dark backdrop (opacity 0.5, backdrop-filter: blur(4px))
- Centred container: max-width 600px, border-radius 12px, background #1e293b
- Top: search input (auto-focused, placeholder "Search people, zones, nodes, commands...")
- Below: results list (max 8 visible, scrollable)

Close on: Escape key, click on backdrop, second Ctrl+K/Cmd+K.

## Search Scope

All entities are cached client-side in the dashboard's state store (already maintained from WebSocket updates). No server round-trips for search — purely client-side matching.

Search categories (in priority order in results):
1. People: names from the people registry. Icon: person silhouette. Action: jump 3D camera to person's current blob.
2. Zones: zone names. Icon: location pin. Action: jump 3D camera to zone centroid, select zone.
3. Nodes: node labels or MAC addresses. Icon: radio antenna. Action: jump 3D camera to node position, select node.
4. Recent events: last 20 timeline events (titles). Icon: clock. Action: open event detail in timeline.
5. Commands: static list of dashboard commands (see Commands below). Icon: lightning bolt. Action: execute command.
6. Time navigation: if query starts with "@", attempt time parsing (see below). Icon: clock. Action: seek replay to that time.

## Fuzzy Matching

Client-side fuzzy matching for all categories. Use a simple Jaro-Winkler or Levenshtein-distance based scorer:
- Exact prefix match: highest score. "Kit" -> "Kitchen" gets top score.
- Subsequence match: "kitch rm" -> "Kitchen" matches (not adjacent characters but in order).
- Typo tolerance: 1 character substitution tolerated for strings > 4 characters.

Implementation: a custom 30-line fuzzy match function in JavaScript. No dependencies needed. The function returns a score in [0, 1]; results with score < 0.3 are excluded.

Sort: primary by category priority (commands highest if starting with "/"), secondary by match score.

## Commands

Static list of commands accessible from the palette:

Navigation commands:
- "Open settings" -> navigate to /settings
- "Open fleet page" -> navigate to /fleet
- "Open automations" -> navigate to /automations
- "Open simulator" -> navigate to /simulate

View commands:
- "Toggle Fresnel overlay" -> toggle the Fresnel zone debug layer
- "Toggle flow map" -> toggle the flow map layer
- "Toggle dwell heatmap" -> toggle the dwell heatmap layer
- "Toggle zone volumes" -> toggle zone cuboid visibility
- "Reset camera" -> fly camera back to default top-down position

System commands:
- "Enter away mode" -> POST /api/mode {"mode":"away"}
- "Enter home mode" -> POST /api/mode {"mode":"home"}
- "Enter sleep mode" -> POST /api/mode {"mode":"sleep"}
- "Trigger fleet OTA" -> opens the fleet OTA dialog
- "Add a person" -> opens the Add Person form in the People & Devices panel
- "Add a zone" -> starts zone creation mode in the 3D view
- "Add a portal" -> starts portal creation mode

Debug commands (shown at bottom, lower priority):
- "Export all events CSV" -> GET /api/events?format=csv and download
- "Show link health table" -> opens the link health panel
- "Run diagnostics" -> triggers a diagnostics pass and shows results
- "Check firmware updates" -> fetches latest firmware version and compares to all nodes

## Time Navigation

If the query starts with "@": attempt to parse as a time expression and offer a "Jump to time" result.

Supported formats:
- "@3am" -> today at 03:00
- "@3:15am" -> today at 03:15
- "@yesterday 11pm" -> yesterday at 23:00
- "@2026-03-27 14:23" -> specific datetime
- "@-30min" -> 30 minutes ago from now
- "@-2h" -> 2 hours ago

On selection: triggers time-travel replay to the parsed timestamp (same as clicking a timeline event's tap-to-jump).

Show parsing preview in the result item: "Jump to 2026-03-27 03:00:00" with a clock icon.

## Result Item Rendering

Each result item in the list:
- Left: icon (category-appropriate SVG, 16px)
- Centre-left: primary text (entity name or command label, 14px)
- Centre-right: secondary text (grey, 12px): for zones: "[N] people currently", for nodes: "[online/offline]", for commands: keyboard shortcut hint if any
- Right: arrow icon (shows the item is actionable)

Selected item (keyboard navigation): blue #3b82f6 background highlight.

## Keyboard Navigation

- Arrow up/down: move selection through results
- Enter: execute selected item
- Tab: same as Enter (for keyboard-first users who use Tab to confirm)
- Escape: close palette
- Character keys: type to refine search (selection resets to first result on each keystroke)

## Recent History

When the palette opens with an empty query: show "Recent" header with the last 5 palette actions (stored in localStorage "spaxel_palette_history"). Format: same as search results but without scores. "Recent" category shown before search results.

Recent history excludes time navigation entries (those are ephemeral).

## Files to Create or Modify

- dashboard/js/commandpalette.js: CommandPaletteManager, fuzzy match, time parsing, result rendering
- dashboard/js/commandpalette.css: modal overlay, input, result list styles
- dashboard/js/app.js: keyboard shortcut listener (Ctrl+K / Cmd+K), integrate CommandPaletteManager

## Tests

- Test fuzzy matching: "kit" -> "Kitchen" score > 0.7; "livig rm" -> "Living Room" score > 0.5; "xyz" -> "Kitchen" score < 0.3 (excluded)
- Test time navigation parsing: "@3am" parses to today at 03:00; "@-30min" parses to 30 minutes ago; "@2026-03-27 14:23" parses correctly
- Test that commands list is complete (all documented commands present in the registry)
- Test keyboard navigation: arrow down moves selection, Enter executes, Escape closes
- Test recent history: execute 5 actions, open palette with empty query -> 5 recent items shown
- Test that palette does not activate in simple mode or ambient mode (keyboard listener absent)
- Test that viewport reposition correctly positions the palette centred on the screen

## Acceptance Criteria

- Command palette opens with Ctrl+K (or Cmd+K on macOS) in < 50ms
- Fuzzy search returns "Kitchen" for query "kitch", "kit", "ktchn"
- Time navigation "@3am" correctly seeks replay to 03:00 today
- All documented commands are accessible and execute correctly
- Arrow key navigation works correctly through results
- Recent history shows last 5 palette actions on empty query
- Palette unavailable in simple and ambient modes
- All features accessible in 3 keystrokes or fewer from palette open
- Tests pass
close_reason: Implemented command palette (Component 34): commandpalette.js with CommandPaletteManager, fuzzy scorer, time expression parser, 20-command registry, recent history, entity search. commandpalette.css with modal overlay styles. 64 passing Jest tests covering all acceptance criteria. Integrated into app.js and index.html.

title: Firmware: NTP clock sync for TX stagger accuracy
description:
## Overview
Implement NTP synchronization on ESP32-S3 so all nodes share a common clock, enabling accurate TX stagger scheduling to avoid CSI collisions.

## Firmware (firmware/main/wifi.c or ntp.c)
- Call esp_sntp_setservername(0, ntp_server) before esp_sntp_init() on boot
- ntp_server read from NVS 'ntp_server' key (default: 'pool.ntp.org')
- Attempt sync for up to 10 seconds after WiFi connect; log WARN to UART if sync fails
- On sync failure: proceed without stagger (rely on CSMA/CA for collision avoidance)
- Resync every 10 minutes via esp_timer periodic callback
- Include NTP sync status in health JSON message: {type:'health', ..., ntp_synced: true/false}

## Mothership (provisioning payload)
- Read SPAXEL_NTP_SERVER env var (default: pool.ntp.org)
- Embed ntp_server field in provisioning payload JSON
- Support config downstream message field ntp_server to push updated server to nodes

## Acceptance
- Node health messages show ntp_synced: true when pool is reachable
- ntp_synced: false when NTP blocked — node still operates normally
- Resync occurs every ~600s (verified via UART logs)

title: Implement Settings REST endpoints
description: Implement GET and POST /api/settings endpoints. Return all configurable settings as JSON, support partial update with merge semantics. Persist to SQLite across restarts. Include OpenAPI-style godoc comments.

title: Phase 3: Multi-Node & Localization
description:
Goal: Spatial positioning with 4+ nodes. Humanoid blob rendering.

Deliverables:
- Bidirectional node protocol (registration, health, BLE relay, role/config push, OTA commands)
- Fleet manager (node registry in SQLite, role assignment, stagger scheduling, self-healing)
- Multi-link fusion (Fresnel zone weighted localization on 3D grid)
- Biomechanical blob tracking (peak extraction, ID assignment, UKF with human motion constraints)
- 3D spatial visualization (room bounds, floor plan, humanoid figures, footprint trails, node meshes)
- Node placement UI (TransformControls for dragging nodes in 3D, space dimension editor)
- Live coverage painting (GDOP overlay, updates during node drag, virtual node support)

Exit criteria: 4+ nodes produce a 3D view with humanoid figures tracking a walking person at ±1m accuracy.
close_reason: Phase 3 core complete: bidirectional protocol (c41), fleet manager (8u3), multi-link fusion (6th), blob tracking (iq3), 3D viz (tr7) all closed. Node placement UI (qq6) continues as parallel task. Exit criteria met: 3D view with humanoid figures tracking via Fresnel zone fusion.

title: Implement Timeline Storage
description:
Create SQLite events table with indexes on timestamp, person_id, zone_id, and type. Implement timeline storage subscriber goroutine that reads from EventBus and writes to SQLite. Use 1000-event buffered queue with drop-oldest behavior on overflow.

Acceptance Criteria:
- All event types are stored in SQLite within 1 second of firing
- Storage subscriber never blocks publishers
- Handles queue overflow gracefully with warning log
- Tests pass
close_reason: Verified timeline storage fully implemented. SQLite events table with indexes on timestamp_ms/zone/person/type (migration 001). timeline.Storage subscriber reads from EventBus, writes to SQLite with 1000-event buffered queue and drop-oldest overflow. All tests pass, go vet clean.

title: Firmware: dual-partition OTA rollback validation
description:
## Overview
Ensure the ESP32-S3 firmware correctly uses dual OTA partitions and only marks a new image as valid after the mothership confirms connectivity — enabling automatic rollback on failed upgrades.

## Partition layout (firmware/partitions.csv)
factory  app  factory  0x0       0x400000  (4 MB)
ota_0    app  ota_0    0x410000  0x400000  (4 MB)
ota_1    app  ota_1    0x810000  0x400000  (4 MB)
nvs      data nvs      0x9000    0x6000    (24 KB)
otadata  data ota      0xE000    0x2000    (8 KB)

## OTA validation sequence (firmware/main/websocket.c or main.c)
1. After OTA download complete: esp_ota_set_boot_partition(new_partition) then esp_restart()
2. New firmware boots from new partition
3. Firmware sends hello WebSocket message within 10 s
4. Firmware waits for role message from mothership (up to 60 s)
5. On receipt of role: call esp_ota_mark_app_valid_cancel_rollback()
6. If role not received within 60 s: do NOT call mark_valid; ESP-IDF rollback to previous partition on next reset
7. Log: 'OTA validation: marked valid after role received' or 'OTA validation: timed out, rollback on next reset'

## Test scenarios
- Happy path: new firmware installs, connects, receives role, marks valid — confirmed with esp_ota_get_running_partition()
- Crash before hello: simulate crash before ws_send_hello(); verify rollback restores old firmware
- Role timeout: simulate mothership not sending role; verify rollback after next reset cycle
- Version mismatch: mothership rejects connection (wrong token); verify rollback

## Acceptance
- partitions.csv present and correct with dual OTA layout
- esp_ota_mark_app_valid_cancel_rollback() called ONLY after role received
- Firmware logs show validation state transitions
- Rollback confirmed working in at least one test scenario

title: Responsive CSS grid layout for dashboard (no absolute-positioned panels)
close_reason: Verified: responsive CSS grid layout already fully implemented. Fixed integrations.html missing viewport-fit=cover and apple mobile meta tags.

title: fix: notify/service.go color variable shadows color package
Zones: occupancy reconciliation after server restart
description:
## Overview
Restore zone occupancy counts after mothership restarts using SQLite-persisted values + portal crossing history, avoiding reset-to-zero artifacts.

## Algorithm (mothership/internal/zones/ or fleet/)

### On startup (after schema migrations):
1. Load last_known_occupancy per zone from zones table (stored before each graceful shutdown)
2. Mark all zone occupancies as 'uncertain' — dashboard shows grey/amber badge
3. Compute midnight timestamp (local timezone, today)
4. Query: SELECT zone_id, SUM(direction) FROM portal_crossings WHERE timestamp_ms >= midnight GROUP BY zone_id
5. Apply net crossings to loaded occupancy: reconciled_count = last_known + net_crossings
6. Clamp to >= 0 (committed crossing out of empty room never goes negative)
7. Use reconciled_count as starting occupancy

### Reconciliation validation (runs every 30s for first 60s of operation):
- Compare portal-based occupancy vs. blob-count-per-zone (from fusion output)
- If they differ by > 1 for 2 consecutive checks: apply blob-count as ground truth; log discrepancy
- After 60s of live operation: mark occupancies as 'reconciled'; clear uncertain badges

### Persistence:
- On graceful shutdown (SIGTERM): write current occupancy to zones.last_known_occupancy for all zones
- On each zone occupancy change: update zones.last_known_occupancy in SQLite

### Dashboard:
- Uncertain occupancy: zone card shows amber border + 'Estimated' label
- Reconciled: green border + no label

## Acceptance
- Restart with 2 people in kitchen: occupancy restored to 2 within 60s
- Portal crossing computed correctly from midnight
- Blob-count override triggers correctly if >1 discrepancy for 2 checks
- Graceful shutdown persists occupancy: verify via sqlite3 query after SIGTERM
close_reason: Already implemented: occupancy reconciliation in zones/manager.go - reconcileOccupancy() loads persisted counts + net crossings since midnight, ReconcileTick() validates portal vs blob counts, PersistOccupancy() on shutdown. All 13 tests pass.

title: Mothership: startup phase sequencing with timeout enforcement
description:
## Overview
Implement explicit startup phase logging and a 30-second total timeout so the mothership fails fast and clearly on misconfiguration.

## Phases (plan lines 3450-3475):
Phase 1/7 — Data directory: create /data, acquire flock() lock (prevents dual-instance)
Phase 2/7 — SQLite: open database, enable WAL mode, set busy_timeout=5000
Phase 3/7 — Schema migrations: apply pending migrations (spaxel-9z3)
Phase 4/7 — Config & secrets: validate env vars (spaxel-env-validation), load/generate install secret
Phase 5/7 — Subsystems: start all managers (fleet, ingestion, signal, fusion, etc.) with 5s per-subsystem timeout
Phase 6/7 — HTTP + mDNS: bind HTTP server, advertise _spaxel._tcp.local
Phase 7/7 — Health: run initial health check; log [READY]

## Implementation
- context.WithTimeout(30s) wraps all 7 phases
- Each phase: log '[PHASE N/7 — Description]' on start; '[PHASE N/7 OK] (Xms)' on completion
- Phase 5 subsystem timeout: each manager.Start() called with 5s context; if any fails, abort startup
- On 30s deadline exceeded: log fatal '[STARTUP TIMEOUT] Failed to reach ready state in 30s'; exit(1)
- Write /tmp/spaxel.ready on Phase 7 success (optional, for Docker --health-cmd alternative)

## Acceptance
- Startup log shows all 7 phases with timing
- Missing data directory: fails at Phase 1 with clear error
- DB corruption: fails at Phase 2 with clear error
- Subsystem timeout (e.g., SQLite locked): fails at Phase 5 within 5s
- Total startup within 5s under normal conditions (logged timing confirms)
close_reason: Startup phase sequencing with 30s timeout enforcement already implemented in commit 76ac271. Package mothership/internal/startup provides: 7 sequential phases, 30s total timeout, 5s per-subsystem timeout, phase logging with timing, ready marker file. All 15 tests pass.

title: Implement repeated-setting change detection
description: Detect when a user changes the same configuration setting more than 3 times within 24 hours. Show a non-intrusive prompt offering help. 'Help me tune this' button opens guided calibration flow: test for false positives (walk around room), test for missed motion (sit still), suggest optimal value based on diurnal baseline SNR and link health, apply suggested value button. Track changes in localStorage. Files: dashboard/js/proactive.js. Acceptance: help prompt fires after 3+ changes in 24h; calibration flow tests both directions; suggests value based on system data; apply button works.

Add person dropdown, zone dropdown, date range selector (Today/Last 7 days/Last 30 days/Custom), and text search for fuzzy matching on descriptions. title: Diurnal adaptive baseline
description:
## Background

The EMA (Exponential Moving Average) baseline implemented in Phase 2 adapts to ambient conditions on a roughly 30-second timescale. This handles rapid environmental changes well, but misses systematic daily patterns: more WiFi interference in evenings due to neighbour activity, temperature changes affecting the ESP32's hardware oscillator characteristics, different background activity patterns at different times of day (e.g. higher ambient motion from TV or household activity in the evenings). A diurnal (24-hour) baseline learns these patterns and crossfades to the appropriate hourly baseline, dramatically reducing false positives at different times of day.

## What is a Diurnal Baseline?

Instead of a single EMA that tracks the most recent state, we maintain 24 separate baseline vectors — one per hour of the day. Each hour-slot baseline is an array of float64 values, one per CSI subcarrier (matching the existing baseline format). The hour-slot is updated only by readings that arrive during that hour, using a very slow EMA (tau = 7 days, so alpha ≈ 0.00017 per sample at 2 Hz). This means each hour-slot slowly learns the characteristic ambient CSI for that time of day, averaged over many days.

## 7-Day Learning Period

A freshly deployed system has no diurnal data. For the first 7 days, the system continues to use the standard Phase 2 EMA baseline for all detection decisions. During this period, all received readings are silently updating the diurnal slots, but those slots are not used for detection. The dashboard shows: "Learning your home's daily patterns: {N} days remaining."

After 7 days of data (where "data" means at least 100 readings in each hour-slot — this threshold prevents partial-data slots from being used), the diurnal baseline activates automatically. A one-time notification is shown: "Your system has learned your daily patterns. Accuracy should improve this week."

## Crossfade Algorithm

A naive implementation would jump discontinuously at each hour boundary, causing false detections at e.g. exactly 14:00 when the baseline switches from the 13:xx slot to the 14:xx slot. The fix is a smooth crossfade:

Let h = current_hour (0-23), frac = current_minute / 60.
Effective baseline B_eff = (1 - frac) * B_slot[h] + frac * B_slot[(h+1) % 24]

This is a linear interpolation between the current-hour and next-hour baselines. At 13:00, B_eff = B_slot[13]. At 13:30, B_eff = 0.5*B_slot[13] + 0.5*B_slot[14]. At 13:59, B_eff is nearly B_slot[14]. A cosine crossfade (frac_smooth = (1 - cos(pi * frac)) / 2) can be used instead of linear for a perceptually smoother transition.

The crossfade is applied per-subcarrier to the full baseline vector before comparing to incoming CSI readings.

## Confidence Indicator

The confidence score is a per-link float in [0, 1] that summarises the reliability of detection on that link. It is broadcast as part of the "link_health" WebSocket message and rendered in the dashboard as a colour-coded indicator per link (green > 0.7, amber 0.4-0.7, red < 0.4).

Confidence inputs:
1. Baseline age: time since the diurnal slot for the current hour was last updated. Staleness reduces confidence. If a slot has not been updated for > 3 days, its confidence contribution is 0.
2. Diurnal learning progress: 0.0 before 7 days, interpolates to 1.0 at 14 days. This ramps in the diurnal component gradually as more data accumulates.
3. Packet rate health: actual received packets per second divided by the configured sample rate. If packet rate drops to 80% of configured, confidence = 0.8. At 50%, confidence = 0.5.
4. Composite: weighted_avg(baseline_age: 0.3, diurnal_progress: 0.3, packet_rate: 0.4).

## SQLite Persistence

The diurnal baseline must survive mothership restarts. Extend the existing BaselineManager.Snapshot() / RestoreBaseline() methods to include diurnal data.

Add SQLite table: diurnal_baselines (link_id TEXT, hour_slot INTEGER, subcarrier_idx INTEGER, value REAL, sample_count INTEGER, last_updated DATETIME, PRIMARY KEY (link_id, hour_slot, subcarrier_idx)).

On startup, RestoreBaseline() loads diurnal slots from SQLite. On shutdown (or every 5 minutes as a background snapshot), Snapshot() writes updated diurnal slots to SQLite. The snapshot is incremental: only write slots that have been updated since the last snapshot.

File: mothership/internal/signal/baseline.go — extend existing BaselineManager struct.

## Implementation Structure

New struct DiurnalBaseline within baseline.go:
- slots [24][]float64: per-hour baseline vectors (len = num_subcarriers)
- sampleCounts [24]int: readings accumulated per slot
- lastUpdated [24]time.Time: timestamp of last slot update
- alpha float64: slow EMA coefficient (default: 1/(7*24*3600*2) per sample at 2Hz)

DiurnalBaseline.Update(hour int, values []float64) method: applies slow EMA to the appropriate hour slot.
DiurnalBaseline.EffectiveBaseline(t time.Time) []float64: returns the crossfaded baseline for the given timestamp.
DiurnalBaseline.IsReady() bool: returns true if 7+ days have elapsed since first update AND all 24 slots have >= 100 samples.
DiurnalBaseline.Confidence(t time.Time, packetRateRatio float64) float64: returns composite confidence score.

## Tests

- Test that hour-slot selection is correct for timestamps at boundaries (23:59:59 -> slot 23, 00:00:00 -> slot 0)
- Test that crossfade at half-hour produces the correct blend of two adjacent slots
- Test cosine crossfade is smooth (no discontinuity at integer hours in the smooth version)
- Test that the 7-day learning gate correctly returns IsReady() = false before 7 days and true after
- Test that confidence score is 0 when packet_rate_ratio = 0
- Test SQLite snapshot round-trip: snapshot diurnal data, clear in-memory state, restore, verify values match
- Test that baseline staleness correctly reduces confidence for a slot not updated in > 3 days

## Acceptance Criteria

- Diurnal baseline automatically activates after 7 days of data collection per link
- Hour-boundary crossfade is smooth (no visible discontinuities in false positive rate)
- Confidence indicator visible per link in dashboard, updates in real-time
- Diurnal baseline data persists across mothership restarts via SQLite snapshot
- Detection accuracy measurably improves (target: <5% false positive rate after 7-day learning vs >10% with standard EMA baseline in homes with consistent daily patterns)
- One-time "patterns learned" notification fires exactly once after 7 days
- Tests pass

title: Spatial automation: trigger volume geometry builder
description:
## Overview
3D trigger volume editor and point-in-volume evaluation engine for the spatial automation system.

## Backend (mothership/automation/ or zones/)
- Geometry types: box {type, x, y, z, w, d, h} and cylinder {type, cx, cy, z, r, h} stored as shape_json
- Point-in-volume tests: axis-aligned box test and cylinder test functions
- Trigger state machine per (trigger_id, blob_id) pair: track inside/outside state and transition edges (enter, leave, dwell, vacant, count)
- Dwell timer: fire dwell action after N continuous seconds inside volume
- SQLite triggers table: id, name, shape_json, condition TEXT, condition_params JSON, actions_json, enabled BOOL, last_fired_ms
- REST CRUD at /api/triggers (requires spaxel-6ha)

## Dashboard (dashboard/js/automation-builder.js)
- Automation panel via panel framework (spaxel-896)
- Draw box volume: click + drag to define base footprint, height slider
- Draw cylinder volume: click center, drag radius, height slider
- THREE.js TransformControls for translate/scale/rotate after placement
- Volume visualization: translucent colored box/cylinder; pulse animation when condition fires
- Condition picker: enter zone / leave zone / dwell N seconds / zone vacant / count >= N
- Action list: webhook URL, MQTT topic/payload, internal (arm security, rebaseline, notify)
- Trigger log: last 10 firings with timestamp and matched blob

## Acceptance
- Box and cylinder volumes render correctly in 3D view
- Point-in-volume evaluated on each fusion tick (target <1ms per trigger)
- Dwell trigger fires at correct time ±1s
- Trigger state persists across server restart
- Requires: spaxel-896, spaxel-6ha, spaxel-9eg

title: Passive radar: auto-detect router AP as virtual TX node
description:
## Overview
Automatically detect the home router as a passive radar TX source, eliminating need for a dedicated active TX node.

## Firmware changes
- During hello message, include ap_bssid and ap_channel from esp_wifi_sta_get_ap_info()

## Mothership (mothership/fleet/ or ingestion/)
- On hello: extract ap_bssid; if >=80% of nodes report same BSSID create virtual node entry with virtual=1, position unset
- OUI lookup: embed IEEE OUI registry as Go map compiled via go:embed; display router brand
- Detect AP BSSID change (router reboot/replacement) and emit system alert
- SQLite nodes table: add virtual BOOL, node_type TEXT, ap_bssid TEXT, ap_channel INT columns

## Dashboard
- After AP auto-detected: 'I detected your router (ASUS). Place it on the floor plan to improve accuracy.'
- Drag-to-place virtual node (distinct router icon) in 3D editor
- Confirmation dialog with 'Use as signal source' toggle

## Acceptance
- Virtual node appears in /api/nodes with virtual=true
- 3D view renders virtual node with distinct icon
- AP change detection fires a system event within 30s of BSSID change
close_reason:
Implemented passive radar auto-detection of router AP

Firmware: Added ap_bssid/ap_channel to hello message using esp_wifi_sta_get_ap_info()

Mothership: Created apdetector package for >=80% BSSID agreement detection, OUI lookup for router manufacturer, AP change detection system events

Dashboard: AP detection notification, distinct router icon in 3D (box+4antennas), drag-to-place positioning

Virtual nodes appear in /api/nodes with virtual=true, node_type=ap

title: Add ntfy delivery client tests
description: Write tests for ntfy delivery with mock HTTP server: verifies headers/body. Acceptance Criteria: ntfy delivery tests pass with mocks.

title: merge: remove phase6 build tag and unify main.go
description:
## Problem
`cmd/mothership/main_phase6.go` is gated behind `//go:build phase6` which excludes all Phase 6+ code from default builds. The directory has both `main.go` (Phase 5) and `main_phase6.go` (Phase 6) — both define `package main` with `func main()`, so removing the build tag would cause a duplicate symbol error.

## Prerequisites
All Phase 6 package compile errors must be fixed first (spaxel-glq, spaxel-9nj, spaxel-19h, spaxel-uln, spaxel-7nk, spaxel-she).

## Steps
1. Confirm all Phase 6+ packages compile cleanly:
   ```bash
   cd /home/coding/spaxel/mothership
   PATH=$PATH:/home/coding/go/bin go build ./internal/...
   ```
2. Delete `cmd/mothership/main.go.bak` (stale backup)
3. Delete `cmd/mothership/main.go` (Phase 5 entrypoint, superseded)
4. Remove the `//go:build phase6` line and the blank line after it from `cmd/mothership/main_phase6.go`
5. Build and verify:
   ```bash
   PATH=$PATH:/home/coding/go/bin go build ./...
   PATH=$PATH:/home/coding/go/bin go test ./...
   ```

## Acceptance
- `go build ./...` passes with no errors
- Binary is built from the Phase 6 entrypoint
- No `phase6` build tag exists anywhere in the codebase

Dependents:
  <- spaxel-jcc

title: Implement post-feedback explanations
description: When user marks detection as FALSE_POSITIVE, show explanation: 'The system detected motion here because: [link]'s signal exceeded threshold by Nx at [time]. Could be caused by: [root cause or 'ambient RF interference']. We've noted this and will apply a correction.' Uses ExplainabilitySnapshot to identify contributing link, runs diagnostic check on link health at event timestamp. Show as expandable section on feedback confirmation card. Files: dashboard/js/proactive.js, mothership/internal/diagnostics/linkweather.go. Acceptance: explanation shown after any FALSE_POSITIVE feedback; contains contributing link name; shows diagnostic result or default message.
close_reason: Implemented post-feedback explanations for FALSE_POSITIVE detections. The system now shows detailed explanations including contributing link name, signal threshold ratio, timestamp, diagnostic root cause analysis, and correction note. Updated dashboard/js/feedback.js to properly display explainability data from the backend API.

title: Diurnal adaptive baseline: 24-hour slot learning
description:
## Overview
Extend the EMA baseline system with per-hour-of-day slots to eliminate false positives caused by daily environmental cycles (sunlight, HVAC, temperature changes).

## Backend (mothership/signal/baseline.go extension)
- Data structure: 24 hourly slots per link per subcarrier; each slot stores amplitude blob and sample_count
- Learning phase (7 days): accumulate motion-free CSI into hourly slots; require >=300 samples/slot to mark ready
- Steady state: on each fusion tick, select active baseline = weighted blend of diurnal slot (if ready) + EMA fallback
- Crossfade: over first 15 min of each hour, linearly blend from EMA to diurnal slot; after 15 min use diurnal exclusively
- Motion-gated updates: EMA updates continue during the hourly window, improving diurnal slot over time
- Outlier protection: skip update if deltaRMS > motion threshold (don't train on motion frames)
- SQLite diurnal_baselines table: link_id, hour_of_day (0-23), n_sub INT, amplitude BLOB, sample_count INT, confidence REAL, updated_at INT

## Dashboard visualization
- Per-link detail panel: 24-hour polar chart (or horizontal bar chart) showing baseline amplitude variance by hour
- 'Diurnal learning' progress indicator: 'Learning hour 14... 6/7 days'
- Confidence color per hour: green (ready), amber (partial), red (no data)

## Acceptance
- Baseline correctly crossfades at hour boundaries (±60s)
- Motion events during learning do not corrupt slots (outlier protection confirmed by test)
- Polar chart renders for links with >=1 ready slot
- No performance regression: baseline lookup remains O(1)
- Requires: spaxel-jcc (phase 6 integration)

title: Ingestion: CSI frame validation with malformed counter and auto-close
description:
## Overview
Implement strict CSI binary frame validation with per-connection malformed frame counters and automatic connection closure on persistent malformed input.

## Validation rules (plan lines 303-324):
- Minimum frame length: 24 bytes (header only, zero subcarriers valid)
- Maximum frame length: 280 bytes (24 header + 128 subcarriers × 2 bytes I/Q)
- n_sub field: must be ≤128
- Payload length: must equal n_sub × 2 bytes exactly
- channel: must be in [1,14] for 2.4 GHz; drop if 0 or >14
- rssi: int8; 0 treated as invalid/missing (not an error, but log at DEBUG)
- timestamp_us: any uint64 value accepted

## Per-connection malformed counter (sliding 60-second window):
- Track malformed_count and window_start_ms per WebSocket connection
- On each validation failure: increment malformed_count; log at DEBUG
- Every 60s: check counts → if malformed_count > 100: log WARN 'Node {mac} sent {N} malformed frames in 60s'
- If malformed_count > 1000 within 60s: close WebSocket with message 'Excessive malformed frames — possible firmware bug'
- Reset counter every 60s

## Acceptance
- Valid frame: passes all checks in <1 μs
- Frame with n_sub=200: rejected (n_sub > 128)
- Frame with len=10: rejected (< 24 bytes)
- Frame with channel=0: dropped silently
- 1001 malformed frames in 60s: connection closed with correct message
- 101 malformed frames: WARN logged, connection kept open
close_reason:
Implemented CSI frame validation with DEBUG logging and performance benchmark.

All validation rules from plan lines 303-324 implemented:
- Minimum frame length: 24 bytes ✓
- Maximum frame length: 280 bytes ✓  
- n_sub ≤ 128 ✓
- Payload length = n_sub × 2 bytes ✓
- Channel in [1,14] for 2.4 GHz ✓
- RSSI=0 logged at DEBUG (allowed) ✓
- timestamp_us any value ✓

Per-connection malformed counter (60s sliding window):
- DEBUG log on each validation failure ✓
- WARN log when count > 100 ✓
- Auto-close when count > 1000 ✓
- Counter resets every 60s ✓

Added benchmark tests to verify <1 μs validation performance for valid frames.
