title: Spatial context notifications with floor-plan thumbnails
description:
## Background

Push notifications without context are ignored or disabled. "Motion detected" tells you nothing useful. "Alice walked into the Kitchen — Bob is already there" is genuinely interesting. "Possible fall: Alice in Hallway — unacknowledged for 3 minutes" demands immediate attention. The plan specifies server-side rendering of mini floor-plan thumbnails attached to notifications to provide instant spatial context without opening the app.

## Server-Side Floor-Plan Renderer

New package: mothership/internal/render/floorplan.go

The renderer produces a top-down 2D PNG (300x300 pixels) showing:
- Room outline: outer boundary of all zones as white rectangles on dark background
- Zone fills: each zone as a semi-transparent coloured fill (zone.color at 20% opacity)
- Zone labels: zone name in small white text at zone centroid
- Node positions: small white circle dots
- Person blobs: coloured circles (person.color) at their last-known position, diameter proportional to detection confidence (min 10px, max 20px)
- Name labels: person name in white text above each blob circle, if identity is known
- Portal planes: thin lines in purple (#a855f7)
- Event highlight: the zone where the event occurred rendered with brighter fill and a white border

Rendering library: use github.com/fogleman/gg (a pure-Go 2D graphics library). Alternative: standard image/draw + image/png for maximum portability. The fogleman/gg approach is recommended for its higher-level drawing API (bezier curves, text, etc.).

The PNG must be generated within 200ms to not delay notification delivery. At 300x300 with simple geometry, this should be easily achievable.

The rendered PNG is stored as a []byte and passed to the notification delivery function. It is base64-encoded for attachment in webhook payloads or passed as a file to ntfy/Pushover APIs.

## Notification Types and Triggers

1. zone_enter: "{{person_name}} entered {{zone_name}}" — LOW priority unless security mode is active
2. zone_leave: "{{person_name}} left {{zone_name}}" — LOW priority
3. zone_vacant: "{{zone_name}} is now empty" — LOW priority
4. fall_detected: "Possible fall: {{person_name}} in {{zone_name}}" — URGENT, always immediate
5. fall_escalation: "URGENT: Fall unacknowledged for 5 minutes — {{person_name}} in {{zone_name}}" — URGENT
6. anomaly_alert: "Unexpected presence: {{zone_name}}" — HIGH priority (breaks quiet hours)
7. node_offline: "Node {{node_label}} has gone offline" — MEDIUM priority
8. sleep_summary: "Last night: {{sleep_duration}}" — LOW priority, morning delivery

## Smart Batching

If multiple LOW or MEDIUM priority events fire within a 30-second window, batch them into a single notification:
- "Alice entered Kitchen. Bob left Living Room."
- "2 presence events in the last 30 seconds."

Batching rules:
- Batch only events of the same priority level
- Never batch URGENT events — those are always immediate
- Never batch events involving different notification types if the combination is confusing
- Batch counter: if more than 5 events in 30s, summarise as "N presence events in the last minute"

Batching implementation: a 30-second window timer per notification channel. When the first LOW event fires, start the 30s timer. Accumulate events. On timer expiry: merge into one notification and deliver.

## Quiet Hours

User-configurable quiet hours: from_time, to_time (e.g. "22:00" to "07:00"). Stored in SQLite notifications_config (channel, quiet_from, quiet_to, quiet_days_bitmask).

During quiet hours:
- LOW priority notifications are queued
- MEDIUM priority notifications are queued
- HIGH and URGENT notifications are delivered immediately regardless of quiet hours

At the end of quiet hours (07:00 on non-override days): deliver all queued notifications as a morning digest bundle: "While you were asleep: [summary of queued events]"

## Delivery Channels

ntfy:
- POST to https://ntfy.sh/{topic} (or self-hosted server URL)
- Headers: Authorization: Bearer {token} (if configured), Priority: urgent/high/default/low/min
- Body: the notification text
- Headers: Attach: {base64_encoded_png_url} — for ntfy, attach the floor-plan as a URL if mothership is publicly accessible, or send as base64 data URL for local deployments

Pushover:
- POST to https://api.pushover.net/1/messages.json
- Fields: token, user, message, title, priority, attachment (PNG as multipart form upload)

Generic webhook:
- POST to user-configured URL
- Body: {"event_type":"...", "message":"...", "person_id":"...", "zone_id":"...", "timestamp":"...", "floorplan_png_base64":"..."}

## Configuration UI

Dashboard Settings panel -> "Notifications" tab:
- Delivery channel selector: None / ntfy / Pushover / Webhook
- Channel-specific credential fields (ntfy server URL + topic + token, Pushover API key, webhook URL)
- Test notification button: sends a test notification to verify configuration
- Event type enable/disable toggles: per event type, can disable e.g. "zone_enter" while keeping "fall_detected" enabled
- Quiet hours: time picker from/to, day-of-week selector
- Smart batching toggle (default on)
- "Morning digest" toggle (default on — delivers batched quiet-hours events at wake time)

## Files to Create or Modify

- mothership/internal/render/floorplan.go: floor-plan PNG renderer
- mothership/internal/notifications/manager.go: NotificationManager, batching, quiet hours logic
- mothership/internal/notifications/ntfy.go: ntfy delivery client
- mothership/internal/notifications/pushover.go: Pushover delivery client
- mothership/internal/notifications/webhook.go: generic webhook delivery
- mothership/internal/dashboard/routes.go: GET/PUT /api/settings/notifications, POST /api/notifications/test

## Tests

- Test floor-plan renderer produces a 300x300 PNG with correct dimensions
- Test that zone boundaries appear in the rendered PNG at correct coordinates (check pixel colors at known positions)
- Test batching: 3 LOW events within 10s -> 1 notification; 1 URGENT event -> immediate even if batching timer is active
- Test quiet hours gate: LOW event at 23:00 with quiet hours 22:00-07:00 -> queued; URGENT event at 23:00 -> delivered immediately
- Test morning digest delivery: queued events are bundled and delivered at quiet_hours_end
- Test ntfy delivery with mock HTTP server: verify correct headers and body format
- Test webhook delivery with mock HTTP server: verify correct JSON body and base64 PNG field
- Test test-notification endpoint fires correctly

## Acceptance Criteria

- Notification received via ntfy within 5 seconds of trigger event for URGENT priority
- Floor-plan PNG correctly shows zone boundaries and person positions in the notification
- Smart batching prevents more than one notification per 30-second window for LOW events
- Quiet hours suppress LOW/MEDIUM notifications and queue them for morning digest
- Fall detection and anomaly alerts always bypass quiet hours
- Morning digest delivered correctly at quiet hours end
- Test notification button correctly verifies channel configuration
- Tests pass

title: Mothership: adaptive load shedding & resource throttling
description:
## Overview
Implement a 4-level load shedding system to keep the fusion pipeline responsive under CPU/memory pressure, especially for large fleets.

## Pipeline instrumentation
- Time each of the 8 fusion pipeline stages per iteration using time.Since()
- Maintain 5-iteration rolling average of total iteration time (ring buffer of 5 durations)

## Load shedding state machine
Level 0 (normal): rolling avg < 80 ms — full pipeline
Level 1 (light): rolling avg >= 80 ms — suspend crowd flow accumulation (~3 ms saved/iter)
Level 2 (moderate): rolling avg >= 90 ms — also suspend CSI replay buffer writes (~2 ms saved/iter)
Level 3 (heavy): rolling avg >= 95 ms — drop CSI frames when ingest channel > 50% full; push rate reduction config to all nodes (10 Hz cap)

Recovery: when rolling avg < 60 ms for 10 consecutive iterations, step down one level

## Integration points
- Health endpoint GET /healthz: include shedding_level (0-3) in response
- Dashboard status bar: show 'System load: NOMINAL / LIGHT / MODERATE / HIGH'
- WS alert when Level 3 triggered: {type: 'alert', severity: 'warning', description: 'System under load — CSI rate reduced to 10 Hz'}
- Level 3 recovery: push config message to all nodes restoring their prior rate

## Acceptance
- Load shedding level changes logged at INFO
- Level 3 triggers correctly when ingest channel >50% full
- Node rate restoration confirmed after Level 3 recovery
- Health endpoint reflects current level
- No mutex contention from shedding logic itself (must be lock-free reads)

title: Interactive onboarding wizard
description:
## Background

Phase 4's central goal is that a non-technical user can go from an unboxed ESP32-S3 to streaming CSI in under 5 minutes. The onboarding wizard is the centrepiece of this experience. It uses the Web Serial API (available in Chrome/Edge) to communicate with the ESP32 over USB — no driver installation needed, no CLI, no app download. The wizard is embedded in the existing mothership dashboard, accessible at /onboard.

## Why Web Serial?

The alternative approaches — a dedicated mobile app, a WiFi provisioning AP, or a CLI tool — all have significant UX friction. Web Serial lets us flash firmware, provision WiFi credentials, and guide the user through calibration all in one browser session. The dashboard already knows the mothership IP/port. Chrome and Edge (95%+ of desktop browser market) support Web Serial natively since 2021. The only caveat is that Web Serial is not available in Firefox or Safari — this must be documented prominently at the start of the wizard.

## Wizard Steps

1. Browser check: Detect navigator.serial availability. If missing, show: "Please use Google Chrome or Microsoft Edge to use the setup wizard. Firefox and Safari do not support USB device access."

2. Connect device: Call navigator.serial.requestPort(). Guide the user to hold BOOT button while plugging in if the device does not appear. Show a SVG illustration of the ESP32-S3 board with the BOOT button highlighted.

3. Flash firmware (if not already spaxel firmware): Use esp-web-tools (espressif/esp-web-tools). This open-source library handles the full ESP32 flashing pipeline via Web Serial, including ROM bootloader protocol, chip detection, and progress reporting. It needs a firmware manifest.json at GET /api/firmware/manifest describing binary addresses and offsets. Show a progress bar during flashing. Estimated time: 45-90 seconds.

4. Provision WiFi: Show a form for SSID and password. Optional: mothership host/port override (for non-mDNS setups). Assemble the provisioning payload and send to the ESP32 over serial as JSON (see Provisioning Payload bead for format).

5. Detect mothership: Once provisioned and rebooted, the ESP32 boots and discovers the mothership via mDNS (spaxel-mothership.local) or the configured host. Poll GET /api/nodes every 3s for up to 120s waiting for the new node to appear. Show animated "Connecting..." indicator. On timeout: show WiFi troubleshooting guidance (5GHz check, SSID typo check, distance check).

6. Guided calibration: Show the CSI waveform for the new node's links as they come online. Steps:
   a. "Walk around your space for 30 seconds" — CSI amplitude should show activity. If flat: check node orientation.
   b. "Stand still at the far end of the room" — capture baseline. Show countdown. Green check when baseline is captured.
   c. "Walk through the centre of the room" — Fresnel zone lights up in 3D view, blob appears. "The sensor can see you!"

7. Node placement guidance: Transition to the coverage painting UI (spaxel-qq6) for optimal node positioning. Show GDOP overlay for the current node placement. Suggest additional node positions if coverage is poor.

## Files to Create or Modify

- dashboard/js/onboard.js: wizard state machine, Web Serial API calls, step rendering
- dashboard/index.html: add /onboard route and wizard container div, import esp-web-tools
- mothership/internal/dashboard/ routes: add GET /api/firmware/manifest route
- Firmware manifest JSON served at GET /api/firmware/manifest with chipFamily, parts array containing path and offset

## esp-web-tools Integration

The library esp-web-tools is loaded from CDN as an ES module. A custom-element install-button is used for flashing. The manifest served by the mothership includes the firmware binary path (/firmware/latest) and flash offset (0x0). The library handles the bootloader handshake, erase, and write automatically.

## Wizard State Machine

States: BROWSER_CHECK -> CONNECT_DEVICE -> FLASH_FIRMWARE -> PROVISION_WIFI -> DETECT_NODE -> CALIBRATE -> PLACEMENT -> COMPLETE

Each state has: render() function, onEnter() side effects, onNext() transition, onBack() for revert, onError() for failure handling.

Persisted in sessionStorage so a page refresh during onboarding resumes from the last step — critical for the reboot-then-detect step where the browser must survive the ESP32 reboot cycle.

## Error Handling

Map every known failure to a human-friendly message:
- NotFoundError (no port selected) -> "No device detected. Make sure the USB cable is connected and hold the BOOT button while plugging in."
- NetworkError during flash -> "The connection was interrupted. Check the USB cable is not loose and try again."
- Node not appearing after 120s -> "Your node connected to WiFi but cannot reach the mothership. Check: 1) Your router blocks device-to-device communication (AP isolation). 2) The mothership address is correct. 3) Your network uses a VLAN that separates devices."
- Wrong SSID/password -> Node will fall into captive portal mode after 10 failures, triggering a "Captive portal detected" guidance flow.

Never show stack traces, WebSocket error codes, or Go error strings to the user.

## Tests

- Mock navigator.serial API in Jest to test wizard state transitions without real hardware
- Test that provisioning payload is correctly assembled and sent over the mocked serial port
- Test that polling GET /api/nodes correctly detects node appearance and transitions to DETECT_NODE -> CALIBRATE
- Test that BROWSER_CHECK step correctly detects missing serial API and shows the correct error
- Test that sessionStorage correctly restores wizard state on page refresh at each step

## Acceptance Criteria

- Wizard completes in under 5 minutes on a fresh ESP32-S3 with a working WiFi network
- User sees live CSI waveform during calibration step
- Node appears in dashboard after wizard completion, with correct label
- All known error conditions show human-friendly guidance, not technical errors
- All existing dashboard tests pass
- Wizard state is resumable after page refresh
close_reason:
Fixed 4 failing tests in the onboarding wizard test suite:

1. WebSocket mock: Changed from constructor-prototype pattern to factory function so jest.resetAllMocks() doesn't break the mock. Fixed 'state.ws.close is not a function' errors during calibrate step cleanup.

2. TextEncoderStream mock: Added functional readable/writable with pipeTo mock and data capture helpers (__getLastEncodedData/__clearLastEncodedData) to support provisioning serial send tests.

3. flash_firmware test: Fixed assertion to check wizard-nav element for 'Skip Flashing' button instead of wizard-content (the nav button is rendered separately from step content).

4. provisionAndSend 'no port' test: Changed getPorts mock from mockResolvedValueOnce to mockResolvedValue([]) so both the primary and fallback provisioning paths consistently fail when no port is available.

All 60 tests now pass. The onboarding wizard implementation (onboard.js, index.html, mothership firmware manifest route) was already complete from the previous commit.

title: Phase 6: Identity & Spatial Automation
description:
Goal: Named presence, actionable automations, safety features.

Deliverables:
- BLE device registry (People & Devices panel, auto-detected type, user labels/color)
- BLE-to-blob identity matching (multi-node RSSI triangulation → nearest CSI blob)
- Room transition portals (doorway planes, directional crossing, zone occupancy counters)
- Spatial automation builder (3D trigger volumes, conditions, webhook/MQTT actions)
- Fall detection (Z-axis descent + sustained stillness, alert chain, person-identified)
- Spatial context notifications (push with mini floor-plan thumbnails, smart batching, quiet hours)
- Home automation integration (optional MQTT for HA auto-discovery, webhooks)

Exit criteria: BLE-identified blobs show correct names. Fall detection fires on simulated falls <10% FP.
